We're planning some side-by-side comparisons for the long term review, so stay tuned for that. In the meantime, I'm willing to say that the increased tunability and lighter weight might put the Vivid above a coil shock on the right bike, for the right person. Gotta love the sag gradient. Rebound knob. And cheeky little 3 mil. Pros + Silent performance + Effective and distinct adjustments + Hydraulic bottom-out will complement some kinematics very well Cons - Lack of high speed rebound adjustment - Fitment may prove tricky on some frames Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Shock Week Shocks RockShox Rockshox Vivid Author Info: Dario DiGiulio Bellingham, WA, United States Member since Dec 25, 2016 484 articles Dario has been obsessed with bikes from an early age, and has worked in the industry since 2015. With a Bachelor's in Architecture from Cal Poly, San Luis Obispo, he aims to bring that mix of creativity and technicality to his work in the mountain bike world.

title: Review: Specialized's New Turbo Levo 4 Has More Power, More Battery
author: Mike Kazimer
article_text: Specialized Turbo Levo 4 https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/NtGDwbpv Copied Loading ad Live 00:25 00:00 09:05 Last year Specialized revamped nearly their whole mountain bike lineup - new versions of the Epic, Stumpjumper, and Status models were all launched, and a DH race bike was teased as well. Granted, there’s still no new Enduro, but I’ve stopped holding my breath on that one - hopefully the wait is worth it when it finally emerges. For 2025 the focus shifts to the motorized side, starting with the Turbo Levo. The new Levo picks up where the previous generation left off, and now has more power (thanks to a new motor), an 840 Wh battery, and there’s even in-frame storage. The 150mm of rear travel and 160mm fork are the same as before, and there’s still the wide range of possible geometry configurations that Specialized has become known for. There are four complete models, with prices starting at $7,999 for the Levo 4 Comp Carbon, and going all the way up to the S-Works model shown here, which retails for a whopping $13,499 USD. Specialized Turbo Levo 4 Details • Wheel size: Mixed • Travel: 150mm rear / 160mm fork • Carbon frame • Specialized S-Works 3.1 motor: 111 Nm torque, 720 watts peak output • Specialized 3.1 motor: 101 Nm torque, 666 watts peak output • Removable 840 Wh battery (range extender compatible) • Sizes: S2, S3, S4 (tested), S5, S6 • Weight: 52.3 lb / 23.7 kg (S-Works, S4) • Price: $7,999 - $13,499 USD MORE INFO Pinkbike may earn commission on any sales through the affiliate link above. Learn more . You can also go the frame-only route, still something of a rarity when it comes to eMTBs, assuming the $7,499 asking price of the S-Works frame doesn’t make you spit out your coffee. Similar to what Bosch has done in the past by offering a Race edition motor, Specialized’s S-Works motor is more powerful than the ‘regular’ Specialized 3.1 motor found in the other three models, with a max torque of 111 Nm and max power of 720 watts, compared to the still-impressive 101 Nm torque and 666 watt power of the other models. Contents Introduction Construction & Features Geometry & Sizing Suspension Design Specifications Setup Climbing Descending Technical Report What's the Best Value? How Does it Compare? Pros & Cons Pinkbike's Take On extra-techy sections of trail, the type with moves that slow speeds down to a crawl, the torque is especially impressive - pushing down on the pedals results in an instant surge of power, perfect for getting up and over rooty steps. — Mike Kazimer Frame Details The Levo has an entirely new carbon frame, with the battery accessed by a door on the side of the downtube. In recent years we’ve seen more and more e-bikes released with batteries that are aren’t easily removed, and in many cases unbolting the entire motor is required to pull them out. That’s typically done to save weight, since there aren’t any extra grams from the latching mechanism of a door, and there's no need to reinforce the rest of the frame to compensate for that big port. In this case, Specialized has made battery swaps about as easy as possible - all that’s required is a hex key. An 840 Wh battery is the stock configuration, but there’s a 600 Wh option available for riders who want to save some weight (the 840 Wh battery is 9.3 lb), and there’s also a 280 Wh range extender that sits in the bottle cage. No matter which battery is installed, there’s room for storing a spare tube or other repair supplies in the head tube area. A small zippered bag is supplied, and a magnet holds it into place. You do need to pull the battery out to get to those goodies, so it’s not really where I’d put snacks; think of it more like the spare tire and jack that get crammed under the trunk of a car. It’s also possible to remove the internal battery entirely and run the Levo solely on the range extender. In that setting the motor power is reduced by 50%. You could also ride the bike with no batteries installed at all, but that’s a move reserved for masochists, since the total weight with that setup is still 43 pounds. 0 seconds of 14 seconds Volume 90% https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/B4dTiszi Copied Live 00:00 00:14 00:14 Motor There’s a bit of an arms race going on at the moment when it comes to eMTB motors - max torque and wattage outputs are increasing, as companies inch closer and closer to the limit of Class 1 ebikes. Regulations vary, but in general a Class 1 ebike must be pedal assist only, with a maximum assisted speed of 20 mph. The motor’s power must be limited to 1 horsepower, or 750 watts. With the Levo, the S-Works motor has a max output of 720 watts, and a max torque of 111 Nm. The previous levo had 90 Nm of torque and 565 watts of power, so the increase is substantial. Keep in mind that the increased torque and power does drain the battery more quickly, so it’s a good thing that 840 Wh is the stock option. 85 - 90 Nm of torque and 600 watts of power used to seem seem like the standard, until DJI arrived on the scene with their Avinox motor, which has 105 Nm of torque and 850 W of peak power, and a Boost mode that bumps those numbers up even further for a 30 second burst. There are rumors that Bosch will be increasing the output of their Gen 5 motors later this year, but that hasn’t officially been confirmed. As for the actual construction of the motor (it’s manufactured by Brose), it uses larger, full metal gears that have undergone a surface treatment to increase their durability. Care was taken to reduce the amount of noise and vibrations produced by the motor, and so far that seems to have paid off out on the trail - I didn’t find the whir of the motor to be overly loud or annoyingly whiny. Display / Modes Specialized’s Mastermind top tube display has always been one of the best, and that continues with the Levo. There’s a huge range of customizable screens, and Bluetooth devices can also be connected to it. The motor modes can easily be customized in the app, where there are presets that can skew the output to use less battery, or to provide maximum assistance for riders that want to cover the most distance in the shortest amount of time. Along with the Eco, Trail, and Turbo, and Auto modes, Micro Tune and Dynamic Micro Tune can be accessed from the remote. Dynamic Micro Tune has become the mode I use the most - it gives access to all the motor’s power when you pedal hard, but saves battery by doling out less power when you’re not mashing on the pedals. You can select the amount of assistance in increments of 10, so 10/100, 20/100, 30/100, etc… will show up at the top of the screen. The handlebar remote has a new shape, with a small toggle the sticks up out of the middle between the power and walk buttons. It took me some time to get used to the toggle - I still prefer the old Levo’s two button switch - but after a month of riding it’s feeling much more familiar. Playing with fire There’s a menu option on the Levo that’s going to raise some eyebrows, and rightly so. By pressing a few buttons on the controller, you can increase the Levo’s max speed limit to 28 mph, effectively turning it into a Class 3 e-bike, at least on the versions sold in the US. (Class 3 e-bikes are pedal-assisted and have a max assisted speed of 28 mph). A large Class 3 logo shows up on the display to let others know that you’ve unlocked the cheat code. It’s mainly designed for riders that have a paved road commute to the trailhead, and in that scenario it really is nice to have, since you don’t get that lurching sensation when the 20mph limit is reached. That said, I think Specialized are playing with fire by enabling this mode; it’s like leaving a bowl of Halloween candy out with a "Take one" sign - I’m sure there will be plenty of riders who use the mode in areas where it’s not allowed. It’s not going to affect average climbing speeds, since it’s rare that you’d be able to go up any sort of incline at 20mph, but on more rolling terrain it will bump up the average speed that riders can maintain. We’ll see if it sticks around - I’d vote that it gets removed, even if that means it takes a few more minutes of commuting to get to the trails. That little nubbin helps keep the range extender in place. A 280 Wh range extender is available aftermarket, bumping the total battery capacity up to 1120 Wh. Super Charger Specialized are also releasing a 12 amp “Super Charger” battery charger that can take the battery level from 0% to 80% in less than an hour, a handy feature for all the forgetful riders out there. Along with the fast charge mode, it also has an Eco mode that’s designed to help increase the lifespan of the battery. Geometry There’s no shortage of geometry possibilities with the Levo, but the base numbers aren’t that much different than the last Levo. The head angle can be altered by + or - 1-degree by swapping out the upper headset cup, the bottom bracket height can be raised or lowered via a flip chip at the rear shock mount, and the chainstay length can be changed by 9mm, from 435 to 443mm with the flip chips on the rear chainstay. The nice thing is that the adjustments can be done fairly independently, rather than having one change dramatically alter all of the other geometry numbers. Suspension Design The Levo 5 uses a 210 x 55mm shock to deliver 150mm of rear travel via a Horst Link layout. It's equipped with a piggyback version of the two-chamber Genie shock that debuted on the Stumpjumper last year - for a deeper dive on the inner workings of that shock, Dario Digiulio has the scoop here . The bike is also compatible with standard shocks as well. Specifications Price $13499 Travel 150mm Rear Shock Fox Float X w/ Genie Fork Fox Float 38 Factory, 160mm Cassette SRAM XX Eagle Transmission, 10-52 Crankarms SRAM XX carbon, 160mm Bottom Bracket Specialized S-Works 3.1 motor Rear Derailleur SRAM XX Eagle Transmission Chain SRAM XX Eagle FlatTop Shifter Pods SRAM AXS Pod Handlebar Race Face Era Carbon 40mm rise Grips Deity Lockjaw Brakes SRAM Maven Ultimate Wheelset Roval Traverse HD Tires Specialized Butcher Grid Gravity Seat Specialized Power Pro Mirror Seatpost Fox Transfer Neo Factory Compare to other All Mountain/Enduro/XC Test Bike Setup 195 psi in the Fox Genie shock put me right at 30% sag, and I stuck with that amount for the duration of testing. I trimmed the bars down to 770mm, and the only other change I made was a swap to a 200mm front rotor on the SRAM Maven brakes, downsizing from the stock 220 setup. Testing Info Testing took place in Bellingham, Washington, as winter faded away and hints of spring started to show up. That meant the bike saw plenty of muddy rides until the sun started to stay out long enough to create heroic conditions. Mike Kazimer Location: Bellingham, WA, USA Height: 5'11" / 180cm Inseam: 33" / 84cm Weight: 160 lbs / 72.6 kg Industry affiliations / sponsors: None Instagram: @mikekazimer Download the Trailforks App Climbing I’ll start with the motor performance, since that’s going to be the big question on many riders’ minds. Is the extra ‘oomph’ noticeable? It certainly is, especially on really steep climbs - there’s no drop off in the level of assistance, just a consistent push to get you to the top. On extra-techy sections of trail, the type with moves that slow speeds down to a crawl, the torque is especially impressive - pushing down on the pedals results in an instant surge of power, perfect for getting up and over rooty steps. There’s absolutely zero delay, even when you’re starting from a standstill. The cadence required to make the most out of the motor isn’t wildly fast either - Specialized says it's around 80 rpm, and I didn’t feel like I needed to alter my usual pedal speed that much to maintain a good level of assistance. I didn’t have too many pedal strikes, but riders in rockier areas may want to swap out the fancy 160mm carbon cranks for something shorter, and made of metal. The overall speed of the bike is what really stands out - I knew I was getting places faster than usual, but it wasn’t until looking at my stats that I’d realize just how much vert I’d knocked out in a short amount of time. As always, range amounts will vary depending on rider weight and terrain - if you get Turbo drunk it’s possible to use all 840 watts in under two hours, but by staying in Trail mode a little more often (which is still very quick even in the stock setting) the range increases significantly. I was able to get around 23 miles of range and 4,000 vertical feet of climbing out of a full battery, and that’s using a mix of Turbo and Trail modes. As far as the overall climbing position goes, I don’t have any complaints - the slightly steeper seat angle was appreciated, and the higher rise bars help create an upright and comfortable setup. Descending The Levo and the Stumpjumper share similar geometry, and I’ve spent hours and hours on both bikes in the past, so it didn’t take long to get up to speed on the latest Levo. I tried a few different geo settings for experimentation’s sake and ended up preferring the long chainstay, low bottom bracket, and neutral head angle position - in that setting the front end didn’t feel overly sluggish on more rolling terrain, while still being slack to feel right at home in the steeps. There’s been plenty of questions about the Genie shock, as is typically the case whenever a company produces something that deviates from the norm. Realistically, the design is simply a way to add a little more tunability to the air spring’s curve and to increase the bottom out resistance available, even on a bike with a more linear leverage curve. The Levo comes with one volume spacer in the outer chamber, and I stayed with that setup - the shock and fork felt balanced, with a good level of support to keep the bike from feeling too mushy or wallowy. I didn’t have any harsh bottom-outs either, which isn’t all that surprising, since the shock’s design makes that almost impossible, but it is a handy feature to have when you’re plummeting back to earth aboard a 50+ pound machine. On that note, it’s probably time to discuss the Levo’s weight. Yes, there’s now an 840 Wh battery in that big down tube, but these days 52 pounds isn’t all that light. Look at the recently launched Norco Sight VLT - it’s 49 pounds with an 800 Wh battery, and that’s a high pivot design with an idler pulley. To be fair, that weight is with a Lyrik and trail casing tires, so it’s not exactly a totally even comparison. Or take the Transition Regulator, which comes in a 47 pounds with a 600 Wh battery - add a range extender onto that and you’re still looking at a sub-50 pound bike. Am I being picky? Maybe a little, but weight does make a difference in how a bike handles, and the Levo doesn’t feel nearly as maneuverable and energetic as the Regulator, despite having very similar geometry. It takes more effort to toss it around, and it feels more, well, e-bikey than some of the other options out there. Part of me wishes Specialized had given the Levo more travel - I’d be a little more lenient when it comes to weight if this was designed to be a DH smasher as opposed to more of an all-rounder. It’s approved for up to a 180mm fork - maybe we’ll see a longer travel version released that can take advantage of that capability, since the geometry is already pretty much there. Don’t get me wrong, the Levo’s no lethargic pig - it’s still a blast to plow through chopped up and rutted descents, and it does a very good job of making the most out of that 150mm of travel. Not surprisingly, it has a lot in common with the previous model, with nice and neutral trail manners, at least if you ride with a little more aggression than you would on a non-motorized bike. Specialized Levo 4 Transition Regulator CX How Does It Compare? I had the Transition Regulator on hand at the same time as the Levo, so let’s start with that comparison. Both bikes have 150mm of travel with a 160mm fork, although the travel can be increased on the Regulator by running a longer stroke shock. The Regulator doesn’t have as many geometry options as the Levo, but I have a feeling that the settings most riders will end up at will be pretty similar between the two. The Regulator does have longer chainstays - 448 or 450mm vs 443mm for the Levo in the long setting. Other than the slight geometry differences, it’s the motor, battery size, and weight that separates the two - the Regulator uses Bosch’s gen 5 motor with a 600 Wh battery and checks in at 47.25 pounds, while the Levo’s Specialized motor ant 840 Wh battery puts it at 52.3 pounds. All the stats and numbers aside, out on the trail I felt more comfortable on the Regulator - it’s easier to toss around thanks to that lower weight, all while still maintaining excellent traction. I prefer the slightly longer chainstays as well - they add an element of centered stability that’s not quite as accessible on the Levo. The Levo’s suspension is calm and composed, but even with that fancy Genie shock the Levo’s suspension never felt quite as supple and ground hugging as the Regulator. On paper it looks like the Specialized motor should blow the Bosch out of the water, but that’s not really the case out in the real world. The Specialized motor does an excellent job of maintaining a consistent level of assistance on steep climbs, and the extra torque is noticeable in situations when you’re almost stalled out and need to put the power down to keep moving forward, but the Bosch is no slouch in that department either. I’ll put it this way - I didn’t feel like the Specialized motor allowed me to get up anything that I couldn’t also climb with the Bosch motor. Overall, the Levo and the Regulator are approaching the task of creating an all-mountain eMTB from two different angles. The Levo has a bigger battery capacity, a higher max power output, and a wider range of geometry options, while the Regulator saves weight at the cost of battery size and ease of battery access. For me, the Regulator ticks more of the boxes - it’s closer to the dream of a 40ish pound, full-power eMTB with enough battery for big rides. The Amflow PL Carbon is also a part of nearly every eMTB conversation these days, and for good reason - it’s a 44 lb bike with an 800 Wh battery and a motor that puts out 105 Nm of torque with 850 watts of peak power. To be fair, that weight is for a lightweight build with Fox 36; it'd be a few pounds heavier with the same parts as the Levo 4. At the moment, it’s not easy to obtain that bike in the US, but several companies will be announcing bikes that use the DJI motor in the coming days - we’ll get a real-world comparison going as soon as possible. Which Model is the Best Value? At the risk of sounding like a broken record, the best value is always going to be a bike without a motor - Stumpjumper 15 alloy frames are currently on sale for $1,500. Digging into the spec sheets of the Levo 4, the Levo 4 Expert model is the one that catches my eye. It has Fox's Performance Elite suspension, Maven silver brakes, Roval alloy wheels with DT Swiss 370 hubs, and SRAM's wireless GX Transmission drivetrain. It's a solid build kit that doesn't need any immediate upgrades. Granted, for $9,499 you'd hope it'd be fully dialed. Value probably isn't the term I would use here, but that build kit is the closest to how I'd build up a bike of my own if I was trying to maximize the performance without going fully bankrupt. Technical Report Fox Transfer Neo dropper post: The Neo post’s response time is incredibly fast - it feels instant, and a slight push of the trigger is all it takes to lower or raise the seat. It’s held up well to plenty of mud and grit, and I’ve managed to remember to charge the battery often enough to keep it from dying out on the trail. That’s really my only tiny nitpick (other than the post's sky high price) - I wish the it was hardwired to the main battery somehow, so that there was only one battery to charge. Otherwise it’s currently the best wireless dropper post that I’ve used, a list that includes the newest RockShox Reverb AXS, KS Lev Circuit, and the TranzX EDP01. Butcher Grid Gravity Tires: It’s great to see proper tires spec’d on a full power eMTB - the higher speeds and heavier overall system weight mean that DH tires are the way to go to prevent flats or vague handling. Specialized’s T9 compound isn’t the absolute stickiest, but it holds its own when things are wet and slimy, and the tread pattern is easy to get along with in a variety of conditions. SRAM Maven brakes: Mavens are ideal for slowing down big e-bikes. Sometimes, almost too good - after having a couple of unwanted front wheel slides I swapped out the 220mm rotor for a 200 in order to tone things down a little. I’d rather have too much power than not enough, and for most riders the 220 / 200 combo will be fine, but if you find yourself overbraking, swapping to a smaller or thinner rotor should do the trick. Pros + Instant power delivery, and plenty of it + 840 Wh battery as the stock configuration + Very adaptable, whether that's geometry or battery size Cons - Heavier than expected - Deserves more travel - Class 3 option shouldn't be a feature Pinkbike's Take I spent a lot of time on the previous Levo, and back in 2021 I called it ‘the new benchmark.” At the time, it was a much more refined offering than the other options on the market. Four years later and the eMTB landscape isn't as desolate looking - it’s as if a sleepy village with one restaurant morphed into a bustling metropolis with no shortage of good food options. That makes it harder to stand out, and even with the extra torque and power the Levo 4 doesn't tower over the competition. Still, the new motor is very impressive, as is the bike's adaptability - for riders who want an e-bike that can be turned into almost anything, the Levo could be the one. — Mike Kazimer Video / Action Shots: Doug Jambor Posted In: Reviews and Tech eMTB Reviews Trail Bikes Specialized Specialized Levo Levo 4 Author Info: Mike Kazimer Bellingham, WA, United States Member since Feb 1, 2009 1,975 articles Based in Bellingham, Washington, Mike Kazimer is Editor in Chief at Pinkbike, where he’s been covering all things mountain bike related for the last 14 years. Before joining Pinkbike, he worked in bike and ski shops for over a decade. Between all the biking and skiing he managed to pick up an English degree from Western Colorado University.

title: Oak Components Levers Claim Increased Power for Hope Tech 4 V4 Brakes
author: Dario DiGiulio
article_text: The folks at Oak Components have been hard at work recently, pumping out all sorts of aftermarket levers to alter the feel and function of many of the most popular brake systems on the market. Their most recent launch targets the Hope Tech 4 V4, which proved to be one of our favorite options in last year's Big Brake Test . With impressive power and a very light lever feel, the Tech 4 V4 is an excellent option for those looking outside the norms of the big S brands. Though we didn't find it to be an issue, some users have complained about the shape of the Hope lever, which is a little more square at the grip point than other options. Oak wanted to address this and add some features of their own, leading to the HT4 lever you see here. Beyond that, they added a bit of length to the lever blade itself, increasing the mechanical advantage and making modulation a little bit easier. Longer lever, longer arc, more distance to cover while pushing the same master cylinder through its cycle. For reference, the stock lever is 87mm from pivot to tip, where the Oak lever is 93mm. As a result, you will likely have to move your brakes inboard slightly to account for the difference. For folks like me with larger hands, this could make fitting the brakes on all cockpits challenging, as they already sit a bit further inboard. With the Oak lever installed, the Hope's adjustments are the same as stock, with separate reach and bite point adjusters allowing you to fine-tune the position and throw of the brake. SRAM still wins when it comes to the range of adjustment baked into a brake, thanks to their Contact Point adjustment feature, but the Tech 4 is closely competitive, providing a wide range of independent adjustment. There are ten colors available, with a rainbow of anodization to match your special build. A pair of lever blades retails for $159 USD, and includes all the hardware required to install them on your Tech 4 V4s. Having installed a few Oak lever kits at this point, I'd call the Hope kit the easiest by a margin, thanks primarily to Hope's service-minded design principles. A few common tools is all it took, and the job was done in about 10 minutes. For complex jobs, Oak does a good job of publishing how-to videos, but this kit felt easy enough to complete on intuition alone. The Oak lever has a rounder, more hooked tip. Both feature dimpling for grip. Ride Impressions If you watched our Big Brake Test last year, then you'll recall that the Hope Tech 4 V4 was one of the top contenders from our 10-brake lineup. They feature a very light lever action with a very consistent bite point, and have all the power you need for the steepest and most demanding trails. One of our only complaints about the brakes out on trail had to do with the flex felt in the system after you'd hit the bite point. During heavy braking moments, you can pull beyond the bite point, without much increase in power. This isn't a major con, but is a phenomenon that bothers some riders. I bring that up to touch on the one con I've experienced with the Oak levers. Due to the longer lever blade and increased leverage, that flex in the system is more apparent. Where the flex didn't really bother me on the stock levers, it became a bit odd feeling with the very stiff Oak levers installed, but that's a detail one could easily get used to with time. Other than that one element, the Oaks are a nice feeling upgrade for the Hopes. I wouldn't say that I felt the stock levers to be uncomfortable or lacking, but if you're sensitive to the shape of the lever blade, then perhaps the Hope blades are a bit square in shape. The extra hook on the Oaks is great, and allows you to really perch at the tip of the lever, giving even more control over light-touch braking moments. You can learn more about Oak's HT4 lever kit - as well as the rest of their lineup - at their website . Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Brakes Hope Oak Components Hope Tech 4 Oak Ht4 Author Info: Dario DiGiulio Bellingham, WA, United States Member since Dec 25, 2016 491 articles Dario has been obsessed with bikes from an early age, and has worked in the industry since 2015. With a Bachelor's in Architecture from Cal Poly, San Luis Obispo, he aims to bring that mix of creativity and technicality to his work in the mountain bike world.

title: Review: The Pivot Shadowcat is Niche & Nimble
author: Jessie-May Morgan
article_text: REVIEW Pivot Shadowcat WORDS: Jessie-May Morgan PHOTOS: Finlay Anderson Pivot introduced the Shadowcat in 2022, adding it to what is now a vanishingly small group of bikes to put 27.5" wheels on a mid-travel platform. A configuration that would have been fairly standard 10 years ago is now a rarity, thanks to the rise of 29" wheels and the broad appreciation for their improved rollover capability. Nevertheless, as niche as it now is, the complete 27.5" wheelset still has its place in the lineups of some big brands. These smaller bikes are oft pushed as fun-focused, with less flipped on its head to actually mean more. The Pivot Shadowcat, with its 160mm fork and 140mm of rear wheel travel, is no different, with words like "agile," "lithe," and "feline creative mayhem" littering the marketing copy. Pivot Shadowcat Details • Carbon frame • Travel: 140mm / 160mm fork • Wheel size: 27.5" • 65.8º head angle • 76º seat tube angle • 430mm chainstays • Sizes: XS, S, M & L • Weight: 27.6 lb / 12.5 kg (size S) • Price: $8,899 USD (Pro XT/XTR w/ Carbon Wheels) • pivotcycles.com It all sounds incredibly attractive, and I can't deny I was very excited to give this one a shot. Alas, I shan't pretend I didn't miss the rather more carefree riding style that a 29" front wheel allows, but happily there's a bit more to the Shadowcat than its numbers alone. The Shadowcat is offered in no fewer than eight complete builds, with prices ranging from $6,399 USD to $11,399 USD, all of which get the same custom-tuned Fox Float DPS Factory shock. I tested the $8,899 USD Pro XT/XTR w/ Carbon Wheels, which comes with Shimano Deore XT brakes, a 160mm Fox 36 with the FIT4 damper, and a 150mm Fox Transfer Factory dropper seat post. It rolls on Reynolds Blacklabel Trail Pro carbon wheels with an Industry Nine Hydra hubset, mounted with Maxxis Dissector 2.4" tires in the EXO casing. Contents Introduction Construction & Features Geometry & Sizing Suspension Design Specifications Setup Climbing Descending Technical Report What's the Best Value? How Does it Compare? Pros & Cons Pinkbike's Take Credit: Finlay Anderson The Shadowcat was at its best when I slowed things down a little, instead of just trying to ride fast everywhere. Its quick, responsive handling and poppy suspension encouraged me to seek out little hops and alternative lines off the main rut, which was a great deal more pleasurable than just trying to plow down — Jessie-May Morgan No thru-headset routing here, nor is there on any bike in the Pivot range. Construction & Features The Shadowcat is lightweight, with our size small test bike tipping the scales at just 12.5 kg (27.6 lbs). Like all Pivot frames, the Shadowcat gets a size-specific carbon layup. So, for each frame size, the orientation and positioning of the carbon fibers is subtly different, and the resulting tube sizes are appreciably different. The aim is to give all riders, no matter their chosen frame size, the same ride quality. They say that torsional stiffness and vertical compliance should “feel” the same whether you're a 5’2” rider on an XS or a 6’5” rider on an XL. While some Pivot trail bikes run a SuperBoost swingarm for increased stiffness, the frame designers did not deem that necessary here. Five sets of Enduro MAX bearings articulate two short aluminum links relative to the front triangle. All are of a considerable diameter that lends itself well to durability. LLU seals with a dual-lip sit against the inner race, helping to minimize contamination. Cable routing is internal, though happily not through the headset. While some carbon frame manufacturers go to the pains of molding in internal guides for cables and the rear brake hose, Pivot is not one of them. However, nicely executed ports at the entry points seem to do a good job of holding them securely in place, such that there is no rattle to report. There is also a handy hatch on the bottom of the downtube that grants access for routing the dropper cable up into the seat tube. Indeed, the Shadowcat is quiet. No cable rattle, and no undue noise from chain slap, credit to very well-considered frame protection on the drive side chainstay and seat stay. The lower link also gets some molded rubber protection, largely in place to prevent loam and stones from getting stuffed into the tight space between the link and the back of the seat tube where it could become problematic. Again, this is extremely well-executed, and has remained in place throughout this three-month review period. The downtube gets four bottle bosses, leaving good scope for altering position. Using the lower position, the small frame easily accepts a 750ml bottle. There's space for a second bottle on the underside of the downtube, and another set of bosses on the top tube offer a secure mounting position for a multitool, such as Pivot's Phoenix Dock Tool . Geometry & Sizing The Pivot Shadowcat is available in sizes XS, S, M & L, with reach figures of 410mm, 430mm, 460mm and 480mm. The tallest of riders (6ft+) are unlikely to be well-served by the Shadowcat, and may find a more appropriate fit on one of Pivot's 29" wheeled options, such as the new Switchblade . Sizing options are very good for shorter riders, however, thanks to the short standover height (638mm on S), and short, straight seat tubes that can accommodate long-travel dropper seat posts. While Pivot does dabble in size-specific chainstays on the Firebird, Switchblade and Shuttle SL, the rear-center length is fixed at 430mm across all sizes for Shadowcat. When asked why, Pivot said. "The Shadowcat is designed to be a really light and nimble 27.5” wheeled trail bike. Keeping those chainstays short really enhances the agility of the bike in tight and twisty trails, and for making quick changes or corrections in direction. Longer chainstays would diminish this key characteristic of the Shadowcat. Extended chainstay lengths are really more of a benefit for really tall riders with high saddle positions, or for more aggressive gravity-focused bikes like the Firebird which does have size-specific chainstay lengths, and where stability at speed on steep terrain is more of a priority over agility." The Shadowcat's 160mm fork gives it a head angle of 65.8°, which may seem reasonable for a 140mm travel trail bike, but it is certainly on the conservative side. There's now a good number of short- and mid-travel mountain bikes with considerably slacker head tube angles that can make for improved confidence and stability in steep terrain. Pivot publishes a 76° seat tube angle for all frame sizes. They don't measure effective seat tube angle in the same way as other manufacturers, so comparing a Pivot's seat tube angle with almost any other bike's seat tube angle is like comparing apples with oranges. While most other brands measure effective seat angle at the height of the head tube, Pivot measure it at what they consider to be the average saddle height for a specific frame. That said, they don't publish what that average saddle height is, so it can be difficult to gauge whether your specific effective seat tube angle will be the same as, slacker, or steeper than the published 76°. Can the Shadowcat go MX? Absolutely. However, since the geometry is designed around a 160mm 27.5" fork, raising the front end with an equivalent travel 29" fork and front wheel would be detrimental to the bike's handling and geometry. Pivot recommend dropping to a 130mm fork if you want to run a 29" front wheel to get a mullet configuration. Alas, I did not test the bike in this setup. Suspension Design The Shadowcat runs Pivot's proven take on the dw-link suspension platform, wherein two short links connect the swingarm to the front triangle, co-rotating as the rear wheel is displaced. The top rocker link drives a trunnion mount shock with a 55mm stroke to damp 140mm of rear wheel travel. The leverage curve is progressive (31.8% overall progression). While all models of the Shadowcat are sold with an air shock, the leverage curve would work well with the linear spring of a coil shock, and indeed there is clearance for a Fox DHX2. While they do opt for size-specific shock tunes on the Mach 4 SL XC bike, Pivot don’t bother with that for the Shadowcat. Instead, they use the tunability and adjustments of the Fox Float DPS shock to cater to a wide range of rider weights. They're able to do that by virtue of the three compression positions for the Open setting. Position 1 is recommended for the lighter riders (around 110 lbs, or 50 kg), Position 2 for heavier riders between 185-200 lbs (84-91 kg), and the 3rd Position for riders over 200lbs (91+ kg). Pivot tell us the rebound tuning range is broad enough to accommodate a wide range of rider weights. 0 seconds of 8 seconds Volume 90% https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/YlJ1d7Uo Copied Live 00:00 00:08 00:08 Specifications Release Date 2022 Price $8899 Travel 140mm Rear Shock Fox Factory Float DPS Fork 160mm Fox Factory 36, 44mm offset, FIT4 Damper Headset Pivot Precision Sealed Integrated Cartridge Cassette Shimano XT M8100 Crankarms Race Face Æffect R Rear Derailleur Shimano XTR M9100 SGS 12-Speed Shifter Pods Shimano XT M8100 Handlebar Phoenix Team Low Rise Carbon Stem Phoenix Team Enduro/Trail - 45mm Grips Phoenix Factory Lock-On Brakes Shimano XT M8120 4-piston Wheelset Reynolds Blacklabel Trail Pro Hubs Industry Nine Hydra Tires Maxxis Dissector 27.5" x 2.4", EXO Seat Phoenix WTB Pro High Tail Trail Seatpost Fox Transfer Factory Compare to other All Mountain/Enduro/XC RIDING THE Pivot Shadowcat Test Bike Setup I tested the Shadowcat Pro XT/XTR with Carbon Wheels in a size small, with a 430mm reach. The bike comes with a 760mm Phoenix Team Low Rise Carbon handlebar that I cut down to my preferred 740mm. I was happy enough with the 45mm stem. The bike arrived with the stem positioned high on the steerer tube, but I preferred the bike's handling with a lower ride height, with just a 10mm spacer underneath the stem. For the suspension, 120 psi in the Fox Float DPS Factory shock put me at 30% sag. As supplied, the shock was home to a 0.6 in³ spacer that I removed for the latter half of the review period. For the most part, I ran the shock fully open on the compression damping, and fully open on the rebound, too, for the fastest return speed. I removed the fork's single volume spacer and pressurized it to 66 psi, with compression and rebound fully open. Jessie-May Morgan Location: Tweed Valley, Scotland Height: 5'4" / 163cm Inseam: 30" / 77cm Weight: 130 lbs / 59 kg Industry affiliations / sponsors: None Instagram: @jessiemaymorgan The shock has a three position lever for compression damping adjustments. The firm position feels almost like a compete lock-out. The medium position is much more supple, while the open position gives the least amount of resistance through compression. The shock also has an "Open Mode Adjust" dial, with another three positions on offer. Setting 1 is the most plush, while setting 3 is the most firm. Conditions were wet and muddy for most of the test period, not terribly ideal for the Maxxis Dissector tires. They were totally sufficient for hard pack trail center, but for straying off the overly-beaten track, I wanted something with a little more bite for softer, fresh-cut trails. Extra tread was provided by a Maxxis Minion DHF, DHRII pairing. Download the Trailforks App Testing Info Testing took place over the last three months in the Tweed Valley, Scotland, at the Glentress trail centre and the enduro and downhill tracks around Innerleithen. Trail conditions were largely wet, with some damp greasy days as well as sopping muddy ones. The Shadowcat is capable of handling a good variety of terrain. This Trailforks route includes a bunch of trails that were ridden during the test period. Climbing The Shadowcat's seated pedaling position feels a little off-the-back. While the geometry chart states a 76° effective seat tube angle, it's actually as slack as 75° for me at my saddle height of 628mm. That's measuring to the center of the saddle rails. Luckily, pushing the saddle forward on its rails buys me an extra 1.5°, giving an effective seat angle of 76.5°. That steeper angle is a little closer, but still a way off, the 77.5°+ angles I've grown accustomed to, and am most comfortable on. Even then, it still feels a little too biased toward the rear, but is much more manageable. It's most prominently felt while pedaling up very steep singletrack, experienced as a tendency of the front wheel to lift unless I'm very aggressively leaning down to the stem. As a result, I found myself opting to ride fire roads and mellower gradient singletrack on the ascent, instead of steeper, more challenging singletrack options. Running reduced sag in the shock (25%) alleviates some of the issues with the slack seat tube angle, but it's far from ideal for descending. I wouldn't recommend implementing that fix unless you are disinterested in descending performance, which I assume you are not. That geometry holds back what would otherwise be an excellent bike for climbing. Traction under power is plentiful, and the comparably steep head tube angle makes navigating tight switchbacks effortless. The suspension does bob while pedaling along flat ground, but as soon as the gradient tips up a little, there is no discernable bob and pedaling feels incredibly efficient. The firmer, middle shock position is useful to eliminate that pedal bob along the flat, but becomes entirely unnecessary when the bike is pointed uphill. I found it easy enough to reach down and flick the lever between positions. There is a cable port on the underside of the top tube for those who want a remote option. Descending The Shadowcat is a well-balanced bike, with predictable handling. The suspension has the rear wheel tracking beautifully through rough terrain. It's not the plushest feeling suspension, but it provides a solid, dependable feeling through the pedals that is firm enough to let you know it's working hard, but it never feels overwhelmed. The shock tune is sufficient for me at 60kg, but I am running the dials at the extreme (open) end of the adjustment range, so am unaware of any additional performance that could be had from an even lighter tune. The small is fitted with a Fox Transfer Factory post with 150mm drop, which to me is the absolute minimum acceptable drop on a trail bike. I found that my thighs would lightly tap against the top of the saddle on most descents, even those with a fairly moderate gradient. That said, at my saddle height of 628mm, it is possible to run the 175mm Fox Transfer thanks to the seat tubes plentiful insertion depth (Pivot has a fit guide for Fox Transfer and Reverb AXS posts here ). I did simulate that lower dropped saddle position, but it actually wasn't preferable, given that the saddle then contacted the inside of my knee in a problematic fashion. The caveat here is, of course, that I do run an inordinately wide saddle (detailed explanation of why, here ). So, others who run a much narrower saddle are more likely to benefit from that additional drop. I'm also running it slammed forwards on the rails to get that more comfortable effective seat tube angle. With the saddle positioned in the middle of the rails, or toward the back, that wide portion of the saddle is less interfering. The fairly steep 65.8° head tube angle holds the Shadowcat back on steep, fall line trails. It helps give the bike quick handling on trails with less gradient, and keeping weight pushed through the front wheel comes naturally, giving good confidence in flatter turns. But, it does have its rider feeling a little pitched and vulnerable when the hill falls away. This positions it as a bike that is most at home on more easy-going terrain that isn't overly challenging, or too littered with drops and steps in close succession of one another. I found the Shadowcat to be at its best when I slowed things down a little, instead of just trying to ride fast everywhere. Its quick, responsive handling and poppy suspension encouraged me to seek out little hops and alternative lines off the main rut, which was a great deal more pleasurable than just trying to plow down. It has also been a fun, dependable, and safe-feeling bike for sessioning the larger jumps I've taken to riding recently as part of the old "continuing professional development." The bike's progressive leverage curve, combined with the 0.6in³ volume spacer inside the shock (same on all sizes), made it difficult to push the bike toward the end of its travel. I occasionally felt as though I'd bottomed-out, but a glance at the shock's O-ring position indicated that was not the case. I was effectively hitting a wall of progression. Removing that spacer allowed me to access the last 10mm suspension travel with less force. I probably went a little too far with it; the 0.2³ spacer would've been the better option. Anyway, the point is that the Fox Float DPS is nicely tunable in that aspect. However, Pivot do not provide the volume spacers with the sale of the bike; those must be purchased aftermarket for around $25 USD. Pivot Shadowcat Yeti SB135 How does it compare? It's true that the number of 27.5" mid-travel trail bikes has diminished in recent years, leaving the Yeti SB135 as the Pivot Shadowcat's closest competitor. I haven't ridden it myself, but Dario's review describes it was a "a fun-focused trail bike that can handle long days of hard pedaling as well as a day sessioning jumps and features. The geometry and feel of the bike add plenty of excitement to mellower sections of trail, but with enough capability to push things into some gnarlier terrain." The only fair comparison to make in this case is the on-paper one. The Yeti is ever so slightly slacker than the Shadowcat with a 65.4° head angle. It also has slightly less rear wheel travel at 135mm, but runs the larger volume piggyback-equipped Fox Float X shock, which could make it the better option for bigger days where meters of descending are the metric of interest, rather than meters of ascending. Neither Pivot or Yeti are known for their affordability. The $9,000 SB135 LR T3 model fetches you an (arguably) favorable spec over the $8,889 Shadowcat tested here. It runs an electronic SRAM X0 Transmission, and a Fox 36 Factory with the higher-end GRIP2 damper, but runs a heavier aluminum wheelset with the Race Face ARC offset rim. Which Model is the Best Value? Price for the Pivot Shadowcat ranges from $5,499 USD up to an eye-watering $11,399 USD. As all eight models run the very same fork and shock, the performance benefits to be had from the more expensive models are, to my mind, marginal at best. At the more affordable end of the spectrum, the $6,399 Ride SLX/XT model and the (on sale) $5,499 Ride GX/X01 model offer all the performance of the $8,899 model tested here, with only a small weight penalty to accept. That's mostly owing to the use of an aluminum wheelset in the form of the DT Swiss M1900 with the 370 hub. If I was parting with my own cash here, the more expensive models wouldn't get a look in. Pivot Phoenix Factory Lock-On Grips. Maxxis Dissector EXO tire, front and rear. The 4-Piston Shimano XT brakes did not leave me wanting. The small has sufficient insertion depth for a 175mm Fox Transfer. Technical Report Fox 36 Factory Fork with FIT4 Damper: The fork felt a little over-damped for this lighter rider. I've had more comfort out of the higher-end GRIP2 damper before now. Alas, none of the eight Pivot Shadowcat models are furnished with it. Shimano XT M8120 4-Piston Brakes: The XT brakes were plenty powerful and consistent, and I appreciated the light lever feel. It's good to see them spec'd with the higher-end 180mm IceTech Freeza rotors, the benefits of which I'm sure will be more easily appreciated on long descents on a hot summer's day. Maxxis Dissector EXO tires: These are relatively fast-rolling and supple, and are well-suited to the bike's intentions. A more aggressive front tire, like an Assegai or Minion DHF will be beneficial to those riding in soft, sloppy conditions, and heavier riders or those frequently riding rocky trails will benefit from at least a DoubleDown casing on the rear. Pivot Phoenix Factory Lock-On Grips: These were a surprise highlight. They have a relatively narrow profile that is tapered such that they are narrower on the inboard end. The rubber is thicker under the palm for more vibration damping, and thinner underneath where your fingers wrap. I like them a lot. Pivot sell them aftermarket in a range of colors for $29.99 USD. While a 27.5" front wheel does deliver lighter, quicker handling, it is more easily hung-up on chunkier lines. Riders on the shorter end of the spectrum may appreciate the former pros, but the fact remains that we all ride the same trails, with the same size bumps and holes, no matter our dimensions. Pros + Lightweight + Well-balanced descender of mellower gradient trails + Suspension is neutral and efficient while climbing + Very quiet, no cable rattle or excessive chain slap + Low standover and room for long travel dropper seat posts Cons - Not comfortable in steep terrain - The seat tube angle could feel too slack for some Pinkbike's Take The Pivot Shadowcat is lightweight, and an efficient climber of hills. It will be ideal for those looking for a consistent and predictable handling trail bike for riding the trail center and a little beyond. It tracks over rough ground very well, but its conservative geometry makes it feel a little vulnerable on steeper, chunkier terrain. — Jessie-May Morgan Visit the high-res gallery for more images from this review. Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Trail Bikes Pivot Pivot Shadowcat Jessie May Morgan Author Info: Jessie-May Morgan Pitfichie, Scotland Member since Oct 26, 2023 402 articles Jessie-May has been working as a mountain bike technical editor since 2018, joining Pinkbike's editorial team in 2023. In a previous life she worked in bioscience research in Edinburgh where she used microscopes to measure things about the immune system. She likes all bikes, but enduro bikes have historically been the preference. Jessie-May has made up the numbers at a handful of World Enduro races, though she is now in her 'downcountry era', and has an unhealthy obsession with handlebars.

title: Review: Shimano XT 8220 Brakes - More Consistency, Same Value
author: Matt Beer
article_text: Shimano XT Brake Review https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/bmdqgWsE Copied Loading ad Live 00:25 00:00 11:03 Shimano's XT level components have always been about high performance at a more reasonable price. They're not quite as light as the top-tier XTR parts, but share almost all of the same features. The XT 8220 model is the four-piston caliper brake - it's what you'll find on most trail and e-bikes sitting on showroom floors. The XT 8200 model uses the same lever but has a lighter, less powerful 2-piston caliper. The 8200 series master cylinders and calipers received a serious makeover with numerous mechanical changes. The main talking point is the low-viscosity mineral oil inside the system, said to improve the “wandering bite point" issues of the past. XT 8220 Brake Details • 2x15, 2x17mm pistons • Tool-free lever reach adjustment • Tooled free-stroke adjustment • Shimano low-viscosity mineral fluid • Non-ambidexterous master cylinders • Rotor diameters: 160, 180, 203, 220mm • Weight: 308g (front w/finned metallic pads) • Price: $265 per end (w/pads, w/o rotors) • shimano.com The redesigned master cylinder and parallel hose are very distinct. A small bolt at the front edge of the master cylinder adjusts the free-stroke (kind of). Pinkbike's Take Shimano has answered the cries of inconsistent brake performance by switching to a lower viscosity fluid, improving seal flexibility, and easing the bleed procedure on the XT 8220 brakes. What's more, the overhauled brakes have retained their value and power, while maintaining a respectable weight for trail and enduro bikes. One thing Shimano didn't deviate from was their Servowave lever actuation. It's unique and has divided consumers: some love the progressive feel, while others prefer a more linear power band. For those opposed to that functionality on previous brakes, new lever ergonomics and a wider range of adjustments could help to change their minds. — Matt Beer Details and Design The most identifying feature of the XT 8200 series brakes is the angle of the hose and master cylinder. Those now run parallel to the handlebar and are tucked out of harm's way. The hose does require trimming down to a minimum length to reduce tapping against the bar over bumps. The master cylinders are still individually shaped for either side of the bars, which can lower the chances of finding replacement bodies, but Shimano has a good reason. They say that this design allows for the bleed port to move to the highest point of the reservoir for the best bleed possible, something harder to achieve with an ambidextrous lever body. Shimano carries on with its Servowave technology in the XT 8200 levers. A cam at the lever pivot maximizes mechanical advantage on the master cylinder as the lever is pulled. This feature is the driver behind Shimano’s brake feel, which we’ll get into in depth in the Ride Impressions section. More tweaks occur within the lever itself. The tool-free reach adjuster is now housed in the lever blade to protect the dial in case of a spill. Each indent is noticeable, and the sweep range is large, so all hand sizes should be able to find their sweet spot. A second adjustment to change the pad contact, or free-stroke, as Shimano calls it, requires a 2mm allen key. This has more of an effect than the previous 8100 brake, but is still less noticeable compared to the range of adjustment offered by SRAM Maven or Hope Tech 4 V4s brakes. Two big updates are found on the caliper: the bleed port screw and the pad retention system. Bleeding the brakes no longer requires an open-ended wrench. Instead, a 3mm Allen key opens the bleed port, which is in a separate location from where the bleed syringe attaches. Shimano also worked to reduce the brake pad rattle in the caliper by creating an oval slot in the brake pads. Front = 308 with finned, metallic pads. Rear = 320g with pads. Price and Weight As I alluded to, XT components present a high performance for their price. The XT 8220 brakes cost $265 USD per end, which includes a set of metal, finned pads. Rotors and adaptors are not included, since those will vary depending on user preferences. The system comes pre-bled, except the lever body is not connected to the 1000mm (front) or 1700mm (rear) brake hoses, since most frames use internal routing. Included is one crush olive washer and needle fitting. The bleed kit, mineral oil, adapters, and rotors are sold separately. XTR is the cream of the crop MTB groupset from Shimano. XTR has always been lighter and more expensive, yet some components look similar to XT. An XTR 9220 front brake weighs 294g and costs $335 USD. I weighed the XT front brake (pads included) at 308g. That’s only 14 grams lighter than the XTR, yet they cost $70 more. So what gives? The 5-degree upswept on the XTR lever. Mono-block caliper for the top-tier Shimano brake. How are they different in terms of design and function from XTR 9220 brakes? The main difference between the XT 8220 and XTR 9220 calipers is that the latter is a one-piece unit, whereas the XT caliper is bolted together. The master cylinder pistons are the same size: 15 and 17mm. Regardless of being bolted together or not, the newer XT and XTR model calipers are stiffer than the outgoing 8120 and 9120 models. The XTR lever uses a funky blade with a 5-degree upsweep and rides on a bearing, instead of a bushing. Is the power any different between the new XTR and XT brakes? I would be hard-pressed to notice any gain in power or performance. Each system uses the same size master cylinder and caliper pistons, as well as brake pads. Specific model rotors exist, but they are interchangeable between brake systems. The biggest factor might come down whether you jive with the upswept lever, or if you care to spend the extra cash. The 3mm bled screw. The hose attaches to the port in front of the screw. Setup and Bleed Essentially, the system is bled from the bottom up using a syringe on the caliper to push fluid and air up the brake line. The process is approachable by a moderately confident mechanic and takes about 15 minutes, depending on how much air is present in the system and how familiar you are with the procedure. Bleeding the system follows the Shimano protocol found here . The only exception to the document listed is that the caliper uses a 3mm allen key to open and close the bleed port. It seems like a small switch-up, but the new split bleed port and screw make life much easier. You’re far less likely to slip the Allen wrench. One potential complication I found difficult to sidestep was attaching the hose to the caliper without introducing an air bubble. Squeezing the hose will help push the fluid to the very end and requires practice. Otherwise, most bleeds were successful and consistent. If you are experiencing wandering bite point issues, and this goes for any brake, it's likely improper oil volume, or trapped air is causing you problems. Revisit the bleed procedure and follow the instructions, or bring the bike to a professional. It’s stated very clearly and loudly on the packaging, but the new low-viscosity fluid is not backwards-compatible with the previous generation brakes. Doing so will render the 8100 series brakes useless as the fluid will degrade the seals. Shimano XT 8220: $265 USD per end. Hayes Dominion A4: $250 USD per end. How do they compare? The Hayes Dominion A4 could be considered one of the most valuable buys in the braking department. These check in at $250 per end and weigh 303g (w/pads, w/o rotor). What makes the Dominion a favorite choice for many is the extremely light lever pull. I listed this as both a Pro and a Con in our review of the brakes and stand by those statements. For some riders, they will appreciate how easy these are on the fingers, while others may struggle to sense where that bite point makes contact with the rotor during technical maneuvers. Like the XTs, once the pads do make contact with the rotor, they have a sensible and usable amount of power. However, I'd rank the Dominions slightly below in terms of maximum power and fade resistance. The metallic pad option will be your best bet for long and steep descents. Livability is simple with the Dominions. The bleed is straightforward in general, plus the dual ports on the caliper allow for isolated bleeding at this end. The lever adjustments are effective, though you still need an Allen key to change the pad contact (like the XTs). Ride Impressions If you were a fan of Shimano brakes before, then you’ll probably love the new XT 8220 even more. The lever adjustments are more meaningful, and the vulnerability of the lever body has been looked after. Both the reach adjust and free stroke have wider ranges to help balance the position and pull of the left and right levers. When it comes to power, the XT 8220s are a fine choice for enduro bikes and even e-bikes in most scenarios. More extreme use cases would be better suited to the Saint, where the power is incredibly strong, as we proved in our Big Bad Brake Test earlier this year. The lever actuation is fairly light, but still takes a small nudge to get moving. There’s also a large amount of deadband or free-stroke to work through. In that sense, they are still very “Shimano” in operation. By that, I mean the power can kick in suddenly. It takes some learning as to where in the lever stroke that power turns on. Once into the power band (holding pad-to-rotor contact), modulation is excellent, with the metallic pads. Various-sized rotors and pad compounds can be used to bridge the gap between all-or-nothing power. When bled according to Shimano’s guidelines, a fair amount of deadstroke exists. Shimano RT66, RT76, CL800 and their other high-end rotors are 1.8mm thick for the purpose of reducing friction, should a rotor be slightly out of alignment. The lever moves quickly through the deadstroke and ramps up the power as the lever is pulled closer to the bar. For riders wishing for even less free-stroke, thicker aftermarket rotors may be the way forward. The consistency or stability of the brake’s hydraulic actuation has been very well looked after with low-viscosity oil and revised seals. You can imagine how guessing when or where these brakes’ fine bite point kicks in could cause inconsistent results when trying to slow down. These XTs have remained consistent for long periods of time without a bleed, even through the cooler months of the year. Previous Shimano brake pads were known to rattle in the caliper. That problem has been diminished massively with the oval-shaped retainer pin, but not entirely eliminated. The pickiest riders will notice some noise and chatter. Shimano vs Galfer rotors and pads We’ve beaten brake talk to death in recent months, but I thought this experiment would shed some light on how thicker aftermarket rotors affect Shimano brakes. I put Shimano’s metallic, finned pads and MT905 rotors to the test against Galfer’s G1851 Advanced pads and the 2.0mm thick Wave rotor (DB034W), both in 203mm diameters. I conducted back-to-back laps with the Brake Ace sensors on Angy M (my go to test track). Shimano pads and rotors went first, followed by the Galfer bits. Keeping all else equal variables (track, conditions, bike, tires, and brake bleed), there were some pronounced differences. The largest factor was the decrease in free-stroke with the thicker Galfer rotor. That doesn’t come as a shocker. What was surprising was how I was using the brakes and their effectiveness. With the Galfer setup, the braking zones were longer - in other words, I spent more time braking. But I used the rear brake more. There were also fewer braking events (Galfer: 54 vs. Shimano: 64). I often caught myself starting to brake too early and pulled too hard with the Shimano pads and rotors, which explains why peak speeds varied more frequently with this setup. Who cares, right? Well, it depends on what you’re trying to achieve. A rough estimation of the start and finish shows that my times varied by less than one second. All of that tells me that my braking habits could turn on and modulate the power better than with the Shimano setup. Data aside, the Shimano setup had more power once the pads contacted the rotors, which I’d choose for freeride moves on super steep slab moves. If I were enduro racing, I might choose the Galfer setup for less chance of stabbing the brake accidentally. Run 1, Shimano XT, metallic pads and MT905 rotor. Run 2, Galfer pads and rotors. Pros + Compact lever redesign is less prone to damage from a crash + Snappier lever feel and more consistent performance from low-viscosity fluid + Improved bleed experience Cons - Difficult to minimize dead stroke - Dual-bar contact points take up more real estate than other single-clamp brake mounts Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Brakes Shimano Shimano Xt Author Info: Matt Beer Squamish, BC, Canada Member since Mar 16, 2001 607 articles Matt has been a Pinkbike reader since the site's early days. He discovered mountain biking in Newfoundland, where a small community of dedicated riders and trail builders set him on a path to earn two Canadian National Downhill Championship titles. Now based in Squamish, BC, Matt works as a tech editor for Pinkbike, specializing in gravity-focused mountain bike reviews, tech, and racing coverage.

title: Review: Specialized Eliminator / Butcher Tires - Great Performance at a Reasonable Price
author: Dario DiGiulio
article_text: Specialized's tire game has gone through some high and lows over the years, with performance and quality changing as they dialed in their designs. As of late, they seem to have found center, offering a solid lineup with good tread patterns and high quality compounds. In the center of the XC-DH spectrum they offer, we have two tires squarely aimed at the trail market, the Butcher and the Eliminator. Comically aggressive names aside, these tires have been a consistent friend of mine, and offer solid performance that I'll get into below. The Eliminator and Butcher both come in Specialized's Grid Trail and Grid Gravity casings, giving you the simple choice between the two based on weight, durability, and ride feel. There are also options between T7 and T9 rubber, with the higher number indicating stickier rubber used in the construction. The Eliminator uses a mix of the two, optimizing for rolling resistance and grip, as it is a rear-specific tire. Specialized Eliminator • Compound: T7 (Trail casing), T7/T9 (Gravity casing) • Casing: 60 TPI (Trail), 2x60 TPI (Gravity) • Sizes: 29" or 27.5" x 2.3" or 2.6" • Weight: 975g (2.3 Trail), 1270g (2.3 Gravity) • Price: $50 - $75 • specialized.com Specialized Butcher • Compound: T7 or T9 • Casing: 60 TPI (Trail), 2x60 TPI (Gravity) • Sizes: 29" or 27.5" x 2.3" or 2.6" • Weight: 1060g (2.3 Trail), 1350g (2.3 Gravity) • Price: $50 - $75 • specialized.com Specialized Butcher / Eliminator Tire Review https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/QrWJIKkk Copied Live 00:00 05:01 05:01 Butcher. Eliminator. Butcher Tread The Butcher's tread pattern is a classic do-it-all, similar to the staggered 2-2 lug designs many brands offer. Specialized gave the tire a consistent edge, without any staggering to break up the leading edge as you lean things over. The separated center lugs let the Butcher dig in a bit more in corners than something like a Maxxis DHR II, which unifies those two to offer a stronger braking surface. While both work nicely as front and rear tires, the Butcher errs a bit more towards the front, thanks to that independent lug design. Eliminator Tread The Eliminator biases towards speed, while still offering some braking bite and a strong edge knob to corner into. Those edge knobs have a bit of a sloping edge to them, which can feel washy in the wrong scenarios, but does catch well when leaned over. The small center knobs alternate in a 3-2-2-3 pattern, with gradually ramped leading edges to keep rolling resistance down. The closest proxy I've recently ridden would be the Maxxis Dissector, which is also positioned as a fast-rolling rear tire for drier conditions. TESTING Download the Trailforks App I've ridden the Butcher all over the world across a ton of varied terrain, and it's one of those tread patterns that works well just about anywhere. The Eliminator is a little more specialty, and seems best suited to faster trails where scrubbing speed is more important than shutting things down fully. Might as well make it a longer loop, as the fast rolling speed means that'll be just a bit easier. PERFORMANCE I've ridden both Grid Trail and Grid Gravity variants of these tires extensively, with time on both the 29" and 27.5" options - the latter only in the rear. My pressures for the Gravity casings tended to be 21psi in the front, and 23psi in the rear. When running Trail casings, 23psi in the front and 25-26psi in the rear tends to feel correct. While I've run the Butcher as a front and rear across a wide array of bikes, the Eliminator has only ever been a rear tire for me. With many sets of these tires in rotation, I have had the opportunity to mount them to a wide array of rims and see how consistent the casings are. The short answer? Very consistent, though larger than advertised. Both Butcher and Eliminator inflate to around 2.4" (61-62mm) across all 30mm rims, with a nicely rounded profile to them. I have not had any issues with wobbly casings, nor have I had a hard time inflating the tires on normal tubeless rims. ROLLING SPEED As a combination (Eliminator rear, Butcher front), these tires feel quick and efficient for trail and enduro bikes alike. You won't mistake them for cross-country tires, but they're certainly not slowing you down as much as something like a Specialized Cannibal, Maxxis Assegai, or Continental Kryptotal Rear. The tread patterns do a good job of bridging space while allowing for traction, and the T7/T9 rubber combo on the Eliminator saves some watts while you're rolling on that centerline. TRACTION Specialized has done a good job with their T9 rubber compound, which offers plenty of grip in wet and dry weather alike. Though it's not the absolute softest rubber out there, T9 tires do a good job of smearing on rocks and roots without any sketchy-feeling breakaway. Straight line braking is very good with the Butcher, both in the front and rear, with good bite in loose soil and no squirm on hard surfaces. The Eliminator shows its speed in loose terrain, struggling to hook up as well as meatier-lugged tires. That said, it does a good job in typical trail bike settings, where metering speed is more the game than shutting things down in the steepest, loosest chutes. CORNERING The Butcher is predictable and easy to intuit once you've found that breakaway point between the center and edge lugs. That point feels far less drastic than something like a Maxxis DHF, more closely resembling a DHRII in that sense. You know there will be a small give, followed by a secure bite, and it hooks up there every time. As a rear tire, you can kick it out when you want to and trust that it will hook up as the bike leans over. I like both the DHR and the Butcher, and would happily run either as a front or rear on just about any bike. The Eliminator is a little more exciting, with a quick breakaway point that catches further into the lean. This is a hard thing to explain in firm terms, but it feels like it wants to schralp corners more than cut a line across them. If your trails are tight, flat, and loose, it might feel a bit sketchy. If you want something fast that can break away, but catch you right before things go bad, it's quite fun. Overall, for the mix of deep soil and well-supported hardpack that I typically ride, it feels well balanced for a fast rolling tire. I prefer the Eliminator to the Dissector's flimsy-feeling side knobs, and find it more in line with something like the Aggressor, which wins out in tread durability and predictability. DURABILITY The durability of these tires is excellent, further increasing their strong value. The tread wears evenly and fairly slowly, with no unusual knob shearing or severe undercutting to report. Continental's Super Soft rubber seems to wear a little more evenly, but the Specialized tires hold up nicely over plenty of park laps and pedal miles alike. I've noticed that as these tires age, they seem to get a bit harder feeling - whether that's something to do with the rubber itself aging or the harder base rubber getting exposed, I don't know, but if you're looking for the most chemical grip then these are unsurprisingly best when fresh. The Grid Gravity casing feels very robust, and has surprised me many times with what it can survive at low pressures, even in sharp and jagged terrain. Rim hits, rocky chutes, and hard corners all seem to shake out nicely, with no flats or failures across the many Gravity tires I've installed on my bikes. I can't be quite as effusive about the Trail casing tires, which do feel a little prone to rolling at lower pressures, and can deform heavily in a good corner unless you're running very high pressure. This isn't unique to the Specialized casing, but is more just to do with the intended use case. I wouldn't recommend lining up for a long day of enduro racing with these, but they feel very well suited to general trail bike use. As with the Gravity tires, I've had no unusual flats. Pros + Butcher is a solid do-it-all tire, front or rear + Eliminator feels fast yet fairly capable + Consistent, reliable, and relatively inexpensive Cons - Stickier rubber does exist, though T9 holds its own - Gravity casing is fairly heavy, maybe too stiff for some Pinkbike's Take The Specialized Butcher is one of those tires I'd happily run on just about any bike, front or rear, rain or shine. It's consistent and predictable, and the price is right. The Eliminator is a bit more of a specialty proposition, with fast rolling tread and just enough traction to edge into harder terrain. — Dario DiGiulio Visit the high-res gallery for more images. Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Tires Specialized Specialized Butcher Specialized Eliminator Author Info: Dario DiGiulio Bellingham, WA, United States Member since Dec 25, 2016 484 articles Dario has been obsessed with bikes from an early age, and has worked in the industry since 2015. With a Bachelor's in Architecture from Cal Poly, San Luis Obispo, he aims to bring that mix of creativity and technicality to his work in the mountain bike world.

title: Review: Every Pair of MTB Shoes I've Worn This Year
author: Dario DiGiulio
article_text: Shoes can be an annoying investment to make, as they're one of the bits of kit that sees the most abuse on a regular basis. I've been guilty of forcing shoes well past their point of retirement, as once you've found a good pair it can be hard to move on. My collection ballooned this year, and left me with a bit of a pile to pick from on a daily basis. I ride just about every day, so these all got a fair bit of use, though the favorites ended up seeing more time on the pedals than others. Read on for a breakdown of each pair, who I think they'd be good for, and finally which ones came out on top. Some notes about me before we get into it, just for a frame of reference. All of these are a size 45 or 45.5, which is my standard street shoe size. I have a fairly wide forefoot, a narrow heel, and a high arch - that said I think arch support is a hoax to keep your feet soft. I've spent a lot of time pedaling in all of these, and each pair has seen a fair bit of hiking around in the woods. My preferred cleat position is about as far rearward as a typical channel allows, with the cleat as far as it can go outboard to keep my feet close to the cranks. Crank Brothers pedals unless otherwise noted. So Many Shoes https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/Or9DMd1n Copied Live 00:00 18:43 18:43 Contents Crankbrothers Mallet Trail First Degree Fuse AC Ride Concepts Hellion Clip Shimano GE9 Ride Concepts Hellion Elite Clip Fox Union Canyon Scampr Giro Chamber III Shimano GF8 GTX Vans SK8-High BMX Crankbrothers Mallet Trail Mallet Trail Details • Boa dial, hook & loop strap • TPU-reinforced toebox • Ripstop upper • Fiber-reinforced shank • Sizing: 35-49 • Price: $240 USD • Weight: 490g / shoe • crankbrothers.com The Mallet DH sold me on Crankbrothers shoes, and while the Mallet Trail is quite different, they uphold a positive legacy. The upper construction on these is lighter than you'd expect, but feels like it offers plenty of protection where it counts - the toe, heel, and side of foot are all well guarded. The gaiter is well executed, and keeps larger debris from working its way in. Crankbrothers touts their Match concept as being an ideal soul-bonding of shoe and pedal - while I do use Crankbrothers pedals, and like how these shoes interface, I wouldn't say it's drastically different than any of the other options here. You'll have to use some shims to space the cleat out, but once that's done it does lock into the pedal nicely. I've found the soles of these get fairly chewed up, but they remain useable even after plenty of time on the pins. High quality materials. Gumshoe gets a bit torn up. The stiffness is fairly neutral on these, suiting heavy-duty usage without sacrificing walkability. My only real complaint is that they feel a bit tall underfoot, as if you're further separated from the pedal you're standing on than with some of the other options here. Not a big issue, but a detail I noticed when switching back and forth. Pros + Durable, high-quality construction + Comfortable balance of stiffness and give Cons - Simple fit adjustments - Tall-feeling sole First Degree Fuse AC Fuse AC Details • Lace up, hook & loop strap • Agressive outsole • Seamless welded upper • Foam-reinforced tongue • Sizing: 36-47 • Price: 175 € • Weight: 492g / shoe • firstdegree-mtb.com I was initially quite excited about these, as the details, aesthetics, and feel all added up to a shoe that seemed pretty ideal for my use case. Sadly, one detail kept them from working for me: the cleat channel. For some reason, the cleat channel on these is very far forward in the shoe, rendering even the rearmost position a bit awkward feeling to me. I could deal with it and get used to the feel, but the rearward cleat position that all of the other shoes on test deliver was sorely missed here. The other annoyance with the channel is how deep it is relative to the sole - you'll have to run quite a few spacers to match these up with Crankbrothers shoes, and even Shimano pedals required some tall standoffs. Besides that detail, the shoes are quite nice. The stiffness is similar to the Crankbrothers, and the uppers are robust thanks to a smooth welded construction. The recessed laces feel safe from snags, and stay in place nicely once you've snugged up your fit. Speaking of fit, the forefoot on these is nice and wide, without adding too much bulk - points for comfort. Bungee for lace storage. Bummer about the cleat position. Thanks to the fairly aggressive outsole on these, their pedal contact is very strong. Perhaps too much if you're someone who likes to break free easily, in which case you'll have to space the cleat out even further. I liked the locked in feel, and found these to be some of the grippiest shoes for hiking and scrambling around, should you find yourself in those situations. Pros + Nice details, great fit + Grippy sole Cons - Cleat channel needs redesign - Many cleat spacers required Ride Concepts Hellion Clip Boa Hellion Details • Boa dial • D3O padding throughout • Cordura upper • Nylon shank • Sizing: 39.5-47, women's available • Price: $220 USD • Weight: 586g / shoe • rideconcepts.com Ride Concepts overhauled their lineup earlier this year, introducing a few models under the Hellion banner. I've been using the more gravity-focused Hellion Elites, and these: the Hellions. As the more trail-focused version of the two, these shave down on some features in pursuit of simplicity and lighter weight, but ultimately they just feel like the lesser of the two options. The sole, cleat positioning, and rubber compound are all great, offering solid connection to the pedal and great support for the foot. The main upper material is very robust, and offers great protection for the foot. Where these fall short for me is in the smaller details, such as with the Boa loops that started fraying almost immediately. It seems like brushing through undergrowth has been enough to cause these to fray quite a bit, and though they're holding strong so far, the design feels a bit flawed to me. Not quite as burly as their Elite counterpart. Same great sole and shank though. Luckily the general fit of these is quite comfortable, with a generous forefoot and tight heel cup. I've found that the single Boa dial isn't adequate for tightening the whole shoe down in one go, requiring you to either pull the lacing through or flex your foot around to distribute tension as you crank things down. Compared to the Elites, which use two Boas, these feel a bit less secure. A note: these and the Elites have the furthest back cleat channel of any of the shoes on test, so far rearward that I actually moved my cleat up a bit in the channel - a first in quite a while.
