Those who like a cleat at the forward tip of the shoe (why?) might not love this feature, through I really do. Pros + Comfortable fit, good pedal connection + Protective and generally durable Cons - Weak point details - Boa tension is hard to distribute Shimano GE9 GE9 Details • Boa dial, hook & loop strap • Torbal flexible midsole • Debris collar at ankle • Fiber-reinforced shank • Sizing: 38-48 • Price: $250 USD • Weight: 507g / shoe • ride.shimano.com I've put this pair through the wringer, partially because they were the only shoes I had on hand for a little while, partially because they are very comfortable. If you have wide feet, these are plenty accommodating, with a big toebox and a fairly loose fit. One the flip side, if you like a locked-in ski boot feel this probably won't be your ideal shoe. The material feels soft and pliable, with a fair bit of extra padding throughout. While that helps with protection and some comfort, it makes these feel a bit warmer and more prone to getting soggy. I also found that I had to alter my cleat position to keep the inner portion of the shoe from rubbing on the crank too much, due to how wide they are. Lotta' rubbin'. Nice hiking tread. Due to that rubbing, I ended up sawing through the buckle that retains the hook-and-loop strap in very little time, necessitating the addition of a little DIY slot in the upper material. That did the trick, and they've continued to work despite. The plastic Boa lacing loops are great, and assure that the laces tension up the shoe every time, despite grit, grime, and wear. Overall, the feel of the upper and sole of these is softer and more malleable than most of the other shoes on test, which makes them very walkable but takes away some of the racy stiffness some folks want. If you do want more stiffness, Shimano released the GE900HS , which is stiffer and a little slimmer than these shoes. Pros + Wide and comfy + Nice lacing, good tread traction Cons - Soft and baggy feel isn't for everyone - Strap durability Ride Concepts Hellion Elite Clip Boa Hellion Elite Details • Two Boa dials • D3O padding throughout • Cordura upper • Nylon shank • Sizing: 39.5-47, women's available • Price: $262 USD • Weight: 596g / shoe • rideconcepts.com As mentioned earlier, these are essentially the bigger, better version of the Ride Concept Hellion - hence the name. The fit felt a bit potatoey at first, but after a short break-in period they started cinching in to my foot shape and feeling like home. The sole is plenty stiff without being ridiculous, and the overall fit feels comfortable enough for all-day use. The heavy weight on the scale luckily translates to impressive durability in real world use, as these have held up remarkably well to my abuses. The laces are pulling smoothly, the upper fabric is intact, and the sole is still functioning well despite getting chewed up by the pedals. In my book, a bit more weight feels like a reasonable tradeoff for a shoe that lasts longer than just one season. Boa bumpers. Holding strong despite a tone of use. That weight also manifests in a very protective shoe, one that has certainly saved my toes on a handful of occasions. The D3O padding seemed a bit gimmicky initially, but after having some talus rocket into the top of my foot I became convinced of its utility. Same goes for the plastic toecap, which deflects nicely off of trailside obstacles. The Hellion Elites are warm and lack much breathability, but they're not unbearable even through the summer months. These have proven to be a pair that I reach for first, thanks to the well-rounded design and functional details. A note: as I mentioned about the Hellions earlier, these have the furthest back cleat channel of any of the shoes on test, so far rearward that I actually moved my cleat up a bit in the channel - a first in quite a while. Pros + Very protective + Good balance of comfort and utility Cons - Warm, not very breathable - Heavy Fox Union Boa Union Details • Two Boa dials • Welded and reinforced toebox • Ripstop upper • D3O insert, two arch support options • Sizing: 37-47 • Price: $275 USD • Weight: 506g / shoe • foxracing.com The Unions are one of the stiffer pairs in this lineup, with a sole that gives very little even under massive loads. They feel precise, with good pedal contact and sticky enough rubber to lock in. The rigid last doesn't hike very well, but does the job when need be. For me, the Unions have one critical flaw: the material of the upper comes way too far up the ankle, pushing into the tendon at the front of the ankle (tibialis anterior). The rubbing was at its worst on climbs, where the repetitive motion of pedaling really ground things down. After trying to break them in naturally, I resorted to a bit of user modification, opening up that corner with a pair of snips. This helped quite a bit, but didn't completely alleviate the issue. Comfort modifications. Rigid. Fit-wise, the Unions are on the narrower side of things, but they don't feel too uncomfortable for me. The rubberized toe cap keeps things from opening up with use, but my feet had enough room for long rides. The dual Boa dials keep things tight and well-adjusted, and while they lack some of the dial protection that other shoes feature, I haven't had any damage occur. Pros + Stiff and precise + Nice fit Cons - Annoying cuff detail - Not great for hike-a-bike Canyon Scampr Scampr Details • Boa dial • Knit upper • Tri-panel tension fit • Solestar insole, Vibram sole • Sizing: 36-48 • Price: $160 USD • Weight: 451g / shoe • canyon.com Canyon first introduced these shoes to the gravel / adventure cycling market, but from the jump they struck me as a potentially ideal trail bike shoe. The defining feature here is the knit upper, which feels like a durable sock with some structure to it. The single Boa dial cinches the whole thing together, and you're off to the races - or the graveling or adventuring, whatever. Thanks to that upper construction, the Scampr is breathable and light, with a somewhat disconcertingly airy feel. Great on hot days, but not so great when it comes to impact protection. That's entirely not the point here, so it feels like an unfair complaint, but don't expect these to protect your upper foot from much. There is a solid toe cap, and the sole offers plenty of support for real descending. These are my favorite shoe for short-travel bikes where I know I won't be pushing the limits, and for longer days in hot temps. Extreme sports sock. Stiff enough where it matters. The stretchy fit of these should accommodate a wide variety of foot shapes, and felt very comfortable to me. The fit system Canyon implemented here is excellent, hugging your foot tight without any faff. Even if the Boa were to break, the shoe's cuff is sturdy enough to keep them on your foot, in a pinch. My one complaint comfort-wise comes down to the Solestar insole, which has an odd bump right in the middle of your forefoot, and never felt quite right to me - I swapped it out for a random insole that fit, and I've been happy. Pros + Very comfortable and breathable + Excellent fit system Cons - Very little protection in upper - Solestar insole Giro Chamber III Chamber Details • Lace up, Velcro strap • Padded microfiber upper • Low-profile tongue • DH-focused shank • Sizing: 36-48 • Price: $200 USD • Weight: 538g / shoe • giro.com The Chamber III is on par with the Fox Unions as the stiffest two pair of shoes on test here, but the Giros take things a step further with a tighter and more aggressive fit and feel. The last of these is quite narrow, and might not work with those with wider feet as a result. If that doesn't disqualify them for you, then they provide a very locked-in feel, with plenty of fit adjustment thanks to the traditional lacing system and a Velcro strap to cinch things down. Giro reduced the heft and thickness of these quite a bit compared to the last iteration of the Chamber, and we're left with a shoe that feels nicely pared down, while still offering enough protection and durability. The protection here feels more like padding than a shell, especially when compared to shoes like the Hellion Elite - both work, but the shell method seems to withstand a bit more abuse. As you'd expect with a very stiff shoe, the hiking performance isn't amazing, but Giro did give these sticky enough rubber to keep you upright on all sorts of dumb scrambles. Padded faux leather upper. Real stiff, quite narrow. I found the narrow fit required some lace adjustment here and there over long days, opening up slightly with enough time. The lacing feels a bit fiddly compared to the Boa dials featured on most of the other shoes, but you can get a very secure and stay up on your bunny ear technique. As a package, these are a pretty ideal choice for those looking to put a ton of power into the pedals, and get little to no feedback from those efforts or impacts. Pros + Stiff, locked-in feel + Precise fit system Cons - Narrow fit - Fussy to adjust Shimano GF8 GTX GF8 Details • Boa dial • Gore-Tex liner • Tall elastic cuff • Low-temperature sticky rubber • Sizing: 38-48 • Price: $220 USD • Weight: 513g / shoe • ride.shimano.com Shimano's GF8 is the only dedicated mountain bike flat shoe I'm including here, because it's my favorite that I've used this year. They are far from perfect, but the important things are close to my ideal. They used a softer, low-temperature sticky rubber for the sole, which hooks to pedals remarkably well, no matter how foul the weather. The extra traction is welcome, and luckily hasn't some at the expense of a sole that lasts - these still look good despite plenty of use. The stiffness also feels well-suited to my preference, with a bit of give to allow for good pedal feel, without sacrificing impact protection. Yes, they're also waterproof, but that feels far less critical for me, despite living in a notoriously wet place. The upside is a dry foot through splashes and light rain, the downside is a permanently wet foot as soon as any water gets in past the cuff. Waterproof means it keeps just as much in as it lets out. I'm willing to live with that tradeoff, as the boot fit is nice and secure, albeit annoying to get on and off. Weak materials and a very tight cuff. Excellent rubber. The biggest complaint I've had about the GF8 GTX comes down to durability - the liner material has proven to be far too light-duty for the job. Because the cuff is so tight, you have to really yank on these to get them on (or get a shoehorn), and the very thin heel area is prone to tearing as a result. I've continued to use these well past that patch giving up the ghost, but I'd like to see better design on a pair of shoes that otherwise ticks all the boxes. Pros + Great pedal feel + Very sticky rubber, even in cold Cons - Poor durability - Frustrating to put on Vans SK8-Hi BMX SK8-Hi Details • Lace up • Duracap high-wear reinforcement • PopCush footbed • Vulcanized rubber • Sizing: 34.5-47 • Price: $90 USD • Weight: 519g / shoe • vans.com These are just a good old pair of Vans. There's nothing special and mountain bike specific about them, they just happen to be one of my favorite shoes for the job. If protection is important to you, look elsewhere, but if grip and pedal feel are your main goals, then the classic high top is a pretty compelling option. I picked these up to see if the BMX moniker actually improved anything in terms of durability and usefulness for the bike, and so far they've impressed. It's no mystery that Vans durability has been pretty horrible of late, but the rubber and uppers has held up on these so far. Though they lack any sort of upper foot protection, the UltraCush insoles do take the sting off any bottom-of-foot strikes. Crankarm protection. Classic. I don't love the pointed-toe fit of Vans, but after a long break in they do start feeling like home. A simple lace-up fit keeps you locked in if you want it, though these are anything but stiff. The beauty of Vans is how much pedal feel you get, and how well the sole holds onto pedals. They're really not optimized for mountain biking, but that hasn't stopped them from being a great option for the right use cases. Pros + All the pedal feel + Great grip Cons - Lacking protection - Pointed fit Final Choices For those looking for a great all-rounder shoe, I'd recommend the following: 1. Ride Concepts Hellion Elite Boa Clip 2. Crank Brothers Mallet Trail 3. Giro Chamber III For shorter travel bikes and lighter use, the Canyon Scampr is my top choice. For pure downhill use, the stiff and precise feel of the Giro Chamber III would be my pick, with a nod to the Fox Union despite some issues with the design of the upper. Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Shoes Author Info: Dario DiGiulio Bellingham, WA, United States Member since Dec 25, 2016 484 articles Dario has been obsessed with bikes from an early age, and has worked in the industry since 2015. With a Bachelor's in Architecture from Cal Poly, San Luis Obispo, he aims to bring that mix of creativity and technicality to his work in the mountain bike world.

title: Review: Santa Cruz Vala - The VPP-Free eMTB
author: Dario DiGiulio
article_text: The End Of VPP? Santa Cruz Vala Review https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/KdSWHIsK Copied Live 00:20 00:10 00:31 Skip The big news of the Vala's release was Santa Cruz's departure from their synonymous VPP suspension layout, but in many ways that buries the lede. The Vala is another solid bike from a the brand, regardless of what suspension design they chose to implement to make it work. The geometry, weight, and handling characteristics are all targeting a "normal bike" experience, and in some ways they've gotten quite close. Vala Details • 150mm frame travel, 160mm fork • Mixed wheels 27.5"/29" • C or CC carbon frame • 63.9° head angle • 600Wh battery • 85 Nm torque, 600 W max power • High/low and progression flip chips • Price: $7,299 – $12,799 USD • Weight: 48.08lbs / 21.8kg (XL bike) • santacruzbicycles.com Contents Introduction Construction & Features Geometry & Sizing Suspension Design Specifications Setup Climbing Descending Technical Report What's the Best Value? How Does it Compare? Pros & Cons Pinkbike's Take Long story short, the Vala can handle just about anything you throw at it, but errs on the fun side of things, with proportions and weight meant more to mimic regular bikes than to maximize the monster truck e-bike characteristics. — Dario DiGiulio Frame Details This is also Santa Cruz's first eMTB using the Bosch motor/battery system, and it implements the brand new CX Performance refresh to provide the power. With quieter operation, a lighter motor and batteries, and improved software elements, the new CX is certainly on the short list for best full-power systems on the market right now. While the new CX system has been designed around both a 600Wh and 800Wh battery option, Santa Cruz has chosen to make the Vala a 600Wh-only bike, with no option to run the larger package. It is compatible with the Powermore accessory battery, which provides an additional 250Wh of juice for longer missions. Bosch CX motor and a low-slung charging plug. Wireless control for the motor and shifting. C and CC frame options denote the carbon quality and weight, the latter being a bit lighter. High/low geometry. High/low progression. The Vala is a dedicated mixed-wheel bike, regardless of size. That doesn't mean they've done away with adjustability though, as there are two flip chips integrated into the frame. At the back of the rocker link is the High/Low mode adjustment, which slightly alters the frame geometry to suit rider preference and terrain. The lower shock mount flip chip allows you to switch between high and low progression, and all changes can be made easily trailside with a 5mm wrench. As with non-electric Santa Cruz bikes, the Vala comes in either C or CC frame options, with the latter incurring some weight savings and higher-end carbon. The CC bike also eschews any internal derailleur routing, like the new Bronson and Hightower, making it an electric-only bike as it stands. Three of the builds come with the C frame, and two with the CC. Geometry The Vala's geometry closely resembles the recently released Bronson, with a descent-focused demeanor that is meant to handle whatever you throw its way. The high/low geometry adjustment changes bottom bracket height by 4mm, and head/seat tube angles by 0.3°. I'll be referring to the geometry in the Low position, as that's how I've primarily ridden the bike. The 63.9° head angle is plenty slack for even the steepest trails around, and a fairly steep 77.2° seat tube angle keeps you between the wheels on all but the steepest climbs. The actual seat angle at pedaling height is a bit slacker than this advertised figure, but Santa Cruz is now publishing those figures so people get a more accurate sense of things. Reach figures range from 432mm to 522mm, with stack heights ranging from 626mm to 671mm. There are five sizes across the range, with nicely scaled jumps in sizing between each. The rear center lengths also vary by size, going from 440mm on the size Small to 451mm on the XXL. I'm a fan of the proportions Santa Cruz gives their bikes, with each size scaling in a way that rides well and feels balanced. That holds true with the Vala, as my XL fits like a glove and doesn't require any odd setup to get stack and reach suited to the bike's intended purposes. Leverage rate options. Anti-rise. Anti-squat. Suspension Design This may be Santa Cruz's first 4-bar bike, but they're employing many of the same tactics used on their VPP bikes to achieve the ride feel they wanted for the Vala. For the past few years, Santa Cruz has been trending towards lower and lower anti-squat figures for their more descent-oriented bikes, and the Vala is much the same. Though they say it was primarily a packaging issue, the different suspension plays a beneficial role here as well, as they can achieve more linear curves for things like anti-squat and anti-rise when compared to their VPP counterparts. As previously mentioned, this bike has a progression flip chip that allows you to change the leverage rate of the rear suspension to suit your preferences, opting between 26% and 29%. The lower starting ratio (Lo mode) will offer more support off the top, and a more consistent feel through travel, where the higher starting ratio (Hi mode) should feel a little more active at the top of the stroke, with more of a ramp up as you approach bottom-out. The difference between the two isn't gigantic, but it's worth playing with the two options to see which you prefer. Specifications Release Date 2024 Price $11499 Travel 150mm Rear Shock FOX Float X Factory, 205x60 Fork FOX 38 Factory, Grip X2, 160mm Headset Cane Creek 50 IS Integrated Cassette SRAM XO T-Type Crankarms SRAM XO T-Type Chainguide OneUp Chain Guide Bottom Bracket N/A Pedals N/A Rear Derailleur SRAM XO T-Type Chain SRAM XO T-Type Front Derailleur N/A Shifter Pods SRAM XO T-Type Handlebar Santa Cruz 35 Carbon Bar, 800mm Stem OneUp Stem, 42mm -or- Burgtec Enduro Stem, 42mm Grips Santa Cruz Bicycles House Grips Brakes SRAM Maven Silver Stealth Wheelset Reserve 30|HD Carbon Hubs DT Swiss 350 Spokes DT Swiss Rim Reserve 30|HD Carbon Tires Front: Schwalbe Magic Mary, 29x2.5, Trail Radial Ultra Soft | Rear: Schwalbe Magic Mary, 27.5x2.5, Gravity Radial Soft Seat WTB Silverado Medium Fusion, CroMo SL Seatpost OneUp Dropper Post, 31.6, 120-240mm Compare to other All Mountain/Enduro/XC Test Bike Setup The Vala was an easy bike to get up to speed on, with a bevy of familiar components and a geometry package that fits me well. At a certain point, I swapped out the Radial Trail casing front tire, as it felt a bit squirmy for the bike, especially compared to the Gravity rear. I also tried a Race Face Era handlebar in place of the Santa Cruz branded one, as I don't love the sweep of the SC ones. Santa Cruz's recommended suspension setup often works quite well for me, and that rang true here as well. I found I liked the Lo progression mode with around 202psi, and found the stock 0.4 cu. in. spacer worked well for most riding, shy of the heaviest g-outs. For more freeridey feature-heavy trails, I typically ran the bike in Hi progression, with a few more pounds of pressure in the shock. Lo progression and a 0.6 volume spacer was a happy medium, if I had to err on one setup for everywhere. Dario DiGiulio Location: Bellingham, WA, USA Height: 6'3" / 191cm Inseam: 34" / 86cm Weight: 185 lbs / 81.6 kg Industry affiliations / sponsors: None Instagram: @danger_dario The 160mm Fox 38 came with 3 volume spacers installed, and while I was able to use full travel here and there, I ultimately ended up running 2 spacers for the bulk of the test period, as I preferred the more linear feel at 106psi. Download the Trailforks App Testing Info The Vala goes well just about everywhere, with handling that isn't too boring on flatter trails, and capability that can push into some truly proper terrain. Generally, the bike feels ideal for doing just what you'd do on your average 150/160mm bike, but with a boost. Climbing A shocking revelation: the ebike climbs well. But there's more to it than just a motor - the Vala's handling characteristics bias more towards "normal" climbs than the goofy hard enduro climbs that can be a hoot on a full powered bike. For most use cases, this is a positive, as the Vala isn't a handful on climbs of all sorts, thanks to a wheelbase that isn't too extreme, fairly low build weight, and a comfortable seated position. The geo of the bike keeps you fairly centered, but it's not quite as central a spot as some more of the more progressive eMTBs out there. If your typical e-bike ride includes purpose-built climb trails and FSR climbs, then you'll be well served by the Vala. If you seek out more adventurous and challenging climbs, moto ruts, and otherwise inadvisable maneuvers, then some of the longer rear-center eMTBs out there do a better job of keeping the front wheel down when you're scraping up something heinous. A steeper seat angle could help here, but I think the seat angle of the bike fits the use case well, and feels nice and comfortable on flatter terrain - making a longer rear the better solution in my mind. Descending First and foremost, the Vala deserves a lot of praise for the suspension performance. The bike can be pushed well beyond what you'd expect of the travel numbers, and feels composed at the limits of that travel. I rode plenty of trails that regularly scare me, and didn't find the bike holding me back. On the flip side, mellower trails that can be a bit ponderous on a big eMTB were still fun on the Vala, as the geometry biases a bit towards all-around handling versus all-out speed. That geo feels mostly well considered, but I can't help but wonder how much more you could get out of the bike with a couple tweaks. As mentioned in the Climbing section, I think the rear center could be a good bit longer on this bike before you started feeling any detrimental effects. Sure, the shorter chainstay helps keep the bike lively when you're riding mellower trails, but I haven't been unhappy with the bikes I've ridden that push that figure well past the Vala's. For those looking to carry as much speed as possible on relatively low-angle trails (read: not vertical), there are better options out there. But for a mixed bag, where you're riding cruiser jump lines one day and feature-heavy tech the next, I think the Vala strikes a nice balance. While I do think that balance could be maintained while improving the overall stability, I understand why Santa Cruz settled on something a bit more neutral. Long story short, the Vala can handle just about anything you throw at it, but errs on the fun side of things, with proportions and weight meant more to mimic regular bikes than to maximize the monster truck e-bike characteristics. Vala vs. Bullit Part of the reason I ride e-bikes at all is to try to improve skills through the extra riding you can get, so in a way the Vala's sharper handling was a boon to this goal. I realize that's not everyone's intent with getting an eMTB, but hopefully you have a fairly accurate sense of why you're purchasing a very expensive motorized bike before you do so. If all-out capability is the goal, and you're looking to drag brake down the steepest trails around, Santa Cruz's new Bullit is probably the better option. I think the Bullit is a bit more location dependent, where the Vala will probably suit a wider swath of riders and terrain. Despite the longer travel, the Bullit doesn't add much weight, but the geometry is a bit more sprawled out, and the suspension feel biases more towards terrain that can really push a bike. Santa Cruz Vala Transition Regulator How does it compare? A few bikes come to mind when stacking the Vala up against the competition, but two will likely surface as the most relevant comparisons: the Specialized Levo 4 and the Transition Regulator. All three feature 150mm of rear suspension delivered by 4-bar suspension, all three rely on a full-fat eMTB motor to get you up the hill, and all three have fairly similar geometry on paper. The Vala and Regulator are nearly identical on the geometry front, with slightly longer reach on the Transition. Ultimately, build kit, suspension performance, and frame details are going to be the differentiating factors, so best to spin through Mike's review to get a sense of which will be better for you. For me, the user-adjustable geometry and kinematics are a point to the Vala's credit, as is the monetary value you get with a given build kit. If choosing between the Levo 4 and Vala, I'd opt for the latter. The motor and battery spec differences amounted to a smaller effect on trail than expected, and I far preferred the ride feel the Vala provided. The bike feels more balanced between the wheels, especially in hard cornering situations, where the Levo had a tendency to push the front if you weren't aggressively weighting the front end. This could be chalked up to a few different factors, be it the front / rear center geometry balance, the dynamic ride characteristics of the Genie shock, or simply the Levo's heavier weight. In the end, the Vala just felt more composed in a variety of trail situations, and kept things plenty fun. Which Model is the Best Value? Seven build options is a bit of a bloated lineup, especially when the spec differences are only vaguely dissimilar as you move through the models. Every single bike has a Float X shock (all with the same great tune), nearly all have a Fox 38 out front, and every model comes with some form of SRAM drivetrain and brakes. Optimizing for the best of those latter categories, I'd opt for the Vala 90, which comes with a mechanical Transmission drivetrain, Maven Silver brakes, and Reserve Alloy wheels. The suspension is as good as need be, and the finishing touches are all ideal for the use case. While $8,599 is very far from a steal, there aren't any wasted parts, and the bike should hold up to the abuse its sure to receive. Technical Report Schwalbe Radial Tires: It should be no mystery that we're fans of Schwalbe's new radial tire offerings - they offer a unique amount of grip, comfort, and durability without too much of a downside. I haven't gotten along with the Magic Mary very well in the past, but in a radial casing the classic tire starts to make more sense to me. I found the Trail casing to be a bit too light for the eMTB use-case, but the Gravity out back stayed on for the duration of the test. The Trail radials feel sweet on smaller bikes, but I found myself climbing so high in pressure to match the feel of the rear that it brought diminishing returns. A Gravity casing Shredda Gravity lived on the front for most of the test, and all was well. Bosch CX Race Motor Hardware: The motor and battery on the Vala gave me zero issues through the long duration of this test, which is astounding considering the failure rate I've had on eMTBs to date. The only hitch I encountered was a strange one, and hopefully secluded to the pre-production batch my bike came from. On one particularly foul mud ride, I noticed some play develop in the pedals, and upon cleaning the bike, found that the bushings that the motor mount bolts ride on had managed to escape their housing and disappear. Apparently the production versions have a better lip design to retain them, and after Santa Cruz sent a fresh set my way I had zero issues with the hardware working loose. OneUp 240mm Dropper Post: Not only does the 240mm post put the saddle completely out of your way on any sort of gradient, but this post just keeps working without issue. No play, noise, or slow return occurred through the long test period, despite some truly foul winter riding. Pros + Easy handling, adaptable performance + Impressively reliable frame and electronics + Excellent build kit Cons - Battery capacity will disappoint some (but a range extender is available) - Requires attention on awkward steep climbs - Very expensive Pinkbike's Take The Santa Cruz Vala is fun, adaptable, and does a surprisingly good job of simulating the non-motorized riding experience. It may not be the most capable eMTB I've ever ridden, but it doesn't shy away from speed or features, and retains a lively handling characteristic that pays dividends on mellower trails. — Dario DiGiulio Visit the high-res gallery for more images from this review.
