With a Bachelor's in Architecture from Cal Poly, San Luis Obispo, he aims to bring that mix of creativity and technicality to his work in the mountain bike world.

title: Review: Fox Union All Weather Flat Shoes -  For the Wettest Days
author: Matt Beer
article_text: Fox Union All Weather Shoes https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/8RShqB0K Copied Loading ad Live 00:25 00:00 06:56 These shoes aren’t messing about. Look at the size of that gaiter - that’s the first thing a waterproof shoe should have. Without a healthy overlap under your pant cuffs, your toes soon start to swim in foot juice, no matter how impervious the shoe itself is. The Fox Union All Weather Flats are essentially a gumboot specifically designed for riding bikes. They share a similar construction and appearance to the other Union Boa shoes in Fox’s lineup, at least below the ankle. One small difference is that the All Weathers only features one dial to lock your foot in place. Union All Weather Flat Shoes • Boa Fit System closure • extra tall gaiter • Ultratac outsole • D3O insole • Sizes: 37-47 (41-46 in half sizes) • Colors: black, adobe • Weight: 550 grams (per shoe, size 41.5) • MSRP: $329.95 USD • foxracing.com When it comes to grip on the pedal pins, the All Weathers call on the Ultratac rubber, like the standard Union Boa Flat shoes. If flat pedals aren’t your jam, there’s a clip version that looks identical, aside from the cleat bed on the sole. Puddles are irrelevant to these shoes. Ultratac rubber is decently grippy. The concave sole detracts from the otherwise tactile connection to the pedals. Overlap is key. Thankfully, Fox figured that out and went sky high. Price and Weight For how much wet weather gear costs, we sure do treat it poorly. You wouldn’t dare jump in a bog with your white Jordans, but that’s exactly what the Union All Weather shoes are ready for. That being said, $325 USD is a lot of dough. Waterproof socks are a decent alternative, but the name isn’t as accurate as one might expect - they will get wet and clammy. You’ll also lose the close connection to the pedals with those thick, rubbery socks. These hydrophobic moon boots aren't tremendously light at 550g per shoe. However, much of that is owed to the mega-high and very functional gaiter. I don’t necessarily see that number as a hindrance while riding, but it may bother the weight weenies. Pull tabs front and back - they'll come in handy. So far, the stitching hasn't missed a beat. Bunching can occur in forward foot movements. Fit Pulling these puppies on takes practice and patience; getting them off was nearly impossible at first. I nearly separated the back of the sole trying to step on the opposite foot. Sitting down and wiggling out of them in stages is the best method I’ve found. They will break in, ever so slightly. Still, it’s a comically strenuous job to remove them. Once they’re on, the gaiter has a comfortable level of snuggness once your foot is planted. I found the fit true to size, just like all of the other Union Boa shoes I’ve tried - typically, I go for a size 8.5 US in riding shoes. Considering these will be worn in the winter with thicker socks, and the width of Fox shoes in general isn’t the most roomy, I could see bumping up a half size being necessary for riders with wide feet. Fox also provides a second set of taller arch supports. I found the lower option worked best for me. With the pant cuffs rolled down or zipped up, it's hard to differentiate the Union All Weather shoes from summer kicks. Performance Like I said, wet weather gear is a hefty investment. Should the ride connection be given up for gaining waterproof qualities? First, it’s unfair to assume waterproof shoes ride like standard summer issue shoes, especially those with insulating properties. Two major elements are being added to resist the weather, so the extra material will take away from the dexterity. Those features are well taken care of. However, some level of tactile contact with the pedals is crucial for control and less binary for those who choose to clip in. So, what about that all-important “feel”? Looking lengthwise down the sole underside, you can see that it has a concave shape. I’ve come across this conundrum before with a pair of Scott SHR-ALP BOA and wondered why they were so hard to find the perfect spot on the pedals with. Once you do find the LZ, the grip is decent. The tread isn’t as tacky or deep as that found in Five Ten’s special Stealth Rubber formula, but it’s not far off. I’m a fan of the Boa Fit System on pretty much any piece of riding kit. It may not be the Boa laces' fault entirely, but the gaiter material behind the highest piece can scrunch up and cause a pinch point in extreme downward heel flexion. The upper materials of some waterproof shoes are fragile. These are tough and showing little signs of wearing out. So far, so good - no holes from pedal pins. Durability I’ve given these a thrashing already through a drastic shift into fall here in Squamish, but I’m hoping they break in further and the sole flattens out. In that area, the rubber still looks brand new, despite my tendencies to chew through the flat pedal shoe soles at a specific point from the pedal pins. Judging by the two years I’ve spent bashing on the Union BOA Clip shoes, I’m confident that Fox has nailed their TPU rubber joining methods and carried that forth on the All Weather model. Two vulnerable areas I thought might wear quickly are behind the uppermost BOA lace crossing and the cuff’s pull tab stitching. The gaiter is made from an incredibly tough material, but neither area has shown any weakness. How Do They Compare? Fizik Nanuq GTX: $299 USD. The sole is too stiff and the cuff verges on being too low, but with the right pair of pants, you're feet will be dry and warm through the deepest slush. Shimano GF800 GTX: $220 USD. If dexterity and grip are your main focus, these Shimano shoes are among the best. It's a shame they aren't warmer or legitimately waterproof. Five Ten Trailcross GTX: $200 USD. This model from Five Ten is an interesting concept; they're light, flexible, and almost waterproof. Grip and control are high, as you'd imagine from anything bearing the Five Ten logo. Endura MT500 Burner: $189.99 USD. Another strong contender in the warm and waterproof realm, however, the rubber tackiness sits lowest on the scoreboard against these other options listed. Pros + Fully waterproof materials. + Water is less likely to splash under the pant cuff thanks to the extra-tall gaiter. + Sleek design - no velcro to wear out or laces to tangle. Cons - Extremely difficult to remove. - Not as tactile on pedal surfaces as other less water-resistant options out there. - Forefoot gaiter fold can bunch up when leaning far forward. - Performance comes at a high cost. Pinkbike's Take This is the wet-weather riding shoe, or boot rather, that I've been waiting for. In the Union All Weathers, there's no longer a need to avoid puddles - just pick the best riding line and you'll stay dry. They're not perfect, but I'm willing to give up a fraction of grip and feel on the pedals for the rest of their hydrophobic properties. — Matt Beer Visit the high-res gallery for more images from this review. Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Shoes Fox Clothing Fox Union Author Info: Matt Beer Squamish, BC, Canada Member since Mar 16, 2001 607 articles Matt has been a Pinkbike reader since the site's early days. He discovered mountain biking in Newfoundland, where a small community of dedicated riders and trail builders set him on a path to earn two Canadian National Downhill Championship titles. Now based in Squamish, BC, Matt works as a tech editor for Pinkbike, specializing in gravity-focused mountain bike reviews, tech, and racing coverage.

title: Review: Push Nine One V2 Inverted Fork - Plush & Pricey
author: Mike Kazimer
article_text: Push Nine One V2 https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/HBaNuxKO Copied Loading ad Live 00:25 00:00 13:54 Just like baggy jeans and Nirvana t-shirts, upside down forks are experiencing something of a resurgence. Fox, Intend, and Push have all released enduro-oriented options, and Cane Creek has teased a prototype USD fork of their own. Is it a sign of things to come, or will the USD concept remain something of a fringe option in a sea of right-side-up forks? I’m leaning towards the latter, but time will tell. Push first revealed the Nine One at Sea Otter 2023 , but it took some time before it finally hit the market. Once it did, the Colorado-based company didn’t waste any time before rolling out version 2.0, which adds on some clever features not found on the initial version. Push Nine One V2 Details • Coil sprung • Travel: 140-170 mm (internally adjustable) • 15 x 110mm thru-axle • Adjustments: high- & low-speed compression, rebound, Sub Chamber, hydraulic bottom out • 29" or 27.5" wheel • Torque Cap compatible • Weight: 2,950 grams w/ axle • MSRP: $2,250 USD • pushindustries.com Details Let’s start with the basics. The Nine One is coil sprung, with external rebound, high and low-speed compression adjustments. There’s also adjustable hydraulic bottom out, and a feature Push calls the Sub Chamber, which can be toggled on or off to add end-stroke progression. HBO The hydraulic bottom kicks in for the last 70mm of travel. It's almost like a shock within a shock - it uses a monotube layout with its own main piston, plus an adjustable internal floating piston (IFP) that can be inflated up to 40 psi. As the chart below illustrates, thanks to its speed sensitive design it provides much more bottom-out resistance at higher velocities than the ABS (air bump stop) feature found on version 1.0 of this fork. Sub Chamber The Sub Chamber is another very clever feature - it uses the ambient air trapped in the fork to create additional mid-stroke support. Push uses the following analogy to describe how it functions: " Take a plastic water bottle and empty the water from the bottle. Now squeeze the bottle. With the cap removed the bottle is very easy to crush in your hand. Now, expand the bottle, tighten down the cap, and squeeze the bottle again. With the cap installed ambient air has been trapped inside the bottle causing resistance or creating a basic air spring. " That's what the Sub Chamber does - when it's closed off the air is trapped in the fork, adding that extra support. The Nine One's upper tubes measure 44mm in diameter, and the stanchions are 36mm. The Nine One uses a 15mm x 110mm thru-axle, and is compatible with Torque Cap hubs (that fact will be more pertinent later on in this review). The axle is secured by four pinch bolts. The amount of travel can be set between 140 - 170mm in 10mm increments, a procedure that can be performed at home without the need for any additional parts. It does require basic mechanical skills, and it’s not the quickest task, but still, it’s nice not needing to shell out any extra dollars for another spring assembly. The two-position Sub Chamber dial. Low-speed compression and high-speed compression are adjusted on the right side, with nice and distinct clicks with each turn. Price & Weight On my scale the Nine One V2 weighed in at 2950 grams with the axle installed - that’s 255 grams heavier than a Fox Podium, and it’s also heavier than a RockShox Boxxer or a Fox 40, which are dual-crown DH forks. As for the price, that’s on the higher side of things too - it’ll set you back $2,250 USD, and you’ll likely want to shell out another $35 for the fender. Push Nine One V2: 2950 grams | $2,250 USD. 140 - 170mm travel Fox Podium : 2695g | $2,000 USD. 150-170mm travel. Fox 38 : 2381g | $1,249 USD. 150-180mm travel. Intend Flash : 2350g | $2,263 USD. 160-190mm travel. RockShox Zeb : 2324g | $1,159 USD. 150-190mm travel. Setup I’ve had the Nine One V2 in my test rotation for the last four months, and during that time it’s been installed on a Trek Fuel LX, a Specialized Levo 4, and a Yeti MTe. To me, those represent the trio of bike styles that I envision potential customers installing this fork on - a burly enduro bike, a full power eMTB, and a mid-power eMTB. Testing began in the Whistler Bike Park during Crankworx, where the fork was subjected to plenty of deep braking bumps and harsh compressions, and continued on the trails surrounding Bellingham, Washington. The fork arrives with a handy little setup guide, with a section that has the base settings written down, and the signatures of the employees that assembled and inspected the fork. There are a total of seven spring weights available, from 30 to 60 lb/in in 5 pound increments. For my 160 lb weight I went with the 45 lb spring, and started with the low and high speed compression dials at 15 clicks out from closed, and the rebound at 12 clicks out. The IFP for the hydraulic bottom out feature was inflated to 30 psi (40 psi is the max). On the Trail Damping The extra-sensitive initial stroke is the trait that first sticks out, no matter whether you’re climbing or descending. Now, the climbing performance of a fork isn’t usually a big topic of conversation, but the ease that the Nine One goes into its stroke is worth a few extra lines of electronic ink. Some forks are plush enough to make you want to actively run into things just to see what’ll happen, and the Nine One falls into that category. Small undulations in the trail are erased, and larger obstacles, whether that’s an anaconda-sized root or big jumble of chunky rocks, are easily dispatched with a ker-squish , ker-squish (that’s the sound of the damper doing its thing - the Nine One isn’t the quietest fork). That smoothness (and the audible damper noise) carries over onto the descents - straightline plowing is when the fork feels the best. I experimented with both Sub Chamber settings, and found that for the most part I preferred to have it on, due to the extra support it provides deeper in the fork’s travel. For maximum bump-suckability, turning it off gives the fork the fully linear, classic coil spring feeling, right up until the last portion of the travel when the hydraulic bottom out is activated to prevent any clunks or clangs. I ended up pretty much in the middle of the compression settings, and I still had 6 more clicks to go before being fully open on rebound, which means there should be a good range of options for riders to play with in order to achieve their ideal setup. Stiffness Rough, twisty trails, the type that Whistler’s Creekside zone is full of (Miss Fire, Ride Don’t Slide, and BC’s Trail for example) start to bring the Nine One’s main weakness to light - the lack of stiffness in those slower speed, heavy front end movement scenarios. At times it felt like the wheel wanted to wander, as if it was grabbing at the edges of the trail when all I wanted to do was keep going forward. After my first few rides where the amount of front end flex felt unexpectedly high, even for a USD fork, I contacted Darren Murphy, the owner of Push, to see if he had any tips. His suggestion? A different hub. It turns out that some hubs are better than others at resisting the independent leg movement that comes with an upside-down fork design; in this instance he suggested Industry Nine’s Hydra hub, or at the very least using Torque Caps, the oversized end caps that are available for some hubs. I happened to have a Forge & Bond (RIP) wheel with an I9 hub and Torque Caps, so I made the swap and started putting in the miles again. That hub change made a more noticeable difference than I expected - the amount of deflection was reduced, and the stiffness was closer to an acceptable level, although there were still scenarios where the handling was less precise than I wanted. The fork felt very good when the trails were greasy, instances when the best tactic is to avoid overly-aggressive corrections, but when things were drier the extra front-end movement was more noticeable - even with a different hub the Nine One didn't have the level of precision I was looking for. Weight The high weight of the Nine One is noticeable, especially on a bike without a motor. On the Trek, it made the front wheel feel like it wanted to stay glued to the ground, and the bike as a whole felt less eager to get airborne. Granted, it was already pushing 40 pounds before installing the Push, but the extra heft of the fork definitely didn’t help. The plushness, which is great for traction, adds to that glued-down sensation too. On the e-bike, especially the Levo, the weight was a little less noticeable, since that bike is already pretty heavy as it is, but it's not doing it any favors in the maneuverability department. Push Nine One V2 Fox Podium How Does it Compare? The most obvious contender to pit against the Nine One V2 is the Fox Podium . Both forks are inverted, they’re both expensive ($2,250 for the Push and $2,000 for the Podium), and neither one is particularly light (2950 grams for the Push, and 2695 for the Podium). The Podium is air sprung, though, which does save some weight, and it uses a 20mm steel thru-axle, which helps in the stiffness department. Out on the trail, the Nine One wins in the initial sensitivity department. The Podium is no slouch, and it’s extremely smooth, but in this air vs coil battle the Nine One snags the plushness point. Push also takes the point when it comes to bottom out resistance. Yes, the Podium can be tuned with volume spacers to increase the amount of ramp up, but it’s much easier to achieve a smooth end-stroke ramp up on the Nine One thanks to the Sub Chamber and the hydraulic bottom out features. The Podium pulls ahead when it comes to stiffness and precision. It’s not as torsionally stiff as a 38 (and it’s not claimed to be), but I found the Podium’s handling to be more predictable than the Nine One, especially in those aforementioned steep, rough, slower speed twisty trails. With the Nine One, there were times when it was a little hard to discern what the front wheel was doing. It may seem odd to want more feedback from a fork, but just like it’d be hard to climb a steep sport route while wearing thick wool socks, it can be harder to navigate a trail when the sensations reaching your hands are extra-muted. Dario DiGiulio also spent time on both forks, and his findings mirrored mine - he found the chassis flex to be the biggest differentiator between the two. He’s 185 pounds vs my 160 pound weight, and for him the flex in the Nine One was even more excessive in tight corners and hard compressions. His takeaway? "Though the top-end feel of the fork is very impressive, I can’t think of any real-world applications where I’d rather ride this fork than something like a 38, Zeb, or the Podium." Personally, I’m not totally sold on either upside-down option. I’d pick the Podium over the Nine One, since it’s stiffer and quieter, but I’d pick the 38 over either one - I prefer the precision and lighter weight of that fork, even if it’s not quite as plush off the top. It’s also almost half the cost, and to me the performance difference isn’t worth an extra $1,000, no matter how cool an upside down fork looks. Pros + Very smooth and plush thanks to coil spring and USD design + Sub Chamber feature is very effective + Optional fender works and actually looks decent Cons - Noticeable flex in certain scenarios - Heavy - Expensive Pinkbike's Take I’ll always root for the underdog, especially when a small company is trying to go up against the bike industry behemoths. This time, though, David’s flying rocks aren't enough to take down Goliath. The Nine One is as plush as it gets, and it’ll absolutely plow through the rough stuff, at least if you’re going in a straight line, but the lack of stiffness holds it back on slower speed, tighter trails, especially ones that require heavy braking. That chassis flex is the main sticking point - the 15mm thru-axle has its work cut out for it, and there’s a distinct lack of front end precision in certain scenarios. There’s also the extra weight to contend with, and the sky high price. For riders that are enamored with the looks and traction that the Nine One delivers those points can be overlooked, but for most riders going with a right-side-up, less expensive option will be the best bet. — Mike Kazimer Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Suspension Fork Push Push Nine One Author Info: Mike Kazimer Bellingham, WA, United States Member since Feb 1, 2009 1,975 articles Based in Bellingham, Washington, Mike Kazimer is Editor in Chief at Pinkbike, where he’s been covering all things mountain bike related for the last 14 years. Before joining Pinkbike, he worked in bike and ski shops for over a decade. Between all the biking and skiing he managed to pick up an English degree from Western Colorado University.

title: TRP EVO Pro Brake Review: The 'Set and Forget' Favorite Now Has More Adjustability
author: Dario DiGiulio
article_text: TRP Evo Review https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/bisBJiM6 Copied Loading ad Live 00:25 00:00 01:31 TRP EVO PRO TRP's brakes have been on a steady upwards trajectory over the past few years, with the DH-R EVO cementing the brand as one of the main competitors in the space. The recently updated EVO PRO is a refinement of the previous model, adding adjustments at the lever and modifying the ergonomics to better suit the wide array of preferences out there. They piggyback on an existing caliper design, meaning existing users can upgrade the lever without replacing the entire system. EVO PRO Details • Mineral oil • Adjustments: Reach, pad contact • 2 pad options • Price: $616 USD (Black, pair) • Brake weight: 315g (front) • Rotor weight: 224g (203mm) • trpcycling.com BUY AT: Thunder Mountain The EVO PRO is easily associated with their loud gold anodization, but are also available in silver and black finishes, for those that want a stealthier look. TRP uses mineral oil in their brakes, and states that they are only to be used with their 2.3mm thick rotors. Setup The TRP bleed procedure is quite easy, and requires very little upkeep to maintain performance. On the whole, these are some of the best brakes for those that simply want to set them up once and never think about them again, thanks to a robust design and lack of required maintenance or adjustment. TRP recommends a piston massage while initially setting up the brakes, in order to get all four pistons firing at the same time. You can use an old brake rotor or a plastic tire lever to gently move them back in after advancing them, with only a couple cycles needed to get things in sync. New to the EVO PRO is what they've dubbed the Pad Activation Dial, which allows the user to change how sharp the initial bite of the brake is. You can make the bite firm and solid, or soft and easier to modulate, all via a dial inside the lever. The levers still implement a simple reach adjust mechanism, with a far wider range of reaches than the previous DH-R EVO. Though neither Matt nor I experienced any issues with leaking on these brakes or a few other test sets, I have heard of such issues from other users. Livability: Easy funnel bleed, very long service interval. Good lever adjustment, with meaningful change to feel and performance to suit different users. Feel The previous generation of TRP's gravity brakes - the DH-R EVO - was very well received, save for one issue: the ergonomics simply didn't work for some users. The lever reach was quite far from the bar, even at the minimum adjustment, and didn't suit those with smaller hands. The EVO PRO solves for that, implementing a more aggressively hooked lever shape and a revised reach adjuster that allows for a wider usable range. It might not be their calling card, but TRP has achieved a nice light lever feel that doesn't have as much of a learning curve as brakes like the Hayes Dominion. There's little resistance when pulled, but it's still easy enough to intuit where you are in the stroke and get a feel for when the power will come on. That activation point is incredibly consistent, with no change in the throw across long runs and lengthy service intervals alike. Feel Ranking: Light lever action that doesn't feel vague or too feathery. The sturdy and stiff feel gives them a strong feel under force, and works well with their extremely consistent bite point. Water Bottle Pull Test: 503 g pull force (4th overall) As you'll see in the full-length video review, this test simulated the force required to get the brake to the bite point. Water was incrementally added to a bottle attached to the brake's lever, until bite force was achieved. Power The power on tap with the EVO PRO doesn't feel drastically different to that of the DH-R EVO, but don't take that as a negative. The TRP feel is fairly average when it comes to reefing as much power as possible out of the brakes, but that is more than enough to control speed in extreme terrain, especially if you don't have perfect on/off braking technique. The initial bite isn't as sharp as the strongest options like the Mavens, Saints, or Hopes, but once you pull into the stroke the power ramps up nicely. It's worth noting that these new brakes are just a revised lever, with the caliper remaining the same. That caliper houses four 16mm pistons, and is compatible with either lever option, should you want to swap between the two. We ran the blue Performance Resin pads for this test, matched up to the RS05E rotors. Those ultra-perforated rotors add quite a bit of power to the system, and are a notable upgrade for those running the older rotor design. The Resin pads have a nice initial bite, all the more so if you're very diligent about bedding them in correctly - we found that this played a notable role in how much power was felt at the wheel. All brakes benefit from patient bedding, but the TRPs seemed a bit more sensitive to a rushed job. Power Ranking: Mid pack in this group, but adequate for any serious riding. The RS05E rotors go a long way in increasing power, and are a solid upgrade for the older DH-R EVO as well. Honda Fit Stop Test: 27.4 m / 90 ft (5th overall) As you'll see in the full-length video review, this test was meant to highlight the raw stopping power of each brake. We attached Matt to the back of Dario's Honda Fit, revved up to a consistent speed, at which point Matt used only the bike brakes to slow the vehicle to a measured stop. Pros + Easy setup, low maintenance + Approachable power + Good ergonomics Cons - Lacks some initial bite compared to top options - Good setup critical to max power - Old model (DH-R EVO) is just as good Pinkbike may earn commission on any sales through affiliate links. Learn more . Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Big Brake Test Brakes Trp Trp Evo Pro Author Info: Dario DiGiulio Bellingham, WA, United States Member since Dec 25, 2016 484 articles Dario has been obsessed with bikes from an early age, and has worked in the industry since 2015. With a Bachelor's in Architecture from Cal Poly, San Luis Obispo, he aims to bring that mix of creativity and technicality to his work in the mountain bike world.

title: Review: The DT Swiss DEG DF Anti-Pedal Kickback Hub Upgrade is Smartly Designed
author: Mike Kazimer
article_text: DT Swiss Deg DF https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/5VJtV5NL Copied Loading ad Live 00:25 00:00 07:35 The mountain bike world, or at least the portion that dwells in online forums and bike shops, has been spending a lot of time on the topic of pedal kickback lately. Did something change in bike design that suddenly made this a hot topic? Not really, other than the introduction of devices designed to reduce the phenomenon. DT has joined in with their new DF technology (DF stands for Degrees of Freedom). The system, which is retrofittable to any DT DEG hub , is elegant in its simplicity - it doesn’t add any extra weight, and doesn’t require any more maintenance than a standard freehub would. It still uses DT’s star ratchet system, but the inner ratchet ring has 6 protrusions that interact with the ring that’s threaded into the hub shell. That threaded ring has slots that allow riders to easily select from 0°, 10°, or 20° degrees of movement before the star ratchet system engages. Deg DF Details • 3 positions: 0°, 10°, 20° • 90-tooth star ratchet ring • Compatible with all DT Ratchet DEG hubs • Includes ratchet rings, threaded ring, springs, special grease • MSRP: $176.20 USD ($203.30 with threaded ring removal tool) • dtswiss.com When coasting, the spring tension from the star ratchet system pushes the inner ring backwards to its starting position. When the chain is moved forward, whether from pedalling or chain growth, the system moves by that pre-set amount, and then the freehub begins to engage. The ratchet rings have 90 teeth, so at the maximum you're looking at either 14 or 24 degrees of rotation before engagement. The upgrade kit is priced at $176.20, that is, if you already have the tool needed to remove the ring that threads into the hub shell. If you need the tool, the total price bumps up to $203.30 USD. The kits include two 90-tooth DEG DF star ratchet rings, two springs, the threaded inner ring, and a small tube of DT's special grease. There's also an e-bike compatible version that uses a 60-tooth ratchet ring and is designed to withstand the higher toque put out by a motor. There are only two settings for that, 0 or 10-degrees. Only a few parts are required to upgrade a DEG hub. Photo: DT Swiss. What's pedal kickback again? Pedal kickback occurs when the chain tugs on the cranks as a bike’s suspension is compressed due to the increasing distance between the rear axle and the bottom bracket, potentially leading to a noticeable harshness underfoot. That’s the very, very simple explanation - a bike’s suspension layout, gear selection, and speed all play a role. In addition, some of the harshness or feedback that’s transmitted through the pedals isn’t necessarily pedal kickback. Watch any of the Huck to Flat videos from over the years and you’ll see just how much the chain flails around during an impact. There’s a lot going on, even with a clutch-equipped derailleur, and having what’s essentially a steel whip flapping at a high rate of speed influences how a bike feels too. Wouldn’t a low engagement hub do the same thing? This question comes up a lot, and it's a valid one. A lower engagement hub is less likely to transmit pedal kickback, but the issue is that it’s not consistent. Sometimes you might have the full 20 degrees of float before the freehub engages, while other times it might engage almost immediately. With the DT Deg DF, the amount of float should remain consistent. Photo: DT Swiss Installation Installation of the DEG DF is extremely easy; the only potential source of difficulty will be removing the original threaded ring from the hub shell. Those suckers can get really, really tight, and a good bench vice or a long breaker bar will be handy to help remove it. Once that ring is removed the new one gets threaded in and the 90-tooth DF ratchet rings replace the old ones - it’s as easy as that. 0 seconds of 15 seconds Volume 90% https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/dTlEcbZU Copied Live 00:00 00:15 00:15 On the Trail Testing the DEG DF was fairly simple - being able to turn it off by selecting the 0° position made it easy have a good baseline to start from, and switching to the different float settings only required taking my rear wheel off with an Allen key - no other tools were necessary. Most of my miles were spent with the hub installed on a Santa Cruz Bronson, and I also got in a bunch of late season laps in the Whistler Bike Park with it installed on a Trek Fuel LX. Does it work? In a word, yes. I wouldn’t consider myself overly sensitive to pedal kickback, but this hub is clearly doing something that changes the feel of the bike underfoot. I ran flat pedals in order to maximize the chances of being able to notice a difference, and chose the chunkiest trails I could find in Whistler’s Creekside zone. After a lap in the 0° setting, which would be the same as running a standard high-ish engagement hub, I swapped over to the 20-degree setting. The difference was very noticeable - the bike felt smoother, and all the micro-tugs on the chain that I didn’t even think I was feeling previously were erased. It was on the medium speed, stairstep-like sections of trail where I felt the most substantial change. It's a little tricky to put into words, since the hub is removing a sensation rather than adding a new one, but overall the bike felt less busy underfoot, and settled more easily into its travel. I wouldn’t want to run the 20° setting on a pedaly ride with lots of climbing, since the lag between pushing on the pedals and the engagement point is quite noticeable - it’s not the ideal setup for rides where you’ll be pedaling more than coasting. On a DH bike, though, this would be the setting I’d choose for the maxiumum level of calmness. The 10° setting is what I’ve spent the most time running, since the engagement is quick enough for technical climbing, and it still calms down the amount of feedback. DEG DF vs Ochain A good amount of my riding this past year has been on that aforementioned Bronson with an Ochain installed, which I normally run in the 6° or 9° setting (0, 6°, 9°, and 12° are the options). I was skeptical until I tested it, but I’m convinced that the Ochain has real benefits - there a noticeable the level of calm that it imparts on the overall feel of the bike. It’s almost like running a lower shock or tire pressure; there’s a little bit more give when things get rough. On one of my testing days I ran the DEG DF hub and an Ochain active spider so that I could easily do back-to-back runs - both devices have an off position, so I could turn the DEF DF to 0° engagement and focus on the Ochain, or turn the Chain to 0° engagement and focus on the DEG. I did turn both of them on simultaneously for a brief section of trail just to try it out, and that went as expected - it felt like I was running the sloppiest hub in the world, with a huge amount of crank rotation required to get the hub to engage. Weight: The DEG DG takes this point, since it really doesn’t have a weight penalty, while the Ochain adds around 160 grams. Price: If you already have a DEG hub, the upgrade kit is $176 USD. The Ochain R is $450, and that doesn’t include the 4-bolt chainring. Service: There are a lot of small parts in an Ochain, and a rebuild takes time in patience. With the DEG DF, a clean and re-grease takes a matter of minutes. Chain noise: The Ochain does a better job of quieting down chain noise, and it has a softer feel underfoot than the DEG DF due to the spring and elastomer setup that the chainring rotates on. With the DEG DF, especially in the 20° setting there’s the occasional ‘clunky’ feel when going from open to engaged mode, the same sensation you’d find in a lower engagement hub. With the Ochain, that ‘clunk’ is dissipated by the elastomers, giving an extra level of smoothness. Verdict: There’s a place in the MTB world for both of these devices. The DEG DF takes the win when it comes to simplicity and relative affordability. For riders looking for the quietest and smoothest feeling setup, price and extra maintenance be damned, the Ochain is the way to go. If you want to go even further down the rabbit hole of chain-damping devices, Dario recently put together a comparison review of five different options - you can check that out here . One of the great things about hub-based designs like the DEF DF and the e*thirteen Sidekick is how easy they are to test. In an ideal world, skeptical riders could borrow a wheel from a shop or a friend and try it out on a familiar trail in order to see if they notice a difference. The fact that the DEF DF has a 0° or 'off' setting makes it even easier to evaluate. Pros + Works as intended - the difference in bike feel is noticeable + Very low maintenance, extremely simple design + No added weight Cons - Requires DEG hub (not a con if you already have one) Pinkbike's Take The DEG DF upgrade kit is just that - an upgrade on a proven hub design. It doesn't add any extra weight, it's extremely simple, and best of all, it works. The difference will be more noticeable on some bikes than others, but at the end of the day, it's a low-maintenance device that has a tangible benefit when it comes to how settled a bike feels in rough terrain. — Mike Kazimer Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Hubs DT Swiss Dt Swiss Deg Author Info: Mike Kazimer Bellingham, WA, United States Member since Feb 1, 2009 1,975 articles Based in Bellingham, Washington, Mike Kazimer is Editor in Chief at Pinkbike, where he’s been covering all things mountain bike related for the last 14 years. Before joining Pinkbike, he worked in bike and ski shops for over a decade. Between all the biking and skiing he managed to pick up an English degree from Western Colorado University.

title: Review: Reserve 30|HD AL vs Race Face Turbine Wheels
author: Mike Kazimer
article_text: Reserve 30|HD AL wheels. Race Face Turbine wheels. Reserve 30|HD AL HD Details • Internal rim width: 30mm • Rim material: 6069 aluminum • 32 Sapim spokes, DT Swiss 350 hubs • Weight (mixed wheel 30|HD): 2036 grams (989 g front / 1047 g rear w/ valves) • Price: $699 USD / Rim only: $150 USD • Lifetime warranty • reservewheels.com Turbine Details • Internal rim width: 30mm • Rim material: 6069 aluminum • 28 spokes, Race Face Vault hubs • Weight (mixed wheel): 1847 grams ( 850 g front / 997 g rear w/ valves) • Price: $789 USD • Lifetime warranty • raceface.com Lifetime warranties used to be the domain of carbon wheelsets, a perk offered as a way to give prospective buyers incentive to pony up the extra cash that going the carbon route requires. Now, that same benefit is starting to trickle down to aluminum wheels. This season, we saw Reserve kick things off with their new 30|HD AL wheelset, and Race Face followed suit two months later with the new Turbine wheels. Both wheelsets are in a similar price bracket, and they're both aimed at the trail / enduro crowd, so a proper head-to-head comparison seemed appropriate. We'll start with the basic stats, and then dig into the performance out on the trail in order to see which option rises to the top. The Reserve rims have a 30mm inner width... The Turbine rims also have a 30mm inner width, although a different profile is used for the front and rear wheels. Rim Design The Turbine and Reserve 30 AL wheelsets both have a 30mm internal width, a number that's become the norm over the last five years, and it allows them to play well with tires between 2.3 – 2.6” wide. I didn't experience any issues getting tires seated and sealed on either wheelset, and the fact that the rims aren’t carbon makes me feel a little less guilty if I do end up needing to go hard with a tire lever. The Fillmore tubeless valves on the Reserve wheels work especially well, moving a ton of air quickly, and it's great to see them included considering they retail for $50 a set on their own. Both wheelsets have an asymmetric rim profile, a feature that allows for a better spoke bracing angle and more even spoke tension, although Race Face goes a step further and uses a different rim shape for the front and rear wheel. The same rim is used for both Reserve wheels, it's just that the orientation is flipped from front to back. The front Turbine rim has profile that's 18 millimeters high, a design that's intended to give it more compliance than the 20 millimeter tall rear rim. Both of those numbers are lower than the Reserves, which measure 22 millimeters tall. The Reserve wheels use 32 J-bend Sapim spokes, while the Turbines use 28 straight pull spokes. Straight pull spokes can be a little trickier to source in a pinch, but Race Face does include 5 spares with each wheelset. Based on the stats alone there's not a clear winner in this category. Personally, I prefer J-bend spokes to straight pull, but those spares that Race Face includes make that even less of a quibble. On the Reserve 30 wheels the DT Swiss 350 hubs come with the 36-tooth star ratchet rings. The Vault hub found on the Turbine wheels uses a 60-tooth ratchet ring that rotates against 6 pawls housed in the hub shell. Hub Design / Engagement The Reserve 30 HD AL wheels I tested are laced to DT Swiss 350 hubs that use a 36-tooth ratchet ring to achieve 10-degrees between engagement points. Race Face manufactures their own Vault hubs, which have a quicker engagement of 3-degrees thanks to a 6-pawl design and a 60-tooth ratchet ring on the freehub body. I'm not one to get hung up on having the absolute fastest engaging hub possible, especially since I've found it's not something I think about once I'm a few hundred yards down the trail. Still, for riders with that higher up their priority list the Vault hubs take the point here. Weight & Price: Reserve: 2036 grams (989 g front / 1047 rear) / $699 USD Turbine: 1847 grams (850 g front / 997 rear) / Price: $789 USD I tested the mixed-wheel version of both wheels, and on my scale the Turbine wheelset was 189 grams (.4 lb) lighter than the Reserve wheels. The Turbines are also $90 more, which is typically the way it goes – the less something weighs the more it costs, at least when it comes to items like wheels and frames. Overall, the weight of both wheelsets is very reasonable, especially for aluminum enduro wheels. On the trail, I didn't notice the weight difference when going from one wheelset to the other. It's not that .4 lb isn't worth keeping in mind, it just that it's not a significant enough number to noticeably affect the handling. Ding. There are a couple of smaller dents on the Reserve wheels, likely delivered by the Whistler Bike Park. Durability Both wheels saw their fair share of wet rides earlier in the season, and lately they've been subjected to dust, dust, and more dust. All of the bearings are spinning smoothly, and doing a quick clean and re-grease is very simple for both wheelsets – no tools are required to pull off the freehub body to access the racheting mechanisms. As for the rims themselves, the Reserve rear rim has picked up a few dents, although none of them are large enough to really worry about, and the tire is still securely seated. The Whistler Bike Park is hard on equipment, especially when the trails are running at full speed. The Turbine rims are unscathed so far, and are still looking fresh aside from some scuffs here and there. While the dents on the Reserves are worth noting, I wouldn't necessarily take this to mean that the Turbine rims are more durable – my ride time on the wheels is similar, but the rides themselves haven't been exactly identical. Warranties Both wheels have very generous warranties that go beyond the typical coverage against manufacturing defects. Turbine: • If you crash and destroy a wheel, the warranty applies • If you dent or flat spot your Turbine rim and your tire no longer holds air, the warranty applies • If you dent or flat spot your Turbine rim and your tire still holds air, the warranty does not apply. Keep riding! • Seam separation and/or cracks at the spoke hole, the warranty applies • Hub wear such as bearings or freewheels are covered by Race Face's 2-year Limited Warranty Reserve: • Lifetime warranty for original owner • Crash replacement rim or wheel cost at 50% of retail cost • Ship out complete wheels as first option, rims and service credit as second option Detail on Issues • Dented rim, no paint chipping, holding air: crash replacement • Dented rim, paint chipping, holding or not holding air: warranty • Dented rim, not holding air: warranty • Seam separation: warranty • Crack at spoke hole: warranty Ride Performance That's enough comparing points of engagement and spokes sizes – how do the damn things compare on the trail? To find out, I mounted both wheelsets with Continental Kryptotal tires, inflated the front to 21 psi and the rear to 23 psi and headed to the Whistler Bike Park for a round of back-to-back testing. I consider myself fairly well in tune with what a bike and its associated components are doing underneath me, but I wouldn't claim to be able to tell the difference between an extra scoop of tire sealant in one wheel, or a quarter turn less spoke tension on another – it takes something more substantial for me to pick up on it, and that turned out to be the case with these two wheelsets. After switching from the Reserve wheels to the Turbines and heading out to do the same lap, a mix of tighter turns with some chunkier sections followed by a higher speed berm and jump filled trail, the Turbines clearly felt more forgiving. They were also noisier too, but I'll explain that more in a minute. The most compliant wheels I've tried in recent memory are the 3Zero Moto carbon wheels. Those wheels had enough give to them that they could feel vague at times, especially in a bike park setting. The Turbine wheels aren't that dramatically compliant, and they held up well to multiple berm blasting runs on A-line without any unnerving squirminess. That said, the Reserve wheels felt more solid, especially when hitting corners at high speeds, or landing into a chunky section of trail. I was able to get used to the handling of both wheelsets quickly, and didn't experience any undue harshness from either set, but the Reserves are noticeably stiffer, and I didn't find myself thinking about them as much as the Turbines. They just roll along and do their job like good aluminum wheels should. The Turbines rolled well and tracked well, but they also had a tendency to make noise, a resonant 'twang' when loaded up into steep turns, or if an errant trailside branch happened to contact them. The oversized hub shell and shorter spokes seem like the likely culprit here, creating an echo chamber of sorts. I'd put myself on the more sensitive side of the spectrum when it comes to noise (my big ears hear a lot), so some riders might not be as fussed by this trait, but its worth a mention. Pinkbike's Take If I'd narrowed my list of options down to these two wheelsets which one would I choose? Personally, I'd go with the Reserves. They were quieter and had a reassuringly solid feel without being harsh; those traits combined with the reliability of the DT Swiss 350 hub, the added bonus of the Fillmore valve stems, and the lower price makes them my pick. At the end of the day, both wheels are great options in this category, especially considering the lifetime warranties. If quicker engagement and a lighter weight were higher on my priority list I would have gone with the Turbines, but as it is the overall ride feel of the Reserve HD AL wheels won me over. — Mike Kazimer Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Wheels Race Face Reserve Author Info: Mike Kazimer Bellingham, WA, United States Member since Feb 1, 2009 1,975 articles Based in Bellingham, Washington, Mike Kazimer is Editor in Chief at Pinkbike, where he’s been covering all things mountain bike related for the last 14 years. Before joining Pinkbike, he worked in bike and ski shops for over a decade. Between all the biking and skiing he managed to pick up an English degree from Western Colorado University.

title: Video: Welcome to Pinkbike's 2024 Value MTB Field Test
author: Mike Kazimer
article_text: Revealing The 6 Bikes We Tested For The 2024 Value Bike Field Test https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/38cjDbWC Copied Loading ad Live 00:25 00:00 04:53 Mountain biking is an expensive sport, and for newcomers who have never really thought about the price of a decent bike that first trip to a local shop can be a real eye opener. “It costs how much?” is a constant refrain when someone sees the price of the latest carbon superbike, inevitably followed by “I could buy a (insert unrelated item here) for that much.” Luckily, you don't need a bike that costs as much as a year's rent to have a good time on the trails – far from it. That's where the Value Field Test comes in. For this edition, we brought in three hardtails and three full-suspension options, all priced under $2,800 USD. That's still a decent chunk of money, but what you're getting for that price these days is better than ever. Modern geometry has trickled down to even the most affordable options, and brake, drivetrain, and suspension technology continues to improve. All of the bikes shown below could serve as excellent starting points for riders who are starting to get serious about the sport, or even for already-addicted riders looking to upgrade an aging ride without breaking the bank. Marin San Quentin 29 3 • 140mm fork • 29" wheels • 64° head tube angle • 77° seat tube angle • Reach: 470mm (L) • Chainstay length: 430mm • Weight: 31.9 lb / 14.40 kg • MSRP: $1,799 USD • More info: marinbikes.com Haro Saguaro 2 • 140mm fork • 29" wheels • 64.5° head tube angle • 76.5° seat tube angle • Reach: 475mm (L) • Chainstay length: 435mm (L) • Weight: 33.6 lb / 15.12 kg • MSRP: $2,399 USD • More info: harobikes.com Devinci Kobain Deore 12S • 130mm fork • 29" wheels • 65.5° head tube angle • 75° seat tube angle • Reach: 445 (M) • Chainstay length: 435mm • Weight: 31.2 / 14 kg • $1,929 USD (on sale for less) • More info: devinci.com Cannondale Habit 4 • Travel: 130mm rear, 140mm front • 29" wheels (27.5" on XS) • 65.5° head tube angle • 77.5° seat tube angle • Reach: 480mm (L) • Chainstay length: 445mm (L) • Weight: 35.6 lb / 16 kg • $2,300 USD • More info: cannondale.com YT Jeffsy Core 1 • Travel: 145mm rear, 150mm front • 29" wheels • 65° head tube angle • 78.3° seat tube angle • Reach: 475mm (L) • Chainstay length: 437mm (L) • Weight: 36.3lb / 16.3 kg • $2,799 USD • More info: yt-industries.com Giant Stance 29 1 • Travel: 125mm rear, 140mm front • 29" wheels • 65.5° head tube angle • 76.5° seat tube angle • Reach: 445mm (M) • Chainstay length: 440mm • Weight: 34 lb / 15.3 kg • $2,899 CDN (not yet available in US) • More info: giant-bicycles.com Testing Info Download the Trailforks App The bulk of our testing took place in Squamish, BC. Conditions were on the rainier side of things, perfect for finding the limits of budget brake and tire traction. Rupert served as our main test track, and as you'll see in the videos, it has a good mix of rock slabs and roots, without getting too crazy. It's a single black diamond trail that's extremely well built, and can be a good time on everything from a hardtail to an enduro bike. Who Tested the Bikes? Dario DiGiulio Height: 6'3" / 191cm Weight: 175 lb / 79.4 kg Notes: Tech Editor, the king of customization. Mike Kazimer Height: 5'11" / 180cm Weight: 160 lb / 72.6 kg Notes: Gear Director, serial cereal eater Sarah Moore Location: Squamish, BC Height: 5'7" / 170 cm Weight: 160 lb / 72.6 kg Notes: Content Manager, maple syrup connoisseur Reviews, the Huck to Flat, and more value bike content is on the way this week and next.
