title: Staff Rides: Seb Stott’s Pivot Firebird
author: Seb Stott
article_text: Pivot Firebird Staff Ride https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/beSjWRDw Copied Loading ad Live 00:25 00:00 12:48 STAFF RIDES Seb Stott's Pivot Firebird If you want a big, burly enduro bike that could happily be your only bike, the Pivot Fiebird pretty much nails the brief. Its DW4 suspension offers as-good-as-it-gets pedalling performance for the category, while it has plenty of travel and well-judged geometry for descending. Its ride feel is composed and confident on the gnarly stuff without feeling lethargic on mellower terrain. While my riding impressions haven't changed too much since my initial review , I am now more confident in saying this is one of the best all-round enduro bikes out there. I've been riding this bike since last May, using it as a test bench for new parts and a base to experiment with. Let's get into what I like, what I don't and what I've changed about the Firebird. Pivot Firebird Details • Wheel size: 29" or MX compatible • 170 mm rear travel(measured, long chainstay setting), 170mm fork • 63.8° head angle, 76.5° seat angle • Size specific & adjustable chainstay • Three bottle bosses & downtube storage • Sizes: XS, S, M, L, XL • Actual weight (stock): 16.1 kg / 35.5 lb • Actual weight (as it sits now): 17.1 kg / 37.7 lb • Lifetime original owner frame warranty & bearing replacement • Price: $6,499 to £13,699 ($8,699 as tested) • pivotcycles.com What's good Suspenion I've come to think the DW4 linkage could be the best all-around system there is. Thanks to the co-rotating links, it's got a solid amount of anti-squat and anti-rise right the way through the travel, which means the bike doesn't pitch as much when braking or pedalling and responds consistently. It feels stable and composed, but without becoming harsh under braking. It's got a well-judged progression curve, too, offering plenty of support without making the end-stroke abrupt or harsh. I've stuck with the frame's more progressive setting, which plays nicely with both coil and air shocks. Anti-squat. Anti-rise. It doesn't erase bumps as completely as some bikes, but it does a great job of taming the chaos when you really need it, without moving around too much and upsetting the chassis. I measured the usable travel on my test bike at over 170 mm in the long chsainstay setting (5 mm more than claimed), which helps explain the capable feel when things get spicy. Best of all, it pedals really efficiently for such a long-travel bike, which makes the Firebird more rewarding on climbs or mellower terrain - and partially covers for the slack seat tube. Geometry (mostly) I've ridden plenty of longer bikes, and I've ridden plenty of shorter bikes. My current thinking is that somewhere around 1,310 mm wheelbase and 510 mm reach is ideal for me, at 191 cm / 6'3". Much longer, and the bike starts to feel more unwieldy with steeply diminishing returns in terms of confidence and stability. It's been said that the Firebird has a particularly rearward weight bias, but it's about average with the chainstay set to the long setting. It measures 446 mm in XL. In my view, the downsides start to outweigh the advantages when chainstays get much longer than that - at least for the steep tech trails I enjoy. Frame details The frame seems well-made and thought-through. There's sensible cable routing, no cable rattle, good tire clearance and really generous heel clearance. I don't tend to use internal storage often, but the Firebird's downtube door is secure, and the ability to fit two bottles inside the front triangle is a nice bonus. Many of my rides are too long for just one bottle, but doable with two. Reliability While I should caveat that this has been far from my only bike this past year, I've been impressed with how the Firebird's held up. Despite many muddy rides and regular washing, there's been no creaking, rattling or bolts coming loose. And unlike some bikes, it doesn't collect water after washing. Parts-wise, I've become increasingly impressed by SRAM Transmission over the years. While I'm not a fan of having more batteries to charge, I'm now in the habit of carrying a spare. And the shifting reliability is very impressive; even if you shift through multiple gears unsympathetically on steep climbs, cover it in mud, or scrape it off tree stumps, it changes gear crisply virtually every time. The Maven brakes have had two bleeds and a piston massage. The latter is essential to get them to work at their best, which I think explains the mixed reviews. While they can be fussy, the power and control are superb. But I must say that the Hayes Dominions on my staff ride last year had zero maintenance. A simple modification or a cry for help? You be the judge. What's not so good Seat tube angle Pivot were aiming for a 77° effective seat angle, according to their geometry chart, but at my saddle height, it measures 76.5°. Either way, I think it could be steeper. The supportive suspension makes up for the (relatively) laid-back position to such an extent that I'd still call it a good climber, but having tried it with the Fair Bicycle offset saddle clamp , which steepens the angle to 78.5°, Pivot missed a trick by not making it this steep from stock. Turns out, having a steep seat angle and efficient suspension is a winning combination. I had previously tried the high flip-chip setting to improve the climbing position, but I preferred the low setting for descending. My feet felt too high relative to the front contact patch on steep descents in high mode. It's worth pointing out that the flip chip doesn't fully compensate for wheel size changes, so if you want to run it as a mullet, the seat angle will be slacker still. SuperBoost (sort of) I don't consider SuperBoost a deal breaker, but it's been frustrating not to have the option to swap out some of the other enduro/trail wheels I have lying around. (Is it just me who has multiple wheelsets lying around?) On the other hand, the ability to share wheels between your enduro and DH bike could be handy for some people. It's also worth saying that you can adapt a 148 mm wheel to fit a 157 mm frame (with spacers and a re-dish) but not the other way around. Plus, I've subjected the rear wheel to several clumsy landings, and it remains true and even-tensioned, so maybe there's something in it. Having typed all that, I'm not 100% sure this should be in the dislikes section. Price It's $8,699 as tested (not including upgrades). Enough said. What I've changed • The first change was from the 210 mm travel Fox seatpost to a 240 mm OneUp V3. I just prefer more drop. • Peaty's Monarch knurled grips replaced Pivot's own. I find them more comfortable, and I like to keep grips consistent between bikes. • I swapped the WTB Volt for a Fizik Aidon eMTB saddle, which I find gives a more forward position for steep climbs. • Fair Bicycle offset saddle clamp, as mentioned • I tried 40, 45 and 50 mm stems, but settled on 45 mm. The 50 mm stem that comes stock on the XL felt too stretched out for me on steep tech descents. • I tried out a Fox Live Valve coil shock for my own curiosity and because Pivot sells it as an upgrade. Is it worth the money? No. It's impressive, but this bike simply doesn't need it to avoid pedal-bob, and if it's set closed-biased enough to reliably "prop up" the rear suspension on steep climbs (to make up for the seat angle), it occasionally stays locked when I want it open. On a fire road with occasional bumps, the suspension feels like it's ratcheting up on the smooth bits, then jolting down when you hit a small bump and the shock opens up. I'd prefer a remote manual lockout to hold it locked for a sprint or steep climb, then leave it open the rest of the time. The bike works well with a coil shock. As you'd expect, it's slightly more sensitive than the Float X2, but less tuneable and around 250 g heavier. I setted on 500 lb/in for my 86 kg weight, which is slightly firmer than Fox's recommendation. • I fitted a Level Of Future handlebar for testing. I'm still not sure how much difference it makes to hand/arm fatigue, but I prefer its 40 mm of rise to the stock handlebar's 35 mm. • I've also fitted a mudguard and a stickier front tire for winter (the Firebird came with the Soft, not SuperSoft compound from Continental). All these changes have added a full kilogram to the Frebird's weight. But thanks to the offset seat clamp, it climbs even better than stock. Back when it was new last March. Is it a good bike? While it's frustrating that the seat angle is slacker than I'd like, the Firebird remains one of my all-time favorite enduro bikes. The offset seat clamp is a conspicuously ugly solution, but it does work. What you can't change is a bike's frame construction, sizing, and suspension kinematics, and Pivot nailed those. The heart of the bike is the suspension, which is so well-rounded as to create one of the most versatile bikes around. It feels predictable and rewarding on a wide range of terrain, from self-powered singletrack to burly shuttle laps. It was a close contender for Pinkbike's Bike of the Year 2025 , but while we decided that the uncompromising Orbea Rallon is more exciting, it's precisely the Firebird's vanilla versatility that makes it great. Posted In: Reviews and Tech Staff Rides Reviews Pivot Pivot Firebird Seb Stott Author Info: Seb Stott Innerleithen, Scotland Member since Dec 29, 2014 526 articles Seb's been working as a mountain bike technical editor since 2015, and has reviewed hundreds of bikes and components in that time. Before that, he studied experimental physics at the University of St Andrews, where he got into the habit of measuring everything possible. Based in the Tweed Valley, Scotland, he loves riding trail, enduro and e-bikes, and is happiest when testing them back-to-back to see how they compare.

title: Review: 6 Months Aboard the Specialized Levo SL eMTB
author: Matt Beer
article_text: REVIEW: Specialized Levo SL WORDS: Matt Beer PHOTOS: Max Barron Levo Sl V3 0 seconds of 4 minutes, 31 seconds Volume 90% https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/oCoaUW8H Copied Live 00:00 04:31 04:31 More Videos 22:30 Jason Mraz 22:54 Lone Rider | Ultimate Rush 22:24 2017 GoPro Mountain Games 01:00 SENT. | Trailer 22:06 Desert Lines | Ultimate Rush 22:30 Legacy 22:30 Nick Troutman 22:30 Leila Hurst 22:30 Middle East 22:30 Evolution Close What do you get when you hide a battery and motor inside of a Specialized Stumpjumper EVO? The Levo SL, a sub-18kg eMTB with a carbon frame, 150mm of travel, and enough geometry adjustments to keep any style of rider satisfied. Stealthy looks aside, the Levo SL is intended to ride like an unassisted bike too. Compared to other 25kg-plus behemoths out there, riders should be able to pop off the trail without inflicting a hernia and have no trouble slowing down before the brakes catch on fire. Dubbed the “electric Stumpjumper EVO” by Mike Kazimer, it can theoretically tag along with full-powered eMTBs when climbing (at least in their lowest assist-modes) but it’ll cost more energy from you and the 320Wh battery. That's due to the upgrade from 35 to 50Nm on the new SL 1.2 motor. What’s the price tag on this transformable, lightweight eMTB then? Our S-Works model checks out at a cool $14,000 USD, which includes the extra 160Wh range extender. The Levo SL comes with Fox Factory suspension, premium SRAM wireless controls and Roval carbon components - Specialized left little to be desired on the showroom card. Specialized Levo SL Details • S-Works carbon frame • 150mm travel, 160mm fork • Specialized SL 1.2 motor; 50Nm torque, 320W power • 320Wh battery, 160Wh range extender (inc. w/S-Works) • Wheel size: Mixed or dual 29" • Travel: 150mm rear / 160mm front • 64.6º head angle • 75.8º seat tube angle • 432mm chainstays • Sizes: S1, S2, S3, S4, S5, S6 • Weight: 39.3 lb / 17.8 kg (size S4, S-Works) • MSRP: $8,000 - $14,000 USD • specialized.com Contents Introduction Construction & Features Suspension Design Geometry & Sizing Setup Climbing Descending Value How Does it Compare? Pros & Cons Pinkbike's Take You'll have to get out a fine tooth comb to pick out flaws on this machine. Specialized has the entire Levo SL package covered from suspension performance, to motor demands, and of course the spec on the S-Works model is top notch. — Matt Beer The headset comes with two cups, a 1.0-degree and a 0 offset. This opens the doors to a matrix of geometry options. Changing the cups only requires a 5mm allen key and best of all, it never creaked once. The dropout pivot can also be changed to fit either rear wheel size without altering the geometry if desired. Details matter: the mud flap is mounted to the front triangle to shed mud and rocks. We did observe some squeaking when this area became caked in dust, but some silicone spray resolved that quibble. Another flip-chip rests in the shock eyelet for even more angle adjustments. Frame Details The Levo SL takes on classical Specialized shapes with straight legged, round tubes and FSR suspension. There’s also an abundance of geometry tweaks to be found throughout the frame which can hold a 27.5 or 29” rear wheel. Previously the Levo SL featured a frame-stiffening sidearm, bridging the seat tube and top tube. That member has been removed to increase access to the shock and eliminate the chance of bonking your knee on the brace. Inside the front triangle, a Zee side-load cage holds a common water bottle with grazing the shock reservoir. Aside from all of the possible geometry configurations, the new 1.2SL motor is the main talking point of the Levo SL. That’s been rejigged to produce 50Nm of torque, 15Nm more than the previous generation, and updated to tone down the noise levels. At peak power, it’ll pump out 320W, versus 565W of the full-size Levo. Inside the downtube you’ll find a 320Wh battery, which is removable, but you’ll have to partially unbolt the motor to do so. For most rides, slotting the 160Wh Range Extender in the bottle cage will be the play. If you’re embarking on unknown routes or all-day rides, carrying or stashing a second extender isn’t out of the question. One important consideration when using a Range Extender is that you’ll need to toggle the “deplete range extender first” option if you’re planning on ditching it along the way. Wisely, the charge port has a spring-loaded door and the Range Extender cord locks into place there. The SL 1.2 motor has been bumped up from 35-50Nm. Equally important are the noise levels; after 6 months of riding, the clutch remains calm and quiet while descending. Under load, it produces a low whine, but is not as stealthy as the TQ HPR50. The low-profile remote is easy to locate and change motor assist levels, but the walk-mode button can crowded by other controls depending on where you locate the lever clamp. Elsewhere, the finishing is well thought out with the MasterMind TCU which lies inside the top tube, lowering the chance of smashing any electronic controls. The remote is only as large as it needs to be with four buttons, although I’d like to see a wireless option in the future. Depending on where the dropper post remote is located, you may find it interferes with the walk-mode button. The Specialized App is similarly easy to navigate to change the MasterMind display and make other battery or motor tuning choices. Toggling the alarm and motor lock on gives you peace of mind and doesn’t come at any extra cost either, unlike the Bosch’s system. Between the chain routing lies the wavy chainstay protector, welcomed chainstay mud flap, and an integrated chain guide. Shocking, many eMTBs don't utilize a frame skid plate or have chain protection. The Levo SL has adequate ground clearance in this area, but I'd add a chainring mounted bash guard if this was my own baby. Suspension Design Specialized has been using the four-bar Horst-Link arrangement for decades now. The main pivot lies in line with the 32-tooth chainring but the rear axle sits on the seatstay. This allows for greater independence arranging the suspension kinematics. Likewise, the brake mount is also placed on the seatstay, reducing the compressive braking forces on the suspension. Specialized opted for a 210x55mm (210x50 on the S1 frame) Fox Float X air shock on the Levo SL. That’s been built to their unique “Rx Tune” damping requirements and delivers a low-to-medium amount of progression to the wheel rate - hard bottom-outs only occurred on grimacing impacts while the beginning of the travel is supple enough that finding more forgiveness from a coil shock would be questionable. Levo SL Suspension Squish 0 seconds of 12 seconds Volume 90% https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/IIs3V0Ay Copied Live 00:00 00:12 00:12 More Videos 22:30 Jason Mraz 22:54 Lone Rider | Ultimate Rush 22:24 2017 GoPro Mountain Games 01:00 SENT. | Trailer 22:06 Desert Lines | Ultimate Rush 22:30 Legacy 22:30 Nick Troutman 22:30 Leila Hurst 22:30 Middle East 22:30 Evolution Close Geometry & Sizing Specialized built six size front triangles for the Levo SL. The smallest S1’s tiny frame does limit the rear wheel travel to 144mm. Adaptability is a major talking point of the Levo SL and for good reason. There are three places to reposition hardware on the frame. Flip-chips are found along the chainstay and lower shock yoke, plus the head tube also captures an interchangeable upper cup system. All of the necessary components are included with the bike and can be made with the SWAT multi-tool hidden in the steer tube. The dropout pivot is offset to allow for quick changes between rear wheel sizes without altering the geometry. In the case of a 27.5” rear wheel, you have two choices; a 432mm chainstay length with a -29mm BB drop, or a longer, lower ride position at 442mm and -34mm. Specialized felt that these two lengths fit each half of the size range well, while keeping the nature of the bike on the sporty side. They still offer two options to customers of all sizes, however, I'd wager that riders on the S4 frame size and up will opt for the longer position, regardless of wheelsize. The angles can be tweaked further at the head tube with the use of two cups, a neutral or 1.0 offset option. In total, the head angle can range from 63.8- 65.7 degrees. Straight edges on the otherwise circular cups eliminate alignment errors or shifting in the frame At this time, Specialized doesn’t have the same handy Stumpy EVO geometry calculator tool available for the Levo SL, but they do explain the changes further on the support page . Furthermore, Specialized has an extensive user manual available for the Levo SL, and not just a basic one you toss in the bin when unboxing a new bike. Specifications Specifications Release Date 2023 Price $14000 Travel 150mm Rear Shock Fox Float Factory Rx Tuned Fork Fox 36 Factory, 29", 44mm offset, Grip 2 Cassette SRAM XX Eagle Transmission 10-52T Crankarms SRAM Carbon Crankset Bottom Bracket Specialized 1.2 SL Custom R Rear Derailleur SRAM XX Eagle Transmission Chain SRAM XX Eagle Transmission Flattop Shifter Pods SRAM AXS Pod Ultimate Controller Handlebar Roval Traverse SL Carbon Stem Deity Copperhead 35mm Grips Deity Knuckleduster Brakes SRAM Code Stealth Ultimate, HS2 200mm rotors Wheelset Roval Traverse SL Carbon 29/27.5 Tires Butcher/Eliminator, Grid Trail, T9 compound Seat Bridge, 155/143mm, Hollow Ti-rails Seatpost RockShox Reverb AXS, 30.9mm Compare to other All Mountain/Enduro/XC RIDING THE Levo SL Test Bike Setup When I first laid eyes on the Levo SL, it arrived in the slackest position with the shortest chainstay setting. A 35mm length stem seemed fitting for my tastes, given the 44mm fork offset and head tube angle, but something about the steering threw me off. I trimmed the bars to 770mm and found that the 6 degree upsweep of the Roval bar (8 degree backsweep) needed to be rolled further back than I’d prefer. That placed my hands too far behind the steering axis with the short stem, so I played with a OneUp bar that was more familiar. I’d need to resort to a 50mm stem with the Roval bar. 25mm of spacers were kept underneath at all times. When looking at the suspension setup, I added a third volume spacer to the fork and set that to 106 psi.The damper settings were close to Fox's guidelines aside from the rebound running a few clicks further open. Matt Beer Location: Squamish, BC, Canada Age: 37 Height: 5'10" / 178 cm Weight: 170 lb / 78 kg Industry affiliations / sponsors: None Instagram: @mattb33r Rear shock pressures ranged between 187-197 psi depending on conditions, trail choice and if I was carrying extra goods in a pack. The recommended sag is 13.75mm, but I ended up closer to 12mm most days. I also reduced the volume of air in the rear shock, switching the stock 0.2 spacer to a 0.4 after a few metal clangs on bottom-outs. I could see the benefit of a shock with a hydraulic bottom-out to provide softer stops at full-travel and avoiding the higher return forces of additional volume spacers. A 170mm Reverb AXS dropper post works fine for just about all situations for me, but on occasion, lowering it for mega-steep trails or jump tracks provided extra clearance. Throughout the test, I rode the Levo SL in what I’d consider the most likely configurations, including dual 29” wheels. Discussing the habits of each one would be a painful read, but having the ability to switch wheel sizes without skewing the geometry is reassuring. Climbing I like to think of SL eMTBs as the most lucrative way to save energy getting to the top while still enjoying the freedom of a lightweight bike on the descents. They won’t climb up walls like a full-powered e-bike but it's no sweat to gain more vert per hour than a non-assisted bike. The Levo SL grips the ground insanely well, however, the short chainstay and relaxed seat tube angle (in the more aggressive settings) do place the rider’s seated weight generously over the rear axle. When the grade does turn uphill sharply, you’ll have to be mindful about keeping your weight forward in the saddle. With that said, if you can avoid doing wheelies, the rear wheel sticks to the ground. I'd call it efficient but not that effective. In terms of ground clearance, the BB does ride fairly low, even in the steeper settings. You’ll want to use the climb switch, which is thankfully easy to reach, to avoid the pedal strikes. As stated, you can choose the geometry to shine on climbs - a 29” rear wheel, +1.0 head tube adjustment and high shock setting will do the best job of clearing obstacles if you’re want a more agile and reactive bike for pedalling through undulating trails. As for the motor haptics, the torque winds down and picks up again in a timely fashion. There’s no unexpected bursts of energy at the crank. Restarting on hill climbs isn’t impossible either. The motor clutch engagement is extremely quick too. If you wish to divert from the stock motor settings, there’s no shortage of pedal pickup and power delivery to play with in the app either. On that note though, I did notice that the battery life dropped substantially faster in “Turbo” compared to the “Trail” mode even though the outputs were fairly close. Descending Ironically, with all of the laborious hours that went into building this bike, I could sum it up in just two words; buttery smooth. From the rear suspension to the motor’s power delivery, the Levo SL is very pleasant to rider, and I'd like to think that would be the case for a rider of any level. In our Fall Field Test, we talked about frame stiffness quite a bit. It seems to be a common finding, but round tubes don’t send as many vibrations through the frame to the rider and that adds to the smooth ride of the Levo SL. The rear triangle absorbs off-camber hits and holds through chattery flat corners well, even more so in the longer chainstay setting. The Levo SL’s suspension has a floating feeling about it, eerily like a chainless ride. Almost everything about the rear suspension works seamlessly, whether keeping the wheels on the ground to slow down or pushing into the bike to gain speed. There are no irregularities in the travel such as progression spikes or mid-stroke wallowing. From start to finish, it's consistently smooth. If you’re a rider that stabs the brakes hard between consecutive bomb holes, you could find the bike’s balance to be mildly disrupted at times. On steeper trails where you’re fighting to release the brakes, you’ll appreciate how the wheels stick to the ground though. Normally, my first ride on a test bike ends with at least five negative points, but finding things to complain about the Levo SL was a chore. We got off to solid start, however, there was a peculiarity about the steering took longer than anticipated to debunk. I swapped out to the full 29er setup which helped and actually raised the BB. In the end, I stuck with the longer chainstay setting, even with the 27.5" rear wheel and chalked up the quirky turning habits to the low center of gravity and short chainstay, which gave a false sense of security on the steeper, chunky trails found on the North Shore. Frequently, I’d have to remind myself that this is a 150mm-travel bike and not to get too carried away with my downhill antics. In the slacker settings, the significant BB drop and low center of gravity add to the planted feeling of the Levo SL. The short chainstay and 27.5” rear wheel do slightly detract from its stability, but only when the trail becomes choppy and steep. Switching the chainstay into the long setting (27.5” wheel, shock flip-chip in high position, -1.0 cup) was my preferred arrangement. That provided the most balance and therefore the most traction for rallying through Squamish’s best handbuilt trails. After six months of hard riding, the Levo SL hasn’t missed a beat - not one loose bolt, creaking from the headset, or motor irregularities. It faced everything from blasting through muddy ruts and plinko-ing down blown-out moto tracks. Impressively, the motor clutch is still as quiet as day one as well and the wheels are as straight as an arrow. It wasn’t without a few minor quirks though. The derailleur mount did need to be retorqued a couple weeks into testing and extra slack in the TCU meant rearranging the cable internally to avoid rattling inside the top tube. What's the Best Value? Overall cost aside, when comparing the value of the models in the Levo SL line, the Expert is a sensible choice given it's still a carbon frame loaded with high-performance components - basically, you skip the extra pizazz of the S-Works and a few carbon bits. There is a slight difference in the frame layup of the Comp and Expert, versus the S-Works carbon finish itself, but is that extra weight savings really worth the extra dough? Likely not for most riders - that money could be best saved for spare tires and upgrades down the road. On the specifications side, you still receive the same Fox Performance Elite dampers but skip the flashy Kashima coating. Similarly, the brakes a minor step down to Code Stealth Silver, which use the new lever body style and include reach and bite point adjustments. Similarly, the drivetrain is the latest Transmission type from SRAM which eliminates the derailleur hanger and cables. Specialized Levo SL Trek Fuel EXe How Does It Compare? Trek and Specialized have each cooked up SL eMTBs that showcase their product development team’s skills - there’s little left to be desired if you’re in the market for one of these sub-18kg machines. The Fuel EXe and Levo SL don’t exactly line up against one another in their stock configuration, however, depending on how you set them up, there could be considerable overlap. The Fuel Exe has 10mm less travel at either end, but a 160mm fork isn’t out of the question. Out of the box, it arrives with dual 29” wheels. Again, swapping the stock parts for a 27.5” rear wheel will make it more “flickable”. Those two attributes point the Fuel EXe closer to a 60/40 split of up descending and climbing, versus the Levo SL’s 70/30 realm. A critical change for Specialized to keep up with the market meant bumping the SL 1.2 motor up 50Nm of torque. That matches TQ’s HPR50 now, however, Trek’s integrated battery holds 40 more watt-hours. One thing you don't want more of on an e-bike is noise. While the Levo SL motor acoustics aren't loud, it does have a slightly higher pitched whine compared to the TQ, particularly at higher RPMs. Both the Levo SL and Fuel Exe came in the flashiest trim available, however, the Trek slightly undercuts Specialized in terms of pricing. The Levo SL S-Works costs $1000 USD more than the Fuel Exe’s $13,999 USD price tag. House branded carbon bars, wheel are found on each bike, along with SRAM’s top-tier T-type wireless shifting and Code brakes. Tires are another item sourced within each brands’ garage. Specialized’s rubber is light years ahead of the plastic-like Bontrager treads, which were swiftly removed from the Fuel EXe. Another bonus point for Trek is the entry price. Aluminum Fuel EXes begin at $5,499 USD and still use the sought after TQ motor. Oddly enough though, only the aluminum Fuel EXe is equipped with a interchangeable headset cup to alter the angles which is an option that Trek’s customers spending top dollar would appreciate. Of course, this could be accomplished using aftermarket headsets if desired. Technical Report Fox 36 Factory: Before the burly 38 fork, the 36 served as the long-travel single crown for in Fox’s lineup. Between the two, there is a subtle difference in stiffness and how the air spring operates, but it still packs a punch. With the Grip2 damper and fool-proof volume spacers, it’s still a quality bit of kit for enduro and trail bikes. Code Stealth Ultimate Brakes: The master cylinder may look different on the outside, but internally, they use the identical parts to the previous generation Code RSCs. A reshaped lever has a rounder side profile which seems to be welcomed by all. Most apparent, the brake lines exit on an angle to tie into stem clamps for a neat and quiet setup. SL 1.2 motor: Bumping the torque from 35 to 50Nm may not seem like much on paper, but it’s significant on the trail. Grinding up climbs and lunging up tech moves comes with an added boost. It’s also much quieter than the first generation motor and most importantly, less whiny. Pros + Expectations meet price - neatly packaged features + Highly adaptable geometry + Stealth appearance and operation for an e-bike + Descends like a traditional bike Cons - No aluminum option creates an expensive cost barrier - Taller riders may find their center of gravity sits heavily over the rear wheel, making steep climbs tougher to negotiate Pinkbike's Take We’re not here to say that the Levo SL doesn’t cost a lot of money, but it does live up to everything we expected at this price point. Over the course of a 6-month review term, this bike has been put through the wringer and keeps coming back for more. Better yet, it still looks, feels and sounds like a new bike. As for the ride characteristics, the Levo SL handles more like a traditional bike and is quickly excitable on the trail - it’s neither a slug to pull through jumps, yet it doesn’t hold back at full pace either. In fact, I'd often forget I was riding an eMTB when descending. — Matt Beer Visit the high-res gallery for more images from this review. Posted In: Reviews and Tech eMTB Reviews Trail Bikes Specialized Specialized Levo Sl Author Info: Matt Beer Squamish, BC, Canada Member since Mar 16, 2001 607 articles Matt has been a Pinkbike reader since the site's early days. He discovered mountain biking in Newfoundland, where a small community of dedicated riders and trail builders set him on a path to earn two Canadian National Downhill Championship titles. Now based in Squamish, BC, Matt works as a tech editor for Pinkbike, specializing in gravity-focused mountain bike reviews, tech, and racing coverage.

title: Review: The Forbidden Dreadnought V2 Has a Need for Speed
author: Matt Beer
article_text: Review Forbidden Dreadnought V2 WORDS: Matt Beer PHOTOS: Thomas Renborg The allure of high-pivot bikes are strong, and Forbidden is a brand that's built all of their bikes around that suspension design. They can melt away small bumps and give a sense of having more rear wheel travel than stated, yet that design can come with caveats such as complicated drivetrains and uneasy shifts in rider weight bias. Forbidden’s second generation Dreadnought follows the evolution of their high-pivot suspension design, which moves to a multi-link design and touts other widgets. This enduro battleship has been as much of a mainstay between race tape as it has in bike parks, often with a dual-crown fork. Dreadnought V2 Details • Carbon frame • Wheel size: MX or dual 29 • 160mm rear travel, 170mm fork • 4-bar, high-pivot suspension design • 63.1° head angle (63.5° w/ dual 29") • 77.1° effective seat angle • Sizes: S1, S2, S3, S4 • Reach: 430, 450, 471, 491mm • Chainstays (+/-10mm): 431, 446, 460, 475mm • Sizes: S1, S2, S3, S4 • Weight: 15.6 kg / 34.3 lb (S3, actual) • Price: $8,499-11,999 CAD, $6,499-9,299 USD • forbiddenbike.com For 2024, they’ve also increased the rear wheel travel from 154mm to 160mm and revised the kinematics considerably. They’ve also introduced modular drop-outs to carry either a 27.5” or 29” rear wheel and various chainstay lengths. Being constructed from carbon only, the price tag keeps the entry point higher than other aluminum enduro bikes on the market. The component selection doesn’t spare any expense either. Ranging between $6,499-9,299 USD ($8,499-11,999 CAD), all of the builds use RockShox suspension, Maxxis tires, Crankbrothers wheels, and T-type shifting from SRAM. Contents Introduction Frame Details Suspension Design Geometry & Sizing Specifications Setup Climbing Descending Technical Report What's the Best Value? How Does it Compare? Pros & Cons Pinkbike's Take For every action, there’s a reaction. The Dreadnought is a prime example of Newton’s Third Law of Motion. In some scenarios, it can descend wide-open trails at a frightening pace, yet at other times it can be a handful to navigate at slower speeds. — Matt Beer Frame Details From afar, the Dreadnought V2 bears a similar shape to the original, but get up close and you’ll notice a lot more bolts. Interchangeable dropouts give riders the option to change between a 27.5 or 29” rear wheel, although that can be decided when ordering. Savvy thinking allows a single 200mm post mount brake adapter to be used; the axle feeds through the adapter allowing it to pivot, acting as one mounting point while a series of three bolt holes on the upper portion account for the varying dropout lengths. Smart use of the boxed shaped-frame above the bottom bracket continues to provide a gear storage solution, at least enough for a spare tube. Under the top tube is an accessory mount and a full size water bottle fits inside the front triangle. Cleanly routed internal cables are clamped down by the grommets and ample rubber on the inside faces of the swingarm keep the chain very quiet. Part of that calm, quiet ride is due to the chain security. That includes a substantial cover on the idler pulley, skid plate to protect the chainring, and a lower jockey wheel. You’ll notice that the Druid V2 doesn’t require this extra pulley since the frame has less travel and therefore less chain growth. Forbidden uses an 18-tooth idler pulley wheel positioned slightly off-axis to reduce friction when cross-loading the chain in the larger cogs. During the First Ride, we noticed an increase in drivetrain friction compared to the Druid. We experimented by removing that pulley and expanded on that further down in the Technical Report. Dreadnought squish https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/UsnZVO5Z Copied Live 00:00 00:11 00:11 The Dreadnought is built around a 205x60mm trunnion shock and can handle air or coil sprung dampers. Suspension Design High-pivot suspension is defined by the main swing arm pivot’s height above the chainring. In the case of the Dreadnought, that main pivot is located well above the chainring, slightly offset from the pulley. This moves the rear wheel away from the front triangle through the majority of the travel. To visualize this, pretend the swing arm is the hand on a clock. A high-pivot’s rearward axle path would move from 7-9 o’clock through the suspension travel. Bikes like the Dreadnought require a secondary chainring, or pulley, near the main pivot to account for the increased chain growth as the rear axle moves further away from the bottom bracket through its travel. Now with a multi-link design, the Dreadnought V2's kinematics can be controlled with greater independence between one another. The leverage ratio start and end point values have been increased substantially, which should help lighter-weight rider’s opting for a coil-sprung rear shock find the correct spring rate. The curve has also been smoothed out considerably for more consistency on the trail. The anti-squat is considerably higher than before (136% at sag vs. 116%) and should reduce pedal bob further. Under braking, the anti-rise remains similar aside from a reduction later in the travel. Another change is the axle path. It still moves rearward for the majority of the travel, but towards the end of the travel, the axle moves vertically compared to the V1’s single high-pivot which is rearward through 100% of the travel. Geometry Stability largely relies on wheelbase and that’s something that the Dreadnought has heaps of. If you look at the reach and head angle numbers, they’re pretty typical of an aggressive enduro bike (471mm for a size S3 and 63.1° heat tube angle), but it’s the rear end that really adds to the overall length. And that just continues to grow up larger, up to and beyond the sag point. Forbidden calls the balance between the front and rear centers One Ride geometry. Across the four frame sizes, the front to rear center ratio remains the same. The smallest of those has virtually an equal reach and rear center, or chainstay length, of about 430mm (431mm chainstay in MX mode). That doesn’t sound too wild until you consider the chainstay length of the S3 frame. Most brands typically align their standard size “large” to hit a 480mm reach with chainstays under 450mm in length or less. Forbidden shifts both of those numbers by about 10mm. That equates to the S3 frame having a 471mm reach and 460mm chainstays (0mm offset dropout). Regarding setup and my choice of frame size, its unusual for me (I’m 178cm for reference) to want anything shorter than that in terms of reach at least on this style of bike. But given the 1288mm wheelbase, I can understand why two of the Forbidden Synthesis team races, who are taller than I am, went with an S2 frame. Both Alex Storr and Rhys Verner chose S2 frames with dual 29” wheels and the 0mm offset dropout, landing the chainstay at 446mm (Editor's Note: Since writing the review, both riders have moved to the +10mm setting) . The 29" wheel dropout also steepens the head angle by 0.4 degrees and increases the reach by 4mm. Due to frame clearance concerns, there is no -10mm dropout option for the 29" rear wheel, regardless of the frame size. If you’re unsure on sizing, I’d advise scaling down. Test riding a Dreadnought if possible would be a smart move too, or at the very least swinging a leg over one in a parking lot. Specifications Specifications Release Date 2024 Price $9299 Travel X0 Eagle Transmission Rear Shock RockShox Vivid Air Ultimate Fork RockShox ZEB Ultimate 170mm, Charger 3.1 RC2 Headset Cane Creek Hellbender 70, ZS44/ZS56 Cassette X0 Eagle Transmission Crankarms X0 Eagle Transmission, 165, 32t Chainguide MRP custom Bottom Bracket SRAM DUB BSA 73mm Rear Derailleur X0 Eagle Transmission Chain X0 Eagle Transmission Flattop Chain Shifter Pods SRAM AXS Pod Ultimate Controller Handlebar Burgtec Ride Wide Enduro Carbon, 800x30mm Stem Burgtec MK3 42.5mm, 35mm Grips Burgtec Bartender Pro Greg Minnaar Signature Brakes SRAM Maven Ultimate, 200mm F/R, Organic pads Hubs Crankbrothers Synthesis Enduro Std Spokes Sapim CX Ray/Sprint Rim Crankbrothers Synthesis 11 Carbon Tires Maxxis Assegai 29x2.5" 3C MaxxGrip EXO+ F/Minion DHR II 27.5x2.4, 3C MaxxTerra DD Seat Fizik Alpaca X5, 145mm width Seatpost OneUp V2 Dropper, 31.6mm, 180mm Compare to other All Mountain/Enduro/XC RIDING THE Dreadnought Test Bike Setup In the Geometry section, I explained why I chose the S3 Dreadnought. I started with the stock MX wheel configuration but later switched to the -10mm dropouts (450mm chainstay). Since the bike already required a lot of input to change direction, I never went to the longest 470mm setting or experimented with a 29” rear wheel. The 30mm rise handlebars were cut to 765mm with 20mm of spacers under the stem. Regarding suspension setup, guidelines are clearly laid out on Forbidden’s website: set the rear shock sag to 28-30% sag while seated and turn the clickers as directed. On the first outing, the Vivid Air was pumped to 197 PSI to provide 30% sag. The shock arrives with 2 volume spacers from the factory. I’d go on to increase the pressure to 205 PSI, but eventually revert to 197 and add a third volume spacer. The HBO adjustment was kept fully closed. Matt Beer Location: Squamish, BC, Canada Age: 37 Height: 5'10" / 178 cm Weight: 170 lb / 77 kg Industry affiliations / sponsors: None Instagram: @mattb33r The digressive rebound tune (R85) was an interesting choice and meant I ran the red dial fully open. We’ll dive into those choices in the Technical Report below. On the compression side, I settled on placing the high and low speed compression dials at -3 and -2 clicks from closed, not far off of Forbidden’s recommendations (HSC -2, LSC -2). For the 170mm Zeb Ultimate, I ran the pressure lower than usual (around 78 vs. 84 PSI - both with 1 volume spacer) as it helped to keep more weight on the front of the long wheelbase through corners. Trails in the Dreadnought’s repertoire were a mix of fresh and old in the Diamondhead area in Squamish, as well as visits to the North Shore and Whistler Bike Park. Even a few high-speed laps on Harper Mountain in Kamloops were added to the mix to really let the Dreadnought open up. Download the Trailforks App Climbing During the Dreadnought’s First Ride, I immediately picked up on the increased drag from the lower pulley, which isn’t part of the Druid’s high-pivot equation. Extended climbs are where this is most prevalent, as well as in the largest cogs when the rear wheel is turning slower. It’s not terribly impeding compared to other bikes with idlers. In fact, it’s on par or better in terms of perceived efficiency, yet clear as day after riding other non-high-pivot bikes back to back. The stretched out geometry makes the footprint of the Dreadnought lengthy. The rear wheel takes some coaxing to pull around tight switchbacks. We’re not quite into Grim Donut V2 territory, but it’s a long ship to swing around 180-degree turns. Looking ahead and anticipating how wide you need to take those corners is mandatory. However, once understood and anticipated, that length aids its technical climbing ability. Worrying about the cranks bumping obstacles isn’t as much of a concern as I first thought. A high anti-squat value and 165mm cranks help the bike to maintain decent ground clearance and continue pedaling over lumpy ground. A central rider position and rearward axle path make blasting up rocky steps and over logs a breeze as the bike keeps moving forward. 27.5” rear wheels can hamper some enduro bikes’ uphill performance, but that’s where the Dreadnought differs. Although I didn’t try the larger wheel I can imagine how those characteristics would be exploited further, based on past experience. Descending Make no mistake, this isn’t just a long-travel Druid. They both share that same trait of seemingly offering more travel than stated, however, on our First Ride, some aspects of the bike blew me away. Once we towed the Dreadnought through more varied trails, some quirks started to show. Smashing high speed trails are where this beast comes to life. Point it straight and it will gobble up everything in sight. There is a catch though: repetitive square-edge bumps. Being a high-pivot bike, I expected the Dreadnought to erase those stutter bumps but it never gave that "wow"-facter like the Norco Range or Devinci Chainsaw did. Part of that reason may be down to the shock tune on the Vivid Air (R85 C26). Forbidden found a digressive rebound tune to handle the returning spring force best. The shock tune on the Dreadnought V2 is R85 C26, with 2 Volume Spacers. What that translates to is a digressive rebound tune and a light compression tune. This shock tune was chosen based on the changes made to the leverage ratio and the shape of the leverage curve. Compared to the V1 Dreadnought, the V2 has more support in the mid, less overall progression (25% vs 31%) and less end stroke progression (less ramp at the end of the curve). This works better with large volume air springs, like the Vivid. It also takes advantage of the hydraulic bottom out control seen on many shocks these days. Overall, this gives a more predictable feel to the bike throughout its travel. — Forbidden Bikes This reduces that kicking or bucking motion as the rear wheel returns from repetitive, medium-size hits. I can see why that makes sense in some instances. Some riders find high-pivot bikes can push their weight forward in those instances, particularly on jumps. I found the Dreadnought to avoid putting me in that unwanted situation and that could be where that shock tune comes into play. I also had the chance to briefly try a Fox DHX2 coil shock (digressive rebound/light compression tune) on the Dreadnought and found that to break into the travel and handle each consecutive impact more gently and consistently. On descents that best replicate mogul skiing, bouncing from one loamy lump to the next, the Dreadnought provides serious traction by splatting itself to the ground. This can make the bike more involved to turn, especially in steep zones where braking and changing direction needs to happen simultaneously (think Val Di Sole for enduro bikes). I wouldn’t say that this was an issue of the bike being too big for me, though it did require more consideration than most other bikes when it comes to how you weight the bike as it moves through the travel. The balance on each wheel can change considerably. Moving to the -10mm dropouts made a world of difference in regard to maneuverability. Back in the open, where sight lines are much further ahead, that elongating chainstay brings boat-loads of stability. The bike glues itself to the ground - not so much that you can’t pop over obstacles, flick a Scandi turn, or pull a manual. The Dreadnought encourages you to let it fly on high speed trails. As is the case with most high-pivot bikes, it’s a catch twenty-two situation. They can light up in certain scenarios and struggle more than say a Horst link suspended bike. Thankfully, the Dreadnought has enough parameters that could be changed to suit those devoted to the ways of a high-pivot bike. Remember, there’s still two wheel size options, three dropout settings and the possibility to run a coil shock too. Downsizing to a smaller frame and opting for a coil shock would be something to consider, based on how tall the rider is and the terrain they most frequently ride. Technical Report RockShock Vivid Air rear shock: We've been impressed with the Vivid's performance on other bikes but we had some hiccups with one particular shock when the IFP lost pressure. On top of that, the suppleness never seemed to be equal to the Vivid we tested on a Nomad during our Shock Week last summer . Although our time with a Fox DHX2 on the Dreadnought was short, a coil shock would be our preference for the Dreadnought. SRAM Maven Ultimate brakes: It’s been a long time since we saw prototypes of SRAM’s comically large prototype brake calipers, but I’m glad they’re here. Maven Ultimates are a major improvement over the Code RSCs in terms of power, especially when you're on the brakes for considerable lengths of time. The Dreadnought came with organic pads and 200mm front and rear. These give much less bite than the metallic option yet don't fade out as quickly as they would on a Code. MRP chainguide: Another component that I thought may interfere with the small bump sensitivity was the added tension from the lower pulley. I removed that for a few rides and didn’t notice a major improvement - just more noise from the chain. In actuality, the derailleur clutch could be a bigger culprit as it flaps around unpredictably giving inconsistencies throughout the travel. It's no elbow to MRP because the pulley rolls smoothly, but it's still an extra component that isn't used on most other suspension designs. Which Model is the Best Value? If you’re seriously looking at a Dreadnought, then expect to drop a bit of coin. Carbon-only frames do push the entry point higher than competing aluminum enduro bikes. The cream of the crop X0 RS U build doesn’t cut corners but it's the RS S base model that seems the most sensible. It largely has the same components as the RS S+, including the GX AXS T-type drivetrain. The main differences are the damper used in the ZEB chassis (Charger 3 vs. the older RC) and adjustments on the Vivid Air. Other than that, I wouldn’t worry about the Maven Bronze brakes as they are still plenty powerful and have a crispy feel. Upgrading the fork damper to the Charger 3.1 and replacing the rims with something burlier could be considerations down the road. Forbidden Dreadnought V2 Santa Cruz Nomad How Does It Compare? A hot competitor in the world of smashy enduro bikes is the Santa Cruz Nomad. No, it’s not a high-pivot, but for riders looking to lap bike parks, send it off of ridiculous things, and race enduros, these two bikes are built to tackle such endeavors. Neither one would ever win an XC race, however, they are designed to pedal everywhere on the map. The Dreadnought actually wins out in climbing effectiveness by riding a touch higher in the travel and setting the rider in a more upright, centered position on the bike. Efficiency-wise though, as in just spinning up a road, the Nomad’s traditional drivetrain is less draggy over the course of a full day. Point them downhill though, and you can get wild. The Dreadnought is 10mm shorter on travel out back but makes up for that in some sense with the rearward axle path. Both bikes have a noticeably high amount of tension from the drivetrain as the rear wheel breaks into the travel. The shock tune on the Dreadnought could be the culprit but a device like the O-Chain Active Spider remedies a lot of the clanging on the Nomad. At the end of the stroke, the Nomad handles bottom outs best from a more consistent ramp up. Each frame is stiff with aggressive geometry that can be ridden hard by riders at the top end of the sport. At the same time, the Nomad doesn’t need to be pushed to go fast or muscled as hard to turn. For that reason, it’s less demanding to ride than the Dreadnought. As for maintenance, the Dreadnought doesn’t have as many bolts as something like Commencal’s Supreme V5 downhill bike but the Nomad is hands down simpler. There’s no pulley wheels to keep running smooth and the links have grease ports to quickly remedy any creaks. Santa Cruz also offers a lifetime supply of replacement frame bearings for the original customer too. When it’s time to slap down the credit card, neither one is cheap, or available in aluminum. A Nomad frame and shock (Fox Float X2) costs $4,049 USD, $250 less than a Dreadnought frame kit. Looking at the premium build kits, they’re very comparable with Forbidden choosing RockShox Ultimate suspension and Santa Cruz going the Fox Factory route. There’s also carbon rims, an X0 T-type drivetrain, and Maven brakes, stalemating at a cool $9,299 USD. Pros + Calm and quiet ride. + Centered, upright seated position gives a helping hand on technical climbs. + Comes alive at higher speeds. Cons - Not the small bump performance expected from a high-pivot bike. - Long chainstays that continue to grow through the travel can be a handful on steep trails. - Digressive shock rebound tune has a peculiar ride characteristic. Pinkbike's Take After being blown away with the capabilities of Forbidden's 130mm-travel Druid, I thought the Dreadnought would be all that and more, but it didn't eat bumps like the magic carpet ride I dreamt about. Convincing this beast to turn means being forceful in where you point the bike and when to lean it in. With that said though, when you get the Dreadnought on high speed trails and optimize the shock setup, there aren't many other enduro bikes that will unlock the same level of speed. — Matt Beer Visit the high-res gallery for more images from this review. Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Forbidden Forbidden Dreadnought Author Info: Matt Beer Squamish, BC, Canada Member since Mar 16, 2001 607 articles Matt has been a Pinkbike reader since the site's early days. He discovered mountain biking in Newfoundland, where a small community of dedicated riders and trail builders set him on a path to earn two Canadian National Downhill Championship titles. Now based in Squamish, BC, Matt works as a tech editor for Pinkbike, specializing in gravity-focused mountain bike reviews, tech, and racing coverage.

title: Review: Formula Cura X Brakes - 2 Piston Consistency
author: Mike Kazimer
article_text: Cura X review https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/Izne09JS Copied Loading ad Live 00:25 00:00 08:17 Formula's two-piston Cura X brakes were launched a little over two years ago, positioned as the hopped-up version of the Cura brakes that have been in the Italian company's lineup since 2017. The brakes are designed for cross-country and trail bikes, although as you'll see in a bit, they can be used for an even wider range of riding styles, even if they don't have four pistons and calipers that weigh as much as a softball. The X in the model name indicates that the brakes received a carbon fiber lever, titanium bolts, and a braided Kevlar hose. Those finishing touches save around 34 grams per brake compared to the standard Cura, and add $100 to the price. Formula Cura X Details • 2 pistons • Brake fluid: mineral oil • Carbon lever blade, titanium hardware • Kevlar reinforced hose • Weight: 218 grams (front lever, caliper, hose, pads) • MSRP: $250 USD per wheel • rideformula.com Details The Cura X brakes run on mineral oil, which is a little friendlier to work with than DOT fluid. You oviously shouldn't be drinking the stuff or dumping it in your eyes, but it's less likely to eat away at paint or irritate exposed skin. That fluid pushes on two 24 millimeter pistons (for comparison, the piston diameter in the 4-piston version of these brakes is 18 millimeters) that are housed in the forged aluminum caliper. One design feature that could create a small hurdle is the lack of a banjo fitting on the caliper. That means the angle of the hose is fixed; there's no way to rotate it to give the hose a better path into a frame, at least not without purchasing an adaptor and a different hose. I didn't run into any issues on the two bikes I tested the brakes on, but it is something to keep in mind. Moving onto the levers, those use a split clamp design, with different 'MixMaster' back plates available to allow them to be paired with SRAM or Shimano shifters. The carbon lever blade itself is fairly long, especially compared to the length of Shimano's levers – the size and shape are similar to the dimensions of TRP's brake levers. The lever reach is adjusted by turning a small protruding dial, and I was able to make that adjustment without any tools. One some of Formula's brakes that dial can be very hard to turn, but with these ones I'd say it was only medium hard. Installation Getting the Cura X brakes up and running was about as easy as it gets. The Formula bleed kit (sold separately) includes two syringes with fittings that thread into the caliper and the lever. If you're familiar with the bleed procedure for SRAM's brakes, it's essentially the same with Formula. Fluid is pushed from the caliper and through the lever until there aren't any bubbles visible, and then a couple of final steps help ensure the last stubborn bubbles are extracted from the caliper and lever. On the Trail Formula supplied the brakes with an assortment of rotors, brake mounts, and pad compounds, which made it possible to try multiple setups and adapt them to two very different bikes. I started by installing the brakes on a Raaw Madonna , partially because that bike has external cable routing, and also because I was curious if these two piston brakes could supply enough power to adequately keep things under control in the steeps. On the Raaw I ran a 220mm front rotor and a 200mm rear with metallic pads in order to eke out the most power possible. With those big rotors and metallic pads the brakes were strong enough to handle sustained steep maneuvers without any major issues. They don't have the massive power of something like the SRAM Mavens, and they do require a firmer pull on the lever to really slow things down in a hurry, but they worked well enough that I didn't feel like I'd brought a knife to a gun fight. The feel at the lever is similar to how TRP's brakes feel - the initial pull of the lever is light, with a very positive sensation once the pads contact the rotor. This wasn't entirely surprising; when the original Cura brakes hit the market Finn Iles was racing World Cup downhill with them on his DH bike. Times have changed, though, and we've seen the power level of brakes increase in recent years; realistically, an enduro bike isn't the ideal place for these brakes, but it was impressive to see that they could hold their own in that scenario. After the Raaw I put them on a Specialized Chisel FS , running a 160mm rear rotor and a 180mm up front, where they replaced a set of Code RSC brakes that I'd been running with the same rotor sizes. The difference between those two brakes comes down to lever feel rather than a dramatic perceivable power difference, even though the Codes have four pistons versus the two in the Cura X. SRAM's Level brakes would be the more typical matchup, and in that case the Curas handily win. The Levels simply don't have enough power for anything other than mellower trail riding. I do slightly prefer the lever shape of the Codes (or Levels) vs the Curas – they have more of a curve to them, and cradle my finger a little bit better. That's a small nitpick; Curas do have a lighter initial action, and I was able to achieve a comfortable setup without much fuss, and without any pad contact adjustment. How do they compare to Shimano's two piston brakes? I've had good luck with the two-piston XT and XTR brakes in the past, and I can't declare a clear winner in that match-up. The Cura's don't feel quite as on / off as the Shimano's, and the maximum power occurs a little later in the lever stroke, but the difference isn't overly dramatic. Pad availability is typically better for Shimano, but Formula is a large enough company that sourcing replacements shouldn't be too difficult. Weight & Price Shimano XTR two piston: 210 grams / $325 USD Formula Cura X Weight: 218 grams / $250 SRAM Level: 245 grams / $300 (4 piston is 10 g lighter than 2 piston) Formula Cura: 252 grams / $150 Hayes Dominion T2: 254 grams / $290 Shimano XT 2-piston brakes: 294 grams, $170 Pros + Very consistent performance, no wandering bite point + Plenty of power for most applications + Easy to bleed Cons - Weight savings is fairly minimal for the extra price compared to Cura - Lack of banjo fitting at the caliper could be an issue with some frames Pinkbike's Take The Formula Cura X brakes are great, full stop. (Pun intended). They're easy to set up, deliver plenty of power for all but the biggest bikes, and have been trouble-free over months of riding. — Mike Kazimer Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Brakes Formula Formula Cura X Author Info: Mike Kazimer Bellingham, WA, United States Member since Feb 1, 2009 1,975 articles Based in Bellingham, Washington, Mike Kazimer is Editor in Chief at Pinkbike, where he’s been covering all things mountain bike related for the last 14 years. Before joining Pinkbike, he worked in bike and ski shops for over a decade. Between all the biking and skiing he managed to pick up an English degree from Western Colorado University.

title: Review: Push SV Eight Coil Shock
author: Mike Kazimer
article_text: Push released the SV Eight shock earlier this year, a made-in-Colorado offering that comes in at a slightly lower price point than their flagship ElevenSix shock. While the ElevenSix has two separate damping circuits, essentially allowing riders to toggle between two completely different setups with the flick of a lever, the SV Eight has a more standard layout, with just one circuit. It's still highly adjustable, though, and it has a hydraulic bottom out feature that kicks in for the last 15% of the travel. The SV Eight has 24 clicks of high speed and low speed compression damping, along with 18 clicks of rebound. Push uses US-made Hyperco springs for their shocks, which are available in 25 pound increments. Other coil springs are compatible too, as long as they have a 35mm internal diameter. Push SV Eight Details • Adjustments: low speed compression, high speed compression, rebound • Melonite QPQ high strength steel shaft • Hydraulic bottom out piston • eMTB compatible • Weight: 423 g (without spring, 205 x 65mm) • MSRP: $865 without spring • pushindustries.com Rather than being available in a wide range of tune options, the $865 (without spring) SV Eight only comes in two configurations – an A version designed for riders using spring weights between 300 – 500 lb/in, and a B version for spring weights between 525 – 700 lb/in. That's a different approach compared to what Push does with the Elevensix, their $1,600 (with spring) flagship model – that shock is tuned specifically for the bike it's going to be installed on. That said, if a rider does need a different setup than what's possible with the A or B tune, Push offers a custom tuning option for $170. Other Details Hydraulic bottom out: The SV Eight's hydraulic bottom out system kicks in for the last 15% of the shock's stroke, assuming the stroke length hasn't been limited. A secondary piston sits on top of the main piston, where it's pushed into a smaller chamber as the end of the shock's travel is reached. That constriction in the oil path increased the amount of force required to compress the shock, preventing a harsh bottom out. Push do still use a standard sized bottom out bumper, so there are essentially two systems in play to eliminate any jarring impacts. And yes, if you shorten the shock's stroke the bottom out resistance will happen later in the shock's travel; a 62.5mm stroke shock would have that hydraulic bottom out resistance kick in for the last 11% of travel. Unlike option from RockShox and EXT, Push's hydraulic bottom out feature isn't externally adjustable - the amount of force is fixed from the factory. Warranty: Each SV Eight comes with a 1-year warranty, which is extended by 1 year each time the shock is sent in for its annual service. That service is priced at $190, and includes all new oil seals, wiper seals, and o-rings, along with new fluids. The dials are easy to turn, and have clearly defined clicks. Caption Settings Dialog Beginning of dialog window. Escape will cancel and close the window. Text Color White Black Red Green Blue Yellow Magenta Cyan Transparency Opaque Semi-Transparent Background Color Black White Red Green Blue Yellow Magenta Cyan Transparency Opaque Semi-Transparent Transparent Window Color Black White Red Green Blue Yellow Magenta Cyan Transparency Transparent Semi-Transparent Opaque Font Size 50% 75% 100% 125% 150% 175% 200% 300% 400% Text Edge Style None Raised Depressed Uniform Dropshadow Font Family Proportional Sans-Serif Monospace Sans-Serif Proportional Serif Monospace Serif Casual Script Small Caps Defaults Done SV Eight ASMR by mikekazimer Add to Favorites Added to Favorites Views: 1,968 Faves: 0 Comments: 1 Performance The SV Eight was installed on my Raaw Madonna, which has been an excellent test bed for evaluating various shocks. A 475 lb spring put me at the recommended 26% sag. As per Push's instructions, I started off with the dials close to the middle of the range, with 12 clicks of LSC, 12 clicks of HSC, and 9 clicks of rebound, all counted from closed. Those adjustments don't require any tools, and each click is very well defined. A good chunk of my testing took place in the Whistler Bike Park, where I was able to take dozens of runs and experiment with the extremes of the compression range. Even with the low speed and high speed compression fully closed the SV Eight was still rideable – it's obviously a much firmer ride, and not something I'd want to run all day, but it also wasn't overly harsh or jarring. The same goes for the fully open settings – the shock was too active for my taste in that configuration, with more movement and less support than I wanted, but once again it wasn't so extreme as to be unusable. In the end, I only deviated a couple clicks from Push's recommended starting point – I sped up the rebound by a click, and opened up the low speed a few clicks to get a little more traction in the wet conditions. There's no climb switch on the SV Eight, but Push claim that the shock has pedaling efficiency built in thanks to a bridged piston design. That may be so, but I didn't notice much of a platform while climbing compared to other shocks. The Raaw has fairly calm pedaling manners, so I didn't miss having a climb switch, but I do think that's a feature some riders will still want, especially those on bikes with less efficient suspension layouts. Up to two 2.5mm spacers can be added underneath the bottom out bumper to reduce the shock's stroke. The SV Eight has an excellent ground feel, meaning it smooths out impacts very well, but not so much that you lose track of what the rear wheel is doing. It delivers all the grip and small bump compliance you'd hope to get from a coil shock, while also remaining supportive enough for bigger jumps and drops. Those laps at Whistler helped illustrate its well balanced nature – I was able to go from the chunkier, more natural trails like Whistler DH to the freshly spruced up A-Line without feeling like I needed to adjust any settings. It's the high level of traction the SV Eight delivers that impressed me the most – it's very, very supple, quickly getting up and out of the way of even the smallest obstacles, a trait that's even more useful on wet, slimy days where you want your rear wheel hugging the ground as much as possible. The hydraulic bottom out does its job, and I didn't experience and harsh clunks or clangs when full travel was reached. Of course, since there's also a bottom out bumper it's hard to suss out exactly which feature is coming into play, and I should also mention that I didn't have any harsh bottom out events when using the Ohlins TTX22, which doesn't have that feature. There is a small amount of top out, but that was only noticeable when bunnyhopping or jumping at slower speeds; in those instances I could feel a slight 'twunk' as the shock reach full travel, but it was fairly subtle. In the bike park that sensation wasn't noticeable at all. Push SV Eight Ohlins TTX22 How Does It Compare? The SV Eight had a less damped feel than the Ohlins TTX22 I'd been on before; the difference is similar to that of wearing a thicker, insulated glove (Ohlins), compared to a thin silk glove (Push). Both shocks accomplish the same goal, but go about it in a slightly different way. In back-to-back testing, I ended up preferring the feel of the Push; it seemed to provide more traction in wet, slippery conditions, even at slower speeds. Keep in mind that both shocks can be re-tuned to suit a rider's specific needs - a lighter compression tune on the TTX22 could make it feel more like the SV Eight, and vice versa, but I will say that, overall, the TTX22 does tend to feel more damped than its competition. The Ohlins is a top tier shock, and really comes alive as speeds increase and the impacts get harder, but it does have a more heavily damped feel, even in its most open setting. The TTX22 does have a three position HSC lever that can be used as a climb switch, something the Push is lacking. As for price, the SV Eight is $40 more than the Ohlins, at $865 vs $825. For further comparison, a RockShox SuperDeluxe Coil is $550, and a Fox DHX2 is $679. All of those prices are without a spring, so don't forget to factor that into your equation. Pros + Good range of usable adjustments + Great traction and overall feel + Made in USA Cons - Hydraulic bottom out isn't adjustable - Pricey (but it's a lot less than an ElevenSix) - Some riders may want a climb switch Pinkbike's Take I'd be curious to see what the introduction of the SV Eight does to the sales of Push's even-fancier ElevenSix shock. Personally, if I was deciding between the two I'd go with the SV Eight. The dual valve chamber system of the ElevenSix is novel, but I don't think it's all that necessary, especially considering just how well the SV Eight works for hundreds of dollars less. At the end of the day, the SV Eight is a high end shock with performance that goes a long way towards justifying its price – its overall feel left me thoroughly impressed. — Mike Kazimer Visit the high-res gallery for more images. title: Review: Saris' New Edge 2" Hitch Rack
author: Dario DiGiulio
article_text: SarisEdge https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/FT3qWM6K Copied Loading ad Live 00:25 00:00 07:15 Bike racks come in all shapes and sizes, but a common variety seems to be emerging amongst the mountain bike community. The fold up tray rack is pretty ubiquitous, with versions from most brands out there, both small and large alike. Saris already has one offering in this realm - the modular MHS system - but they're adding a second option with the Edge. This rack is fairly simple, with one arm holding the front wheel and a strap securing the rear, but there are some little details thrown in to try to set it apart. Edge Details • 80lb weight limit per tray • Fits wheels 24” to 29” x 3” or 27.5” x 5” • No frame or fender contact • Built-in cable lock • RV rated, 2" hitch • Weight: 67lbs / 30.4kg • Price: $1000 USD • saris.com The Edge has a very high load rating per tray, and adds compatibility with fatbikes and smaller-wheeled bikes to the mix. The 80 pound limit is well in excess of what most folks will need, but consider it extra assurance should you hit a nasty bump in the road with your 60-pound eMTB on the rack. Fits long bikes. The telescoping arm has some tricks up its sleeve, with a spring-loaded design that keeps things tight, and a built in detent to make sure the hook doesn't interfere with fenders, forks, or brake lines. The spring function keeps the arm tight to the tire automatically, and all you have to do is give it a solid push to secure things for the drive. For the rear wheel, things are more typical, but there's one added detail to improve the standard ratchet design. In addition to the very long ratchet strap, there's a sliding block that allows you to raise the rear tire by 2.5", for better clearance should your saddle interfere with the other bike's handlebars. In Use Loading / Unloading The loading process is the same as with any other one-arm rack, but things are made a bit easier by the spring loaded hook and limited range of motion on the arm's pivot. You can't over-clock the arm, it just lands in the same spot every time, right in front of a long fender. The spring helps keep things snug, and is extra handy when unloading the bike and dropping the arm down - the hook quickly returns to the shortest position out of the way. The built-in lock is a nice touch. Not the most secure, but good in a pinch. I find ratchet straps to be a bit annoying to use, as you have to thread them through the spokes every time you load the bike, making sure to not trap them down with the rear wheel when loading things. Unloading is fairly easy, as you just depress the plastic catch and yank the strap out. I haven't had too many occasions to use the rear wheel spacer on the Edge, so mostly it has remained off to the side as more of a tire chock. If you should need it, the block slides around pretty easily with two hands, and stays securely in position once ratcheted down. On and Off Road This is a Honda Fit folks, don't get too excited. The biggest hinderance to off road usage on the Edge is the lack of clearance, as the rack does nothing to lift the bikes above the hitch receiver. For taller vehicles, this might not be an issue, but every car out there benefits from more clearance out back, even if just for steep driveways. The anti-rattle tightening feature works well here, and keeps the rack securely in place in the hitch, without any loosening through the test period. I find two-arm racks to be more secure in true off-road situations, as the extra rigidity that comes from the second set of arms helps reduce how much the bikes can wag. The Edge lets the bikes flop a fair bit, but hasn't done anything scary in my time with it. Part of this extra movement is probably due to the extra tire clearance in the front wheel basket, where there's a fair bit of clearance for the tire to rotate without any support on the sides. When the rack is stowed upright in no-bike mode, there is a noticeable rattle between the rack and the main pivot point, as the body rocks back and forth in the detent. This probably won't be apparent on RVs and off the back of massive vehicles, but it was hard to ignore when driving around town in my smaller car. Stowing the rack in the 3/4 position mostly alleviates this, but that's not the cleanest solution if you're looking to keep the rack folded tight to the back of your car. Easy one handed deploy, usually a two hand stow. What Could Be Improved? The aforementioned rattle is an annoyance, but could be less of an issue for some users depending on the vehicle type. My bigger complaints come down to the finer details of the rack, and the potential durability issues that might come up. The overall chassis is sturdy, with a massive pivot point and beefy trays, but the arms have some play in them that is sure to develop further over time, as pretty much all telescoping hook armed racks are wont to do. The ratchet hardware is already showing some rust, and though it hasn't affected things in use yet, it might do so after a few more months of road grime immersion. I've noticed that after heavy rains the rack drains water for a while afterwards, meaning quite a bit can pool up in the trays if the rack stays on your car. It's possible that won't amount to much in the long term, but only time will tell. My last frustration is with the ratchet straps, namely the damage they can do to the rear rim of the bike. As your bike shimmies back and forth in the rack, the tightened ratchet strap constantly rubs on the location where installed, leading to noticeable scratching of the rim, especially in wet weather or when the bike is dirty. Some ratchet designs employ a slide-on boot to prevent this, but the Edge doesn't include that preventative measure. Plus, those boots are easy to lose as you use the rack, making them something of a half measure. Durability The Edge rack has been living on my car for the past two months, and aside from the concerns listed above, things haven't changed a bit. That's far from a long term test in the world of bike racks though, and time will tell how well the Saris survives. Saris offers a limited lifetime warranty, mostly against any defects in manufacturing, excluding rust, paint damage, and wear and tear. Pros + One hand opening, slick arm design + High load rating, RV approved + No frame contact + Holds large tires Cons - Cost is high for middling quality - Ratchet straps are fussy and can scratch wheels - Rattles when stowed upright Pinkbike's Take The Saris Edge packs a bunch of features into a fairly sleek package, but lacks some refinement when it comes to finish quality and elegance. The design of the front arm makes loading quick and easy, and easily avoids frame contact, but the ratchet strap is a little fussier and can scratch rims. Though it wouldn't be my top choice for a regular car, folks with extremely heavy e-bikes and an RV might find it ideal. — Dario DiGiulio Visit the high-res gallery for more images. Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Bikeracks Saris Saris Edge Author Info: Dario DiGiulio Bellingham, WA, United States Member since Dec 25, 2016 484 articles Dario has been obsessed with bikes from an early age, and has worked in the industry since 2015. With a Bachelor's in Architecture from Cal Poly, San Luis Obispo, he aims to bring that mix of creativity and technicality to his work in the mountain bike world.

title: Review: Shimano GF800 GTX Flat Pedal Shoes - Grippy & Ready for the Rain
author: Matt Beer
article_text: The GF8GTX has all of the features of a premium wet weather flat pedal shoe: sticky soles, Boa closure, ankle gaiter, and Gore-Tex. There aren't many similarities between derailleurs and shoes, but that hasn’t stopped Shimano from producing quality products in both categories for decades. One of their staples has been the MW5, a waterproof clipless shoe. Until recently, an equivalent flat pedal version has been hard to come by. Shimano has answered with the GF800 GTX, delivering all the bells and whistles needed in a waterproof flat shoe. It features a Gore-Tex shield throughout, an ankle gaiter to keep debris out, a Boa dial closure, and a specially formulated rubber sole. GF800 GTX Details • Boa dial closure • Gore-Tex gaiter ankle cuff • Ultread-GR • Added toe protection • Sizes: 38-48 • Weight: 433 grams (per shoe, size 42) • MSRP: $220 USD • shimano.com Shimano has worked diligently to ensure that the Ultread GF rubber compound remains flexible and grippy in cold weather when those materials tend to firm up. No laces to get clogged with mud. This Ultread rubber was designed to remain soft in colder weather. The sole is more flexible compared to previous Shimano flat pedal shoes I've tried. Price and Weight The GF800 GTX is Shimano’s exclusive waterproof flat pedal shoe in the lineup, and it doesn't come at an insignificant cost - $220 USD to be exact. But who can put a price on dry feet? A single shoe weighs 433g in size 42 or M8.5. Shimano includes a one-year warranty and free shipping on orders over $100. There’s also a free return and exchange program offered for that price. You also get the option of a fully black shoe or one with a splash of olive color. When lined up against a direct competitor such as the Five Ten Trailcross Gore-Tex, the Shimanos cost $20 more and weigh slightly more. They’re certainly not heavy, though, and I’d say the price is in the ballpark for a shoe with these features. The ankle gaiter has an elastic cuff to keep debris out but this soft material isn't exactly "waterproof". Overall, the shoe is not cumbersome as you might expect for a wet weather shoe, however, the toe has a stiffer rubber cap for protection. Fit The GF8GTX only comes in full sizes from 38 to 48 - no half sizes or wide options exist in this model. Shimano does add a note stating that if you previously used the ME line of shoes, size down.
