The suspension feels relatively firm off the top. I wouldn't go so far as to call it harsh, but on the whole the bike does have a certain firmness to it. For context, I have ridden more supple-feeling 120mm travel bikes. To begin with, I ran the rear suspension at 25% sag with high and low-speed compression wound to their open positions. On some bigger compressions and small hucks I was blowing through the travel and coming up against a hard bottom-out. I remedied that with an extra volume spacer, and a few clicks of HSC. This made the rear considerably more resistant to bottom-out, and considerably more supportive through the mid-stroke. With these settings, the bike came alive. It was a joy to ride on fast, flowing singletrack, and felt utterly wonderful to push and pump through berms and rollers. The rear end tracks very well in most situations, but under braking the suspension noticeably stiffens up. It caught me off guard on the first few rides, but I soon adapted, taking care to pick my braking zones effectively. It's not always possible of course, but when the opportunities arose, I felt it beneficial to brake before an array of braking bumps, and to roll through them off the brakes. It's not hard work to enjoy riding the Process 134, but it will reward an aggressive and precise riding style. Its firm ride feel and quick handling through turns can make you feel pinned on smooth bike park terrain, but it's easy to come undone when carrying that speed into looser, steeper terrain where a little more suppleness on- and off the brakes would make things a little more comfortable and predictable. The $5,499 USD Kona Process 134 CRDL - sale price $4,499 The $6,999 USD Pivot Trailcat LT Ride GX Eagle Transmission How Does It Compare? The recently-launched Pivot Trailcat LT has many similarities with the Kona Process 134, being MX- and 29" capable with a carbon frame and the same quantity of rear wheel travel. They deliver that travel through very different suspension layouts, but otherwise the two platforms share similar intentions. While the Pivot is considerably lighter (claimed weight of 13 kg in a medium, XTR), the $4,499 USD asking price for the Kona comes in well under the price of Pivot's most affordable Trailcat LT model. I won't do an in-depth analysis here, but know that you are getting a lot more for your money with the components on the Kona, as compared to the Trailcat LT. There are two sides to every coin, though; while the Pivot is sold with a lifetime warranty, Kona sell the Process 134 CRDL with a three-year warranty. The Process 134 is considerably more capable in my hands, up and down the hill, than the Pivot Shadowcat , a 27.5" wheel 140mm trail bike, despite that bike being equipped with a 160mm travel fork - versus the Kona's 140mm affair. On the flip-side, I recently rode a 120mm travel Santa Cruz Tallboy which, despite its XC bracketed travel numbers and 29" wheelset, takes the top spot in my mind as the most capable, playful bike of those mentioned here. The most relevant performance comparison comes with the Pivot Trailcat LT that I have in for review just now; it is the more capable of the two bikes, especially on steeper trickier trails and would therefore be my top choice if money were no object. Which Model is the Best Value? Of the four Kona Process 134 models, the most expensive $4,499 USD CR DL one that I tested will be the lightest build. However, dependent on exactly what you value, the one may not be the best option for you. One rung down the ladder, you can pay $3,199 USD for the 134 CR. It has the same carbon frame as the CR DL, but a subtly different build spec. It switches out the Rockshox Reverb for a TranzX RAD+ dropper (the more adjustable and reliable post, in my experience). Process 134 CR - $3,199 USD It also switches out the SRAM G2 RSC brakes for the G2 R brakes. They have the same 4-piston caliper and lever architecture, and thus the same capacity for stopping power, but they lack the bite point adjustment. Sure, it is a nice to have feature, and can help reduce intervals between bleeding. The other major differences lie in the suspension components and drivetrain. While the CRDL runs the previous generation upper-end suspension components from Rockshox, the CR model gets mid-range options from Fox. The CRDL has an electronic T-Type derailleur, while the CR model runs the SRAM GX mechanical option. The two bikes roll on the same Maxxis tires on the same WTB KOM Team i30 rims, but the CR model uses DT 370 hubs, as compared to the CRDL model's faster-engagement DT 350 hubs. If I was committed to one of the carbon models, I'd have to go for the more affordable CR model given it is a whopping $1,300 USD price difference. Process 134 DL - $2,399 Process 134 - $1,599 If carbon gives you the boke and you don't mind a weight penalty then you have two aluminum models to choose from, though both are equipped with more budget-oriented componentry. Generally, you have to look to more boutique brands if you want to find top-level componentry on an aluminum frame. Both models are going to be considerably heavier than the 14 kg CRDL model tested here, and the cable routing is completely external, but importantly they are sold with a lifetime warranty. It has to be said, $1,599 USD for a complete bike, sold with a lifetime warranty from a well-established brand is undeniably excellent value in 2025 - cheaper than some frame only options from other brands. You'll want to swap out its SRAM Level two-piston brakes almost immediately, but you'll have plenty of cash to spare for a quality upgrade. Technical Report SRAM G2 RSC Brakes: These brakes are reasonably powerful. With the 200mm Centerline rotors I was never left wanting, but my heavier colleagues think these should be thrown in the trash and replaced with the more powerful SRAM Code, or something with a similar amount of power. RockShox Reverb Dropper Seat Post: Early on the post developed undesirable sag. In its top-out position, its sags by around 20mm under my weight. Similarly, in its low position, it extends up by around 20mm, reducing clearance. This is a common issue with the mechanical Reverb, but is very quickly rectified with the Rockshox Vent Valve Tool that is supplied with the bike. Maxxis Dissector: On the few wetter rides, I felt a notable lack of bite from the Maxxis Dissector rear tire on soft climbs, with a similar notable lack of braking traction. Its tread pattern is relatively shallow and the centre tread blocks don't provide much modulation in that regard, tending to lock up with little encouragement. Kona Key Grip: The rubber compound is very firm and uncomfortable on long descents. I also disliked the raised bump at the end of the grip, as it prevents me from comfortably running my hands at the end of the bar. FSA Headset: All sizes of the Process 134 get this Orbit 1.5 E ZS headset with a 20mm top cap that raises the minimum stack height unnecessarily. Pros + Loves a flow trail + Quiet + More affordable than many of its direct competitors Cons - Seat tube likely to feel very slack for taller riders - On the heavier end of the spectrum for a carbon trail bike - Not the most proficient climber - Spec is a little outdated, likely owing to Kona's sale and inventory issues Pinkbike's Take The Kona Process 134 didn't blow me away in any department, but that's not to say I didn't have a good time riding it. Its slack seat tube angle and spec from a bygone era hold it back from greatness, and it left me wanting more predictability and comfort on rougher descents, but it was a fine companion for flattering, flowing singletrack. The value is great though, making this a solid option for those watching their pennies. — Jessie-May Morgan Visit the high-res gallery for more images from this review. Photos: Pete Scullion Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Trail Bikes Kona Kona Process 134 Author Info: Jessie-May Morgan Pitfichie, Scotland Member since Oct 26, 2023 402 articles Jessie-May has been working as a mountain bike technical editor since 2018, joining Pinkbike's editorial team in 2023. In a previous life she worked in bioscience research in Edinburgh where she used microscopes to measure things about the immune system. She likes all bikes, but enduro bikes have historically been the preference. Jessie-May has made up the numbers at a handful of World Enduro races, though she is now in her 'downcountry era', and has an unhealthy obsession with handlebars.

title: First Ride: Auckland Cycle Works Marra Prototype with KOLARP Suspension
author: Jessie-May Morgan
article_text: The Auckland Cycle Works Marra is a dual short-link enduro bike created by bike shop owner, Gary Ewing. The unusual linkage layout arises from an atypical, back-to-basics approach to bike design; rather than making use of any computer aided design, Gary chooses instead to crack out the Lego to visualize and model linkage layouts. That can be an incredibly useful exercise, at least in gaining some level of understanding as to how different linkages deliver different axle paths; Gary's video of this process is worth a view, if only to grasp just how aggressive the rearward axle path of his KOLARP suspension design is. However, it's worth pointing out that, at least to my knowledge, no other modern day suspension bike on the market has been designed in this way, i.e. without the use of a computer and the precision that comes with conventional suspension analysis tools. Marra V2 Details • Steel frame • 165mm travel, 180mm fork • Mixed wheels • Size tested: 451mm reach • Weight: 38 lb / 17.28 kg (w/o pedals) • Head angle: 64° • Seat tube angle: 76° • BB height (unsagged): 350mm • Rear-center length (unsagged): 416mm • Rear-center length (sagged): 441.5mm • Rear-center length at bottom-out: 476mm • Price: £5,500 (frame only, with custom geometry) • More info: aucklandcycle.works The Marra V2 The Reiver Outcomes of Gary's design process were on display at Bespoked in Dresden; the Marra and the Reiver are nothing if not head turners. Both run versions of the Kind of Like a Rearward Pivot URT design, though the Reiver is slightly more out there, given that it houses the BB on a moving link. The Marra is now available to pre-order for the lofty price of £5,500 for the frame. Tested here is the V2 prototype. Testing of prototypes isn't something we make a habit of, but it's nice to make an exception every once in a while. Also, having followed Gary's development story over the last few years, and upon learning that he doesn't know how he could possibly make the KOLARP suspension design any better than it already is, the temptation to give it a shot was all too much. Frame Details & Suspension Design The Marra V2 prototype is constructed from steel tubing, welded to Gary's specifications by Coal Bikes owner, Gavin White. Its suspension takes the form of a short link four-bar layout, with an idler pulley mounted directly to the lower link, such that its position relative to the chainring moves as the rear wheel is displaced. This does indeed fall under the scope of the I-Track patent. Sequence 04 https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/DoDpiQEc Copied Live 00:00 00:38 00:38 Through compression, the lower link rotates counter-clockwise for most of the travel, until it stops at 147mm and begins to rotate clockwise until bottom-out. The upper link rotates counter-clockwise throughout compression, driving the shock via an extender, which is unusually long in the context of the 65mm stroke shock that it's mounted to. The resulting axle path is aggressively rearward; unsagged, the rear-center is 416mm (as tested), extending to 441.5mm at sag, and 476mm at bottom-out. It's not 100% rearward; the inflection point comes at around 113mm travel, at which point the rear axle has moved rearwards along the horizontal by a substantial 32mm from the unloaded state - that's a bigger increase than we see on some of the highest single pivot enduro bikes, like the Forbidden Dreadnought , but not quite as much as the 45mm rearward movement exhibited by the wild-looking Aper KOMPace . There are two points of adjustment on this frame. Firstly, the rear-center length can be adjusted through 30mm, in 5mm increments, by virtue of the sliding dropout - Gary's unique version of which is T-Type compatible. That's a pretty generous range of adjustment. Lengthening the stay does of course increase the axle's leverage over the shock. Going from the shortest position to the longest position, the increase in leverage is enough to warrant around an 18 psi increase in shock pressure to achieve equivalent sag. Then, there's the forward shock mount. Though Gary's original intention was to have use of a full range of adjustment here, in practice, only a few of the furthermost forward positions are viable, owing to the startlingly high leverage ratios that the rearward positions give. More on that shortly. Ride Impressions I rode the the Marra in the 416mm chainstay position, with a 230mm x 65mm Cane Creek Kitsuma air shock mounted in the furthermost forward position, and a 180mm travel Fox 38 fork. That gives the bike its lowest possible bottom bracket height of around 350mm, which is pretty high in the grand scheme. Positioning it further back raises the BB, lengthens the reach, shortens the rear-center, and steepens the head and seat tube angles. Gary's recommendation was to run sag between 20% and 25%, with both high- and low-speed compression and rebound damping close to fully open. Weighing just 59 kg, that's how I would normally run the dials of any shock, to get it as active as possible at the relatively low pressures I would normally run. That said, that sag range is very much on the firm end of the spectrum, especially with such a long travel bike. For context, a conventional enduro bike would normally run around 30% sag, while a reduced sag of 20-25% is normally reserved for much shorter travel bikes, such as 100-120mm travel XC race bikes. Jessie-May Morgan Location: Tweed Valley, Scotland Age: 31 Height: 5'4" / 163cm Weight: 130 lbs / 59 kg, kitted Hopping onto the Marra, the first thing I noticed was how tall the bike is. Relatively little sag combined with 155mm cranks from Hope, and the increased saddle height they require, gave me a center of gravity that felt substantially higher than I am accustomed to. I felt perched atop the bike, which took some getting used to, though the seat tube angle felt plenty steep enough to give an upright climbing position, with the BB tucked underneath my hips in a comfortable position. On the whole, the bike felt very inefficient on fire road climbs. I didn't feel as though the suspension was to blame here, owing to it feeling impressively neutral under pedaling; there really was little to no pedal-induced bob to speak of, even with the shock's damper settings fully open. I'm more tempted to apportion blame to the bike's 17.3 kg (38.1 lb) heft, and the extra drivetrain drag introduced by two extra pulleys; the tensioner pulley and the idler. I didn't do a great deal of technical climbing, but on the odd occasion I did, the rear end felt quite muted over bumps, and traction was not lacking. The bike has a reach of 451mm. That is approaching (but not exceeding) the upper end of the range that I'd choose to ride, but with a 35mm stem it was well within the realm of manageable. I was able to maneuver the bike around to hit my usual lines on well-known enduro trails without undue aggression. What I wasn't able to do was hit those lines with any of the usual pace I'm accustomed to. On the mellower gradient trails, littered with small gaps over furrows and root clusters, I found I was failing to make the backside of features that are normally very achievable. The bike felt sluggish through these twisty, busy sections of track, and was not able to carry good speed. The rear end felt harsh, as though it wasn't efficiently absorbing bumps, delivering a distinct lack of composure. Figuring out where to position my weight between the two wheels proved difficult, and it often felt as though I was getting rocked back and forth, never quite getting a good, consistent body position where the bike felt predictable. There were a few glimpses of things starting to work, but they were few and far between. I did, by and large, stick to Gary's recommendations of around 23% sag. Curiosity did get the better of me, however, and I did do one run with a more standard 30% sag just to see how that felt; sadly, it did not improve matters, and the bike simply felt as though it was folding in compressive corners, with marked tendency to understeer. To get some consistent runs, I completed seven shuttle-assisted laps of a well-established downhill race track. After a couple of sighting laps, I incrementally adjusted sag from around 25% down to 20%. With more air in the shock, the bike actually felt more supple off the top, with a less raw feel to it over the smaller bumps in the track. Still, it felt harsh on successive hits, jarring almost, and that feeling of the rear wheel getting easily hung up did not go away. That said, there was no discernible pedal kickback to speak of, even under firm braking through rough terrain. The harshness instead felt to be coming up through the bike, as though the rear wheel wasn't able to get up and out of the way over bumps, particularly while on the brakes over braking bumps. Throughout the day, I developed an enhanced appreciation for the benefits of not braking in corners - a rule of basic riding technique that can help one carry more speed through corners. This is of course something I've been familiar with for many years, but the benefits of rigidly applying that rule felt particularly important while riding the Marra, if only to make it more predictable through corners. In higher speed, well-supported corners where I could stay off the brakes, the Marra felt at its most predictable. The geometry felt well preserved throughout the apex of the turns, and it was easy enough to hold a consistent line on berms. As the number of laps increased, and the sag reduced, the riding experience improved as I subconsciously adapted to how the bike behaves. Dan Roberts ( of Behind the Numbers fame ), did a spot of suspension analysis on this one to help put some numbers to what's going on here. To be perfectly clear, I refrained from looking at any kinematic graphs until I had ridden the Marra on several occasions. The above account was written up in advance of seeing the outcome of the suspension analysis. The Marra V2 leverage curve, as per the configuration tested Kinematic The graphs are illuminating, to say the least. The leverage curve goes some way to explaining the harshness of the Marra's ride quality. The leverage, or the mechanical advantage that the rear axle has over the shock, is incredibly high initially at 3.8. That is something that can and does give a very supple feel on touch down. However, that leverage ratio falls incredibly sharply, with more leverage ratio change in the first 90mm of travel than other bikes have over their whole travel range. Within 90mm travel, the ratio has already dropped to its lowest value of around 2.34, and from thereon becomes regressive to bottom-out. With the Marra's leverage curve being so progressive through that initial portion, the suspension actually uses quite a lot of its rear wheel travel relative to sag at the shock. A sag of 20% at the shock gives a rear wheel travel sag of 39.5mm (24% sag), meaning the bike has less positive travel available to use as compared to bikes that see less progression through the early stroke. The Cane Creek Kitsuma on the Marra has a stock off-the-shelf tune, not custom to the kinematic of the Marra. In terms of tuning suspension for such a leverage curve... well, manufacturers don't tend to work with such high starting ratios. As an example, RockShox recommend that rear suspension designs have an average leverage ratio that sits between 3.0 and 2.1, and the Marra does conform to that with an average leverage ratio of 2.54. The difficulty comes with the huge variation in leverage ratio throughout the travel; tuning a shock to offer effective damping over such a range is, I am reliably informed, not terribly viable, and a tuner would effectively have to make a decision on which portion of the travel to tune the damper for, leading to big compromises either way. While I felt as though much of the bike's unpredictable ride feel could be attributed to the dramatic changes in rear-center length, the high leverage ratios throughout the first portion of the bike's travel could also have been a contributory factor. High leverage ratios deliver a relatively slow shaft speed, with little damping force generated as a result, something that may have resulted in faster rebound speeds than desired. That'd be compounded by the fact that a high leverage ratio requires relatively more shock pressure to achieve the same level of sag (131 psi gave 23% sag). Auckland Cycle Works Marra magnified rear axle path The Marra's dramatic drop in leverage ratio is occurring while the rear-center length is increasing significantly. At the point at which the rear axle stops tracking rearwards, the rear-center length has grown by a whopping 48mm from the unloaded state, which is around twice as much growth that a conventional bike sees throughout its entire travel. Meaningful changes, both to how much support is offered up by the rear suspension, and how long the rear-center length is, are occurring at a portion in the travel that the bike spends most of its time oscillating about. This unusual set of characteristics is likely responsible for the lack of predictability I experienced while riding the Marra. Marra pedal kickback at three gear ratios There is minimal cross-talk between chain forces and suspension movement while riding in the smaller sprockets of the cassette, as one does while descending. The positioning of the idler pulley on the moving lower link, as per the i-track patent, keeps chain growth to a minimum in these gears. In fact, in the 10T sprocket, pedal kickback values drop into negative values, meaning the required length of chain between the cassette, idler pulley and chainring actually decreases. In the absence of a chain guide, such a design would likely have a tendency to cause the chain to come away from the teeth of the idler or the chainring thanks to the addition of unnecessary chain links along those top spans of chain. Meanwhile, for the larger cogs of the cassette, pedal kickback is much greater. In the 52T, the pedal kickback value sits at 7° at sag, doubling to 14° at just over half way into the travel. It's worth pointing out here that this is higher than we see for other idler-equipped enduro bikes, and actually comparable to more conventional Horst pivot designs. All that said, I honestly can't say I was able to discern that while riding. If anything, I'd say the pedaling action felt pretty smooth over rougher ground, and traction wasn't an issue. To be fair, more noticeable than anything was the change in cadence that comes with very short (155mm) cranks. This was distracting enough that it was difficult to notice much else about the pedaling feel. Auckland Cycle Works Marra anti-squat As you might expect, anti-squat is at its highest in the climbing gears. Though it is remarkably high at 179% (for 32-52T) in an unloaded state, it drops sharply to 102% for 23% sag. That's a value that many manufacturers aim for, as it delivers a bike that doesn't sink into its suspension travel under pedal-induced accelerations. This is the silver-lining of the Marra's suspension design. However, those desirable anti-squat values are only present through a small window around sag, and only really apply to the easiest gears. The story is quite different when moving to the smaller cogs of the cassette. In fact, the anti-squat drops into negative values in the 10T cog. In the real world, that meant the bike wallowed while sprinting in harder gears, making it feel mushy and inefficient. No suspension design is immune to variation in anti-squat through different gears of the cassette, but the Marra's KOLARP suspension design brings about a much wider variation than most. Auckland Cycle Works Marra anti-rise A major contributor to the harsh ride feel of the Marra is how the suspension behaves under braking. Anti-rise is the metric we use to describe suspension's tendency to either rise (extend) or compress (squat) under braking. Many designers look to keep anti-rise around about or south of 100%, which theoretically would give a suspension platform that remains neutral, or even extends a little, under braking. It essentially aims to keep the suspension active under braking, so that the rear wheel can continue to move up and out of the way of consecutive bumps on the trail. The anti-rise of the Marra is inordinately high, sitting at 179% at around 23% sag. Later on in the travel, anti-rise will only drop as low as 97%, which is higher than many bikes will start at. In real life, that translates to the suspension being forced into compression under braking, pushing the bike to a much lower leverage ratio and a higher spring rate, where the suspension is less able to effectively absorb consecutive hits. Having listened to Gary's thoughts on suspension design, and having read the information on his website about the KOLARP kinematic , these high anti-rise values were very much intentional. His interpretation however, is that the suspension stays at sag under braking. Parting Thoughts To some, it may seem as though the many of the mainstream mountain bike brands are are lacking in creativity when it comes to suspension design. Many of the big players are sticking with comparatively plain-looking, conventional designs like the linkage-driven single-pivot or the four-bar Horst link. While these aren't especially visually exciting, they are well-understood designs that can be tweaked subtly to deliver a ride feel that is very predictable, and easy to ride for a wide range of bike handling competencies. Suspension designers can wade in here and tell me i'm wrong, if they like. But, at least to my mind, we are past the point of reinventing the proverbial wheel when it comes to linkage layouts. There are so many tried and tested designs out there that do an excellent job of providing good support, while balancing pedaling efficiency and maintaining a good, safe-feeling geometry under braking. That's not to say that there aren't some innovative new suspension designs on the horizon. Specialized and Norco spring to mind, here. Even they aren't reinventing the wheel, however. Both platforms (still not commercially available) are about finding ways to independently adjust kinematic parameters (anti-rise, axle path, leverage curve), with a view to fine-tuning these elements to suit different terrain types (or more specifically, World Cup race tracks). I guess what I'm trying to say here, is that I'm not surprised that some of Gary's claims around how the KOLARP suspension design works haven't materialized in the Marra. The Auckland Cycle Works website reads, "KOLARP is a new patented suspension system that delivers better performance uphill, downhill, pedaling and braking." It would be surprising if anyone with a fraction of the budget of these companies could, without the use of modern computer aided design and suspension analysis tools, design a bike that out-performed incumbent, race-winning designs. Sadly, the Auckland Cycle Works Marra prototype has not hit the mark. In reality, many of its suspension characteristics are not desirable, which is why you'll struggle to find a modern day bike with a kinematic that comes close to that of the Marra. The Production Marra The Marra V2 tested here is just a prototype, after all. We reached out to Gary to find out what changes the production Marra will be subject to. His response was as follows: "The prototype bike that you rode had an issue which required swapping out rear shocks, this ended up raising the BB height by 10mm and putting the bike outside the window where the various kinematic forces are balanced. This would also prevent you pushing the bike through corners with your feet. Since the front centre shortens and the rear centre lengthens by very similar amounts, standing centrally in the bike is a technique which helps Marra perform at its best. I offered to remedy the adjustment range by fitting a shorter shock yoke. My own further testing with the shorter yoke highlighted the importance of this adjustment and puts the shock back into the centre of the range of adjustment. The rider has the opportunity to fine-tune the BB height to their exact preference. I’m aware that all my kinematic values are outliers, but there is a balance to be found between the highly curved axle path and the equally curved leverage curve. The raised BB means that this balance was missed during these test rides. The next iterations of Marra and Reiver has a number of changes, maintaining adjustability and reducing weight. R&D is ongoing in both CAD and Lego!" Pricing & Availability In 2024, Auckland Cycle Works will produce only 25 Marra frames. Pre-order is open now, with a 50% down payment of £2,750 required to secure a spot. In that £5,500 total price, Gary is offering custom geometry, the choice of any color imaginable, and those hyper-adjustable dropouts. Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Enduro Bikes Auckland Cycle Works Marra Author Info: Jessie-May Morgan Pitfichie, Scotland Member since Oct 26, 2023 401 articles Jessie-May has been working as a mountain bike technical editor since 2018, joining Pinkbike's editorial team in 2023. In a previous life she worked in bioscience research in Edinburgh where she used microscopes to measure things about the immune system. She likes all bikes, but enduro bikes have historically been the preference. Jessie-May has made up the numbers at a handful of World Enduro races, though she is now in her 'downcountry era', and has an unhealthy obsession with handlebars.

title: Review: Virginia Tech's Top 10 Safest MTB Helmets Ridden & Rated
author: Matt Beer
product_name: Virginia Tech's Top 10 Safest MTB Helmets Ridden & Rated
article_text: Virginia Tech's Top 10 MTB Helmets https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/qhWtUAWU Copied Loading ad Live 00:25 00:00 16:56 What makes a helmet “safe”? Is it thicker material, more coverage, or slip planes? From a general physics perspective, more material (considering it’s the correct type) will dissipate the most energy. We’ve seen examples of that theory before - remember the Troy Lee Designs D3 that Cam Zink wore at the 2013 Red Bull Rampage? That was a special use-case, though, plus the average consumer isn't likely to ride around with an enormous bobble-head-like helmet either. That’s not exactly practical, plus extra inertia from a heavier helmet can have other negative effects as well. Helmet manufacturers and other head trauma experts, such as MIPS, have pushed the development of safer equipment in a realistic package. We’ve seen other forms of safety features, especially slip planes, but also designs like 6D’s Omni-Directional System (ODS), and the implementation of variable density materials. Each helmet manufacturer touts their design work, but one entity, Virginia Tech (VT) has been the consumers’ go-to reference to check "how safe a helmet is", or at least, where it stacks up in comparison to other helmets on the market. We’ve rounded up the current top-10 MTB-specific helmets and listed them by their Virginia Tech ranking. Some of those are half-shells, others are ¾ open-face designs, and there’s even one lightweight enduro full-face that made the list. Included is their overall test score, certifications, and technical details. We've also spent time in each one and highlighted the pros and cons. Just want to know which ones we like best? Read our conclusions here . Certifications EN1078: This is a European safety standard for helmets used during cycling, skateboarding, and roller skating. CPSC 1203: The U.S. safety standard for bicycle helmets for persons aged 5 and older, issued by the Consumer Product Safety Commission. AS/NZS 2063: The Australian/New Zealand safety standard for bicycle helmets. NTA 8776: Is a Dutch standard certification given to high-speed e-bike helmets. These are designed to provide increased protection over traditional bike helmets for e-bikes, which reach speeds up to 45 km/h (28 mph). They offer increased coverage and can withstand higher impacts than traditional cycling helmets. How Virginia Tech Conducts Tests The ratings are conducted at their laboratory, independently of the manufacturer. The results are gathered from the data taken from over 24 impact tests conducted from a drop tower. Virginia Tech positions the helmet to receive medium and high energy impacts on 6 locations, measuring the linear acceleration and rotational velocity sustained. The forces are cross-referenced against concussion risk and given a value. Those are added up for an overall score. The lower the score, the fewer concussions the rider would experience, and therefore, the higher the helmet ranks. You can read more specifics about Virginia Tech's testing here . Pinkbike may earn commission on any sales through the affiliate links below. Learn more . #1: POC Cularis BUY AT: Backcountry (USA) Before the Cularis came out, POC was quite far down the Virginia Tech ranking list with the Tectal in 90th, and unfortunately, the beefier-looking Kortal wasn't tested. It was clear that POC had some catching up to do if they were to be more visible on the VTech ranks. Well, they hit a home run with the Cularis as it claimed top spot. It pipped the previous best MTB helmet on there, the Fox Speedframe RS, by a few tenths in scoring. From the outside, and at a glance, there's nothing remarkable about the Cularis - it's quite an average-looking helmet. The coverage is less in terms of EPS foam compared to the Kortal, but that clearly doesn't matter as much when it comes to the impact testing results. Cularis Details • VT score: 7.1 • Fidlock chin strap closure • EPS and polycarbonate shell construction • Mips Air Node • Weight: 360 g • Sizes: S (51–54 cm), M (55–58 cm), L (59–62 cm) • Price: $260 USD • Certifications: EN1078, CPSC 1203, AS/NZS 2063 • poc.com Oddly enough, it doesn't receive the Dutch E-bike safety certification (NTA 8776), which deems helmets appropriate for use over 45km/h. The Cularis uses the newer, slim profile Mips Air Node slip plane built into the sweat liner - no more sharp plastic edges there. It's not the most sweat-absorbing liner, but it does away with any squeaky or sharp plastic inside. It also has a deep fit similar to the Kortal. Glasses storage is found in the forward vents, which feature a fuzzy material to grip those eyewear arms. I wouldn't call it secure, but it does the job if you want to stash them while climbing. The price is getting up there at $260 USD, but should you pick up one of these helmets, you can rest easy knowing it's number one in Virginia Tech's books. You can read more about the Cularis in this full-length review by Seb Stott . The Cularis is a mix of POC's Kortal and Tectal in terms of looks. Even from the inside, the Cularis doesn't show signs of anything unique. Lots of vents up top, yet still the highest rating. The sunglasses' vent storage works well enough to keep them in place uphill. Pros + Secure, deep shell fit. + Mips air node doesn’t have sharp plastic edges. Cons - Doesn't receive NTA-8776 (e-bike) rating. - Air Node liner doesn't have the best sweat absorption. #2: Fox Racing Speedframe RS BUY AT: EVO (USA) The Speedframe RS is the premier half-shell helmet in Fox's lineup. Prior to the POC Cularis, it sat in first place on the VT ranking, and is still a very worthy contender (as are any of the 5-star helmets). It's the only half-shell helmet to use Mips Integra Split, which uses a three-piece, dual-density EPS foam, like Fox's Proframe RS enduro full-face. An inner and outer shell can rotate like one giant Mips slip-plane. The inner shell is even made up of two halves. There's no shortage of features on the Speedframe RS either. The sweat liner is one of the best; the Ionic+ material absorbs sweat well and doesn't irritate my skin, even after long, arduous days. Out back is a BOA Fit System dial to adjust the tension on the fly, while up front is a sunglasses holster. The rubber grippers under the 3-position visor hold them tight enough to descend confidently without them flying out, but locating that stash zone is trickier than it appears. Speedframe RS Details • VT score: 7.23 • Mips Integra Split Dual Density Protection System • BOA Fit System • Fidlock chin strap closure • Ionic+ head liner • Sunglasses storage • Weight: 400g • Sizes: S (51–55 cm), M (55–59 cm), L (59–63 cm) • Price: $319.95 USD • Certifications: NTA-8776 • foxracing.com In terms of fit, it's shallower than Speedframe Pro and 50g heavier. Cost-wise, it's the most expensive at $319 USD, a whole $100 more than the Pro model. The shell is made up of dual-density EPS foams. The inner shell can rotate and is made up of a front and rear half. This bullseye is hard to hit with sunglasses. Once in place, they're locked, though. Pros + Only ½ shell helmet on the market with Mips Split Integra. + Dual EPS construction. Cons - Pricey. - Eyewear storage is tricky to find. #3: Fox Racing Speedframe Pro BUY AT: Jenson (USA) Fox has three new helmet models under the Speedframe heading: a standard model, the Pro, and the RS mentioned above. This one shares the same styling and exterior features of the RS, like the eyewear stowage zone, BOA Fit System, and Fidlock buckle, but the main difference is the shell construction The Pro opts for a Mips Air Node slip plane, opposed to the advanced-looking Integra Split. You still receive the benefit of dual-density EPS foams inside the shell for absorbing variable forces. It's not an inexpensive helmet, however, in my eyes, the Speedframe Pro is a decent value at the higher-end market. Speedframe Pro Details • VT score: 8.1 • Mips Air Node Protection System • BOA Fit System • Fidlock chin strap closure • Dual-density EPS • Sunglasses storage • Weight: 350g • Sizes: S (51–55 cm), M (55–59 cm), L (59–63 cm) • Price: $219.95 USD • Certifications: NTA-8776 • foxracing.com It's worth noting that the fit is slightly deeper than the Speedframe RS. It's lighter and cheaper, too, yet still receives a score of 8.1 on the Virginia Tech ranking scale. Like the Speedframe RS, the visor has three positions. And a BOA Fit System to dial in the tension. The Speedframe Pro has dual-density EPS, like the RS, except it uses the simpler Mips Air Node. The eyewear storage is identical to the RS model. You'll likely need two free hands to store your glasses. Pros + Dual-denisty EPS. + Good value in the high-end price range. Cons - Eyewear storage is tricky to find. - Air Node liner doesn't have the best sweat absorption. #4: Bluegrass Jetro BUY AT: All4Cycling Here's a helmet you don't see too frequently, but then it goes and lands in #4 on the VT rankings. The Jetro is one of three 3/4 helmets on the list and is a solid lid, if you're into that look. It uses Mips Evolve Core for the slip plane, a Fidlock buckle chin strap closure and comes in three sizes. The weight is reasonable at 595g, and the venting is respectable, given the coverage. Bluegrass's own "Safe-T Heta" fit system has a big range with vertical snap adjustments and an easy-to-reach dial. The padding is very comfy and stays in place well when pulling the helmet on and off - something that can be an issue with other helmets on this list. Jetro Details • VT score: 8.41 • Mips Evolve Core • Fidlock chin strap closure • Additional material around critical impact zones • Safe-T Heta retention adjuster • Weight: 595g • Sizes: S (52-56), M (56-5 , L (58-61) • Price: 250 EUR • NTA 8776 certified • met-helmets.com I found a variety of goggles to work with the Jetro, plus there's even sunglasses storage in the vents up top. The Safe-T Heta retention system. Cushy and secure cheek pads along with a Fidlock buckle. For a 3/4 helmet, the venting is decent. Pros + Great airflow for a ¾ helmet. + Secure cheek pads. Cons - Shallower fit than the Fox Dropframe Pro (2023). - Sunglasses fit in vents but aren't totally secure. . #5: 6D Alterra BUY AT: Jenson (USA) The only full-face helmet making the top 10 MTB helmets in the VT ranks is 6D's Alterra. 6D might be best known for their larger volume moto, BMX, and downhill helmets, but they've managed to pack their advanced safety technology into a lighter-weight package. Omni-Directional System is similar in some regard to Mips Integra Split, but absorbs impacts in a 3-dimensional sense, not just rotationally. That's proven to score well in VT's testing, too. On top of that, the chinbar is reinforced with carbon fiber and earns certification for downhill racing. For a lightweight enduro full-face helmet, it also grants a ton of airflow, even with goggles installed. The fit is a bit roomy in some of the rearward areas, but that could be tuned with extra padding. A Fidlock buckle is handy for pulling the helmet off when taking a break at the top, and then back on before dropping back in. Alterra Details • VT score: 8.5 • Fidlock chin strap closure • Omni-Directional Suspension (ODS) • EPP and EPS construction • Weight: 719 g (actual, size M) • Sizes: S (53-56), M (56-59), L (59-62) • Price: $395 USD • ASTM F1952 DH and NTA 8776 certified • 6dhelmets.com Back in the spring, we reviewed the Alterra in depth and were thoroughly impressed with the ventilation and safety tech built into the helmet, given its weight. The recess and padding on the lower edges aim to save clavicles. From the back, the helmet has a bit of a sloping, wide Porsche coupe appearance to it. The padding on the chin strap moves out of the way and the buckle can rest against your jawbone. There's no debating the convenience of a Fidlock helmet buckle. Pros + Impressive venting for a lightweight fullface + ODS system is silent Cons - Chin strap padding rotates to side - Additional padding may be needed to refine fit #6: Specialized Tactic 4 BUY AT: Specialized (USA) What's a helmet that only costs $59.95 USD doing in the Top 10 list? How did Specialized do it? And why would you spend more than that, given its score of 8.55 on the VT scale? Those are all very good questions. Making up the Tactic 4 are a polycarbonate shell, an EPS foam construction, and Mips Evolve Solution - pretty par for the course, yet it's #6. The coverage is great and the weight remains well under 400g. Some answers where the costs were cut lie in some of the less critical materials, and the adjustments. Tactic 4 Details • VT score: 8.55 • Mips Evolve Solution • Breakaway visor • Standard buckle chin strap closure • EPS construction • Weight: 380g • Sizes: S (51-56), M (55-59), L (59-63) • Price: $59.95 USD (on sale from $119.99 • NTA 8776 certified • specialized.com The sweat liner isn't the most absorbent, and, in a previous longer-term review, it delaminated quickly. The chin strap uses a standard clasp buckle, which we can live with; however, the TriFix ear straps aren't adjustable. This can cause the helmet to sit further forward on your brow than you may like. The retention system is a smooth, accessible dial, though, and that offers vertical adjustment, which may alleviate that for you. The best execution of eyewear storage. Dial in the fit. The TriFix strap isn't adjustable. The biggest downside of the Tactic 4 is the liner. This one is intact, but in our previous long-term test, this fell apart quickly. Pros + Unbeatable safety rating for the price. + Best execution of eyewear storage. Cons - Liner delaminates at seams quickly. - TriFix strap is not adjustable and may cause the helmet to sit forward. #7: Sweet Protection Trailblazer Mips BUY AT: Competitive Cyclist (USA) Back in the summer of 2022, Sweet Protection sponsored our Quebec Trail Bike Field Test and sent out Trailblazer Mips helmets, so I've spent a considerable amount of time in this lid . After that test, I continued to wear the helmet because it was comfy, secure, and promoted a ton of airflow. The 4-piece variable elasticity shell technology and Mips Evolve System stack up to give the Trailblazer an 8.69 score on the VT scale, which is nothing to scoff at - it even beats out another one of Sweet Protection's heavy-duty, enduro helmets. Trailblazer Mips Details • VT score: 8.69 • Mips Evolve System, 4-piece shell • 360 Occigrip Fit System w/height and width adjustment • Eyewear storage in front vents • Weight: 325g • Sizes: S/M (53-56), M/L (56-59), L/XL (59-61) • Price: $200 USD • CPSC 1203, CE EN 1078 certified • sweetprotection.com I don't have too many gripes with the Trailblazer Mips, but one of them was the malleable visor. It's not the end of the world, but it can easily sit lopsided. For a helmet that costs $200, that shouldn't be an issue. I'd also appreciate a Fidlock buckle at that price point. The eyewear storage is less refined than their Bushwhacker 2Vi helmet, but it's acceptable. Yellow marks the Mips liner. The flexible visor is durable, however, this one doesn't have any notches to keep it lined up. No Fidlock buckle here. Pros + Great airflow + Fit retention system has height and width adjustment Cons - Would expect Fidlock closure given price tag - Visor can easily be distorted and sit crooked #8: Fox Racing Dropframe Pro (2020 version) BUY AT: REI (USA) If the helmet shape doesn't cause you to raise an eyebrow, then perhaps the model year might. This is the older model Dropframe from 2020. Yes, there's a newer version out now, and looks aside, they are quite different, however, that doesn't detract from its safety ranking. This uniquely shaped helmet from Fox is light for a 3/4 open-face helmet at 485g. Despite coming in four shell sizes, I found the medium to have some pressure points in the upper back half of the shell. The features are on the basic side, too. There's a standard buckle, standard Mips slip plane, and no retention system at all - it relies on padding alone. Dropframe Pro (2020 version) Details • VT score: 8.85 • Standard clip buckle closure • Dual-density Varizorb EPS construction • Weight: 485g • Sizes: S (52-54), M (54-56), L (56-5 , XL (58-60) • Price: $179.99 USD • Certifications: CPSC, EN 1078, AS/NZS 2063 • foxracing.com If nothing else, the Dropframe Pro 202 can be scooped up for some killer deals, and that doesn't detract from the fact that it earns its way into the Top 10. Angular lines and a fixed visor. No BOA Fit System here. Regular old Mips for Princess Leia. And a standard buckle. Pros + Light for a ¾ shell helmet. + Dual EPS foam. Cons - No rention system. - Tight for a medium with small pressure points on top, back corners (there are 4 sizes, however). #9: Fox Racing Dropframe Pro (2023 version) BUY AT: EVO (USA) Landing right behind the 2020 Dropframe Pro is the 2023 version. It's large and in charge at 630g, but there's a ton a features packed into this helmet. Inside the Dual-density Varizorb EPS shell construction is an improved Mips Evolve Core slip plane with an Ionic+ antimicrobial liner. There's also a handy goggle strap recess and a GoPro mount under the 3-position visor. Most notable is the addition of the BOA Fit System, which wasn't featured on the 2020 model, so you can literally dial in the security of the helmet. With that said, the number of shell sizes has been slimmed down to just three, however, the helmet does come with two extra pad kits: one thinner, one thicker. I found the fit to be snug, yet very comfy. The venting isn't as great as the Jetro, and it's best saved for e-biking or cooler weather. Dropframe Pro (2023 version) Details • VT score: 9.03 • Fidlock chin strap closure • Mips Evolve Core • Dual-density Varizorb EPS construction • Ionic+ antimicrobial liner • BOA dial • Weight: 630g • Sizes: S (51-55), M (55-59), L (59-63) • Price: $279.95 USD • Certifications: NTA-8776 • foxracing.com Given the lower levels of ventilation and weight, I'd be more inclined to spend a bit more and go for the 6D Alterra - I like my pearly whites. There is less chance of ending up with a Jerry goggle strap position thanks to the indentations. Lots of vents, but still not the best in terms of ventilation. Mips Evolve Core slip plane and Ionic plus liner. The cheek pads can dislodge when you try to pull the helmet on by the straps. Pros + Snug/comfy fit of a full-face. + Easy to reach BOA Fit System dial. Cons - Cheek pads can dislodge when pulling on the helmet too easily. - Warmer than the previous model. #10: Sweet Protection Bushwhacker 2Vi Mips BUY AT: Competitive Cyclist (USA) Rounding out the Top 10 MTB Helmets in the Virginia Tech Rankings is the big sibling in the half-shell lineup from Sweet Protection. The Bushwhacker 2Vi Mips is a long name with a lot of details packed in. What is 2Vi exactly? This is the name of the technology Sweet Protection gives to the multi-density EPS construction spread throughout the helmet. It's not just randomly placed, but positioned purposefully in common impact zones. The shell is large in volume on the outside and which leads to a deep-fitting helmet. The M/L weighs 430g, has adequate venting, and enough adjustment to suit most head shapes. Bushwhacker 2Vi Mips Details • VT score: 9.05 • Mips Air • 2Vi multi-density EPS construction • 360 fit system • Fidlock closure • Eyewear storage in front vents • Weight: 430g • Sizes: S/M (53-56), M/L (56-59), L/XL (59-61) • Price: $250 USD • Certifications: UKCA, NTA-8776, EN1078:2012, A1:2012, CPSC 1203\ • sweetprotection.com Under the visor are rubber pads in the forward vents to hold glasses tightly. Unfortunately, I found that the visor can shift positions and rattle on rocky descents or head-slapping drops. A precise fit, but the sunglasses storage is solid. The Mips Air liner is as comfy as... well, air. Vertical and lateral occipital adjustments with the 360 Fit system. The visor can jiggle and make noise when charging down the trail. Pros + Snug, wrap around fit + Dual EPS foams in key areas Cons - Visor can rattle in mid position - Excessively long dead-end chin strap loop (I cut mine to avoid choking) Overview Top Choice: Fox Speedframe RS Before the POC Cularis, the Fox Speedframe sat in the top spot of the Virginia Tech ranks for MTB helmets. The Speedframe holds a number of features that make its shell construction more complex with Mips Integra Split, and that seems to have paid off. Fox reworked the shape of the helmet inside, too. In the past, the forehead area didn’t work for me, but now it’s very comfortable. The venting is top-notch, the liner wicks sweat well, and the forward vents hold sunglasses securely enough to ride with. Best Value: Specialized Tactic 4 How could you ignore this helmet? It lands in the top 10 MTB helmets and is currently on sale for $59.95 USD. My favourite part, aside from the excellent safety-to-price ratio of this helmet, is how easy it is to locate the glasses holster - you can place them with just one hand. That’s something I haven’t been able to do with any other helmet (besides the Specialized Ambush). As for that liner, I bet you could retrofit the higher-end Ionic+ fabric from the Ambush helmet into the Tactic with some arts and crafts. Honourable Mention: 6D Alterra Shapes aside, the Alterra is an incredible helmet. Its unique impact protection design, based on Virginia Tech’s testing, proves its worth. For a full-face, it also breathes and vents exceptionally well. Add in the relatively low weight and reasonable price, and you can see why it deserves an honorable mention. Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Ridden And Rated Helmets 6d Bluegrass Fox Racing POC Specialized Sweet Protection Author Info: Matt Beer Squamish, BC, Canada Member since Mar 16, 2001 607 articles Matt has been a Pinkbike reader since the site's early days. He discovered mountain biking in Newfoundland, where a small community of dedicated riders and trail builders set him on a path to earn two Canadian National Downhill Championship titles. Now based in Squamish, BC, Matt works as a tech editor for Pinkbike, specializing in gravity-focused mountain bike reviews, tech, and racing coverage.

title: Review: One Year on the Liv Intrigue LT Advanced Pro 1
author: Sarah Moore
article_text: REVIEW Liv Intrigue LT Advanced Pro 1 WORDS: Sarah Moore PHOTOS: Tom Richards Liv introduced the Intrigue LT early in 2023, first in an aluminum version and then in the 'Advanced' carbon model that I've been riding for the past year. This is an entirely different Intrigue to the one I tested back in 2020, with the 'LT' designation calling out the 150mm of rear travel compared to the shorter travel version's 125mm of rear travel. Earlier this spring, Liv added the Intrigue X to the line, which slots into the range between the 125mm Intrigue 29 and the 150mm Intrigue LT as a 140mm travel affair. The Intrigue LT features an integrated downtube storage compartment, has adjustable geometry thanks to the 3-position flip chip, and comes in four sizes. The extra-small and small come in a mixed-wheel configuration, while the medium and large bikes come with 29" wheels, although they are also compatible with a 27.5" rear wheel. Liv Intrigue LT Advanced Pro 1 Details • Wheel size: Mixed - XS, S / 29" M, L • Carbon frame • Travel: 150mm / 160mm fork • 65.1° head angle (mid setting) • 77.6° seat angle (mid setting) • Chainstay length: 442mm • 3-position flip-chip • Downtube storage compartment • Sizes: XS, S, M, L • Price: $7,000 USD • liv-cycling.com With the Hail no longer in the lineup, the Intrigue LT is now the most aggressive bike that Liv offers and is the same frame that Rae Morrison raced to several top-10 finishes during the 2023 Enduro World Cup season. Contents Introduction Frame Details Geometry & Sizing Suspension Design Specifications Setup Climbing Descending Technical Report How Does it Compare? Pros & Cons Pinkbike's Take I felt immediately at ease on the Intrigue LT Advanced 1. It's got a ton of traction both on the climbs and the descents and it has a predictability to its handling that made me feel like it had my back when venturing onto new trails. — Sarah Moore Frame Details The new Intrigue LT Advanced is the first bike in Liv's line up to get in-frame storage and uses the same system as Giant's Trance and Reign models. When you turn the latch on the downtube 90 degrees, an internal compartment that contains a water-resistant bag is revealed. The door stayed secure and didn't rattle for the duration of the test, but I didn't find the compartment as useful as it could have been had it had a larger opening. I still ran my pump alongside the water bottle cage and my windbreaker strapped to my handlebars. I was slightly disappointed to find that you can only fit a small 21 ounce (620 mL) water bottle on the size medium frame, but at least the medium frame fits a standard sized small water bottle. While there are water bottle mounts on all frames sizes and Liv says that you can put a water bottle on the two smaller sizes, they will only fit a 13.5 ounce (400 mL) bottle. While the shorter-travel Intrigue has a dual-position link that allows you to choose between a high or low position, the Intrigue LT Advanced has three positions. This is actually accomplished with two different flip chips – a combination high/low and a dedicated mid-position chip. The adjustment on the upper rocker arm of the rear suspension changes the head tube angle, seat tube angle and bottom bracket height. It's worth noting that the low position is not permitted for medium and large frames when set up as a mixed-wheel configuration. I was impressed at how durable the top coat is and how good the frame continues to look after more than a year of riding and not always been treated gently. There are a couple minor chips in the clear coating when you look closely, but overall, it's very tough and the raw carbon finish looks great. There are serious rubber protectors along the downtube to keep it safe when rocks fly up from the trail or when you're shuttling and there is a thick ribbed chainstay protector to keep chainslap to a minimum. I did wear through the rear housing cable initially since it pops out of the rear triangle very close to the chain behind the chainring. Under chainslap, the part that isn't covered by a rubber protector got frayed. Once I secured the cable in place with zip ties, it didn't happen again however. Luckily, since routing the cable through the rear triangle proved to be more challenging than it should have been since there was no tube in tube. The frame has integrated cable ports and is compatible with Fox Live Valve. Liv uses a SRAM DUB press fit bottom bracket on the Intrigue LT and the rear triangle is compatible with SRAM's UDH. Most of the pivot bolts have the torque spec and rotation direction listed on them. The frames uses Boost hub spacing and the max tire size that fits in the frame is 2.5". The biggest chainring you can ride is a 32t. Geometry & Sizing Frame sizes Small and XS are solely designed for an MX configuration with a 29" wheel up front and a 27.5" wheel in the rear. Frame sizes Medium and Large come with 29" wheels stock front and rear but can accommodate a 27.5" wheel in the rear if you choose to purchase one separately. The geometry chart for the Intrigue LT is more complicated than most, with the three flip-chip positions making for three different head tube, seat tube and bottom bracket heights per size. In addition, Liv shared the geometry chart for the medium and large sizes in an MX configuration, which adds more numbers to the mix, although the low position isn't actually permitted for frame sizes M and L when set up as a mixed-wheel configuration due to safety standards. I rode the size medium frame and settled on the low setting with the 29" wheels, which gave it a 64.8-degree head tube angle, a 77.3 degree seat tube angle, a 439mm reach, 1208mm wheelbase and 35mm bottom bracket drop. Using the flip-chip will alter the angles by about 0.4 degrees in either direction and move the reach 4mm in either direction. The reach numbers vary from 398 in the Low position on the XS, up to 467mm on the Large frame in the High position. Chainstays are the same regardless of frame size, varying from 443mm in the Low position to 440 in the High position. Suspension Design The Liv Intrigue LT uses the same Maestro suspension design that Giant bikes do which uses four pivot points and two linkages that all work together to create a single floating pivot point. Maestro's upper rocker arms feature trunnion shock mounts and a high-pressure molding process is used to produce the Advanced Forge Composite upper rocker arm. Liv says it is lighter, stiffer and stronger than aluminum. A 185x55mm Trunnion mount shock handles the 150mm of rear wheel travel and the pivot bearings are double sealed. Specifications Release Date 2023 Price $7000 Travel 150/160 Rear Shock Fox Float X Performance Elite 185x55 Fork Fox 36 Performance Elite, 160mm, Grip damper Cassette SRAM GX 10x52 Crankarms SRAM GX, 30t, 55mm chainline Chainguide MRP AMg V2 chainguide Bottom Bracket SRAM DUB, press fit Rear Derailleur SRAM GX Eagle Chain SRAM GX Shifter Pods SRAM GX Eagle Handlebar Giant Contact SLR TR35, composite, 20mm rise, 35mm Stem Giant Contact SL, 35mm Grips Liv Tactal Pro Single Lock-On Brakes SRAM G2 RSC, hydraulic, SRAM CenterLine rotors [F]200mm, [R]180mm Hubs [F] Giant MTB alloy, 6-bolt, [R] Giant MTB alloy, 3-pawl driver, 6-bolt Spokes Sapim Rim Giant TRX 2 29 WheelSystem, composite, 30mm inner width Tires [F]: Maxxis Minion DHF 29x2.5, 3C Max Terra, EXO, TR, tubeless [R]: Maxxis Dissector 27.5 (XS+S) 29 (M+L) x2.4, 3C Max Terra, EXO, TR, tubeless Seat Liv Sylvia Seatpost TranzX travel-adjustable dropper, remote Compare to other All Mountain/Enduro/XC The spec is pretty dialled for the $7,000 USD price tag of the Liv Intrigue Advanced Pro 1 that I rode. You get a carbon frame, wheels, bar, and a Grip 2 damper in the Fox 36 fork. The carbon parts are “house” branded, which makes it easier for Liv to accomplish this at this price tag than it is for other brands. The only house-branded component I had issues with was the hub, which started to make a racket relatively early on in testing so I swapped the rear wheel out for another wheel for the second half of the test. Standouts for me were the narrower handlebars than are found on most unisex bikes at 760mm (XS), 760mm (S), 760mm (M), and 780mm (L). It's a small thing, but it was nice not to have to cut my bars down to my preferred 760mm. While the lever placement wasn't easy to dial in and I felt it sat a bit too far inwards because of the clamp placement, I did appreciate that the Intrigue LT has decent dropper lengths, something that Liv has struggled with in the past and when I rode the 125mm Intrigue. The TranzX travel-adjustable dropper comes in 125mm travel (XS), 120-150mm travel (S), 150mm travel (M), and 150mm travel on the size Large. Touch points can be a personal preference, but I'm a fan of the Liv Sylvia saddle and swapped out the Liv Tactal Pro Single Lock-On grips for something with a bit more cushion. Otherwise, a SRAM GX drivetrain, Shimano XT brakes, and Maxxis tires round out the parts package. RIDING THE Liv Intrigue LT Advanced 1 Test Bike Setup I usually ride a size medium bike and the Liv Intrigue was no exception, with the medium frame being suited to rider 5'5" (164cm) to 5'9" (175m). Dialling in the suspension and fit was relatively straightforward on the Fox Float X Performance Elite rear shock since it just has rebound and low speed compression adjustments. I varied the sag between 28-30% based on the riding location. As for the Fox 36 Performance Elite fork, it has high and low speed compression and rebound adjustments and I rode it with one token and 20% sag. Other than swapping out the grips for something with a bit more cushion, I got straight to ride with the stock bike. I spent a fair bit of time in all three geometry settings on a variety of trails, including everything from flowy XC trails to bike park descents. Sarah Moore Location: Squamish, BC Age: 33 Height: 5'7" Weight: 160lbs Industry affiliations / sponsors: None Instagram: @smooresmoore Testing Info Testing took place in Squamish, British Columbia and Revelstoke, British Columbia over the course of a year. Conditions varied from dusty blown out summer riding in Revelstoke to slippery, wet days in the Squamish winter green room. The Intrigue LT was my one bike for the year I was on maternity leave and I did everything on it, including cross-country trails, alpine epics, bike park, and black diamond tech on it. The Trailforks loop shown here is one of my go-to favourites, which I did several times on the Intrigue LT. Download the Trailforks App Climbing The climbing position of the Intrigue LT is well balanced and it felt natural from day one. I was comfortable climbing it on a wide variety of terrain, from steep fire road climbs, to twisty single track. The seat tube angle is steep enough that, even in the low position, I didn't have to jam the saddle forward on the rails to get over the pedals and I found it easy to keep the front wheel on the ground on steeper sections of trails. In the high position, the bike is definitely a bit more of an aggressive pedaler and gets you around tight corners a bit quicker, but even in the low position, it's easy to wind up tight corners and pedal strikes weren't common. The bike is also relatively lightweight for its 150mm of rear travel, weighing in right around 30 pounds (13.6kg) without pedals on thanks to lots of house-branded carbon bits including its Giant TRX 2 carbon wheels. There's no two ways about it, a lightweight bike definitely makes the climbs more enjoyable. There's also a 30T chainring on this bike that, especially when you're riding the bike in its full 29er set up, is a great size for being able to stay seated and keep a quicker cadence on steeper climbs. Chainring size is a bit of a personal thing depending on whether you like to power up climbs or spin up them seated and it also depends on where you live and how much of a slog your climbs are, but I do think it is a good choice for this bike. I didn't find the Maestro suspension to be the firmest of pedalling platforms; there's a slight bob when you push into the pedals, or the most lively, so I did find myself adding a bit more air to the rear shock, and ended up running a bit less sag (28%) to help counteract that. The active suspension does mean that it has oodles of traction when you do get to technical climbs, and you don't get bumped off line very often. It feels like the Intrigue LT can just keep finding grip and making its way over uphill obstacles long after you think you're a goner. Descending When things point downhill, the Intrigue LT hits a good middle ground between feeling fun to ride on lower angle terrain, while staying, composed when it gets pushed into steep, black diamond trails. Liv calls it their enduro race bike, and it's more than capable of racing, but it is on the lighter end of what we now see from that category. Even in my preferred low position, it isn't an all-out brute that will forgive any bad line or allow you to huck blindly into messes of roots and rocks. That being said, it is a confident descender and it has a predictability to its handling that made me feel like it had my back when venturing onto new trails. The traction that I appreciated on the climbs was also there on the descents, and the Intrigue LT felt controlled whether I was trying to creep down sections of trail or hit loose, high-speed corners. The Intrigue LT tolerates blue flow trails, but never felt like a rocket ship through them. The suspension really comes to life when things get rough. Even at higher speeds, the bike remained composed on chunky sections of trail. Occasionally, on larger drops, I felt like the bike went through its travel faster than I would have expected it to, but overall it was a predictable and balanced ride. I would say that some of the spec choices felt like they were made with weight in mind and not descending prowess. While the bike's light weight made for a more nimble climber, I put beefier tires on and I would have liked bigger brakes. The EXO casing tires might be lighter, but they are squirmy and much more likely to get burped on fast corners and more puncture-prone than EXO+ tires. Other than the brakes and tires, the components held up well to a year of abuse, including several bike park days. Technical Report SRAM G2 Brakes: I absolutely cooked the G2 brakes on the long descents in Revelstoke and would have next to no power left at the bottom of runs, just when my hands were most tired. I also had to change my pads more frequently than if this bike had come with Code brakes. That being said, it looks like Liv has made a spec change for 2024 and the Intrigue LT Advanced 1 now comes with Shimano XT brakes, although both models are currently still available for purchase. TranzX Dropper Post: The lever position of the TranzX dropper is clunky and I found it a bit hard to reach, but the post itself worked very well despite zero maintenance throughout the year. It's also nice to see a 170mm travel post on Liv's medium-sized frame since that was something they struggled to fit in the past. That being said, the seat tube could be a bit shorter since my 27" inseam is the bare minimum to ride the bike with its 170mm dropper. Liv Sylvia Saddle: Touch points are personal, but I'm pretty picky with saddles and one of the first things I usually do on a new bike is switch out the saddle. However, I didn't feel the need to swap out the Liv Sylvia saddle and found the shape of it very comfortable. It also shows no signs of wear after a year of use. Ibis Ripmo How Does It Compare? Another bike that I've spent quite a bit of time on recently is the Ibis Ripmo. Both bikes have 150mm of rear travel and a 160mm fork, but feel very different out on the trails. If we compare the Intrigue LT in the low position to the Ripmo on paper, the two bikes have near-identical seat tube angles and head tube angles, but the Liv sports chainstays that are 8mm longer than the Ripmo and a reach that's 20mm shorter at 439mm compared to 460mm. The wheelbase on the Ripmo is slightly longer at 1219mm compared to the Intrigue's 1208mm. While the reach numbers look very different, the Intrigue doesn't feel as short as the reach number would suggest and both climbing positions are comfortable. However, on the climbs, no one will be surprised to hear that I prefer the Ibis with its DW-link. It just feels much more responsive when you push into the pedals, despite having the same amount of rear travel. For alpine epics and climbing, there's no doubt that the Ripmo is the tool for the job. When things point downhill, it's not as straightforward. The Ripmo is a livelier bike on the downhills as well, but I'd say that the Intrigue LT feels more capable of plowing through rough sections and requires less rider input to makes its way down the hill. On rough, high-speed trails and on bike park days, I would choose the Intrigue LT. Lining up the spec sheets, the Ibis Ripmo XT touts Shimano XT brakes and drivetrain and a Fox 38, compared to the Liv Intrigue LT Advanced 1's SRAM GX drivetrain, GX brakes and Fox 36. Normally, the Liv Intrigue LT would take the price war by a mile, but currently the Ibis Ripmo XT is on sale for $6,524 USD ($8,699 USD regular price) with carbon wheels, while the Intrigue LT Advanced Pro 1 that I tested is on sale for a comparable $6,600 USD ($7,000 USD regular price). The version of the Intrigue LT Advanced Pro 1 that comes with Shimano XT brakes is not currently on sale and sits at $7,000 USD. Pros + Finds traction easily on the climbs and the descents + Composed on rough terrain + Lightweight build Cons - Not the most supportive suspension platform - You'll need to make a couple spec changes to make it an enduro race rig - XS/S doesn't fit a normal waterbottle Pinkbike's Take The Liv Intrigue LT is a well-rounded bike that has tons of traction whether you're heading uphill or downhill. It's not as beefy as some enduro bikes we see these days, but that keep it relatively lightweight for climbing. While it still might not be the liveliest of climbers due to its pedalling platform, the suspension really comes to life when things get rough on the downhills and with a couple of spec tweaks, it can definitely hold up to all-day enduro racing. — Sarah Moore Visit the high-res gallery for more images from this review. Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews PBWMN Enduro Bikes Liv Liv Intrigue Lt Author Info: Sarah Moore Squamish, BC, Canada Member since Mar 30, 2011 1,624 articles Pinkbike Content Manager

title: Review: Orbea Rallon DH - A Recipe For Locking Into Turns
author: Matt Beer
article_text: Orbea Rallon DH Review https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/vE4paEKx Copied Loading ad Live 00:25 00:00 15:53 The Rallon is Orbea’s first downhill bike, and they hit a knock out when it comes to looks. The straight carbon lines are striking, and the packaging of the suspension components is sleek. There’s even downtube storage, an onboard multi-tool, and room for a water bottle. Hang on. This is a downhill bike, right? In short, yes. Orbea realigned the Rallon name into a two-part gravity line based on the same frame - there's the downhill bike seen here, and a long-legged enduro version. Oftentimes, compromises are made when trying to multitask, or in Orbea's case, making two bikes with one frameset. Based on the Orbea FMD Racing’s season, that doesn't seem to hold true. Tahnee Seagrave made a statement at the World Cup season opener, taking the Rallon to the top step of the Women’s Elite podium. Later on, her teammate Martin Maes stomped his way to third place on flat pedals in Les Gets. Does that mean the Rallon is as capable for average riders, or is this just another example of how top-level downhill racers possess the skills to win on even a shopping cart? Rallon DH Details • 200mm travel, 203mm fork • Carbon frame • MX wheels • Concentric Boost 2 suspension design • Downtube storage • Gravity Link bracket with optional weights • 62.95 - 63.7° head angle • 442 or 450mm (two options, non-adjustable) • Sizes: S, M, L (tested), XL • Lifetime frame warranty • Weight: 16.36 kg / 36.07 lb (size LG, no weights) • Frame weight: 3.1 kg / 6.83 lb (size LG, no shock, no weights) • Pricing: $8,299 USD / €7,999 EUR • Frame only: $4,299 USD (with shock) • More info: orbea.com Contents Introduction Frame Details Suspension Design Geometry & Sizing Specifications Setup Descending Technical Report What's the Best Value? How Does it Compare? Pros & Cons Pinkbike's Take After all these years of trying to make bikes lighter, Orbea comes along and adds weights to their flagship gravity bike on purpose. I guess their Frankenstein downhill/e-bike prototype taught them a thing or two about going fast; some weight isn't a terrible idea. It has to be in the right place though, and you shouldn't expect the same result by taping an e-bike battery to your current downhill bike. — Matt Beer Frame Details A few years ago, you would have been called a kook for strapping lead weights to your bike, but now, it’s all the rage for downhill racers. Orbea was onto the benefits early after testing with e-bikes, and they’ve introduced an industry first - specifically placed and shaped weights - 583 grams to be exact. Those are bolted to something Orbea calls the Gravity Link, The Gravity Link acts as an interchangeable shock basement. The name is somewhat misleading. It's not a pivoting arm, but instead a modular bracket that allows Orbea to build two bikes with one carbon mold. There’s one Gravity Link specific to the Rallon DH, and another two for the Rallon EN that dictate the rear wheel size, 27.5 or 29”. On that point, the Rallon DH is only compatible with a 27.5” wheel, which shouldn’t upset the majority of consumers interested in this bike. The bracket and lower half of the shock are guarded by a plastic cover that snaps into the frame with the push of a button. It's convenient, but it did cause some complaints from the noise police and almost fell off on more than one occasion. Another unavoidable downside of the Gravity Link is the access to the rebound adjusters on the Float X2 if you’re running the added weights. The central weight and the cover will have to be removed before you can twist the rebound dials (which should be facing forward). You'll still need an allen key to reach the HSR knob. The only geometry adjustment lies in the head tube delivered by a proprietary headset cup with a -0.75, 0, and +0.75-degree change. I’d wager that 99% of downhill riders will select the slackest angle and never look back. For that reason, I’d rather see this space integrate a reach adjustment cup, which would merge the frame sizes closer together, or at least a straight 56mm head tube for aftermarket headsets up to the task. 0 seconds of 29 seconds Volume 90% https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/wLCrD49J Copied Live 00:00 00:29 00:29 Suspension Design Orbea has been using its Boost Concentric 2 suspension design on all of its full-suspension bikes (aside from the flex-stay Oiz) for some time now. A single pivot swingarm drives the rocker link to compress the shock, similar to Dave Weagle’s Split Pivot or Trek's ABP design. Together with the DH Gravity Link and its 225x75mm Trunnion-mounted shock, the Rallon achieves 200mm of travel. Down in the lower shock mount is a flip-chip to change the leverage rate to 25 or 30% progression. Orbea has specified a Fox Float X2 air shock on the Rallon, but says it’s coil compatible, too. If you check out their team riders’ bikes, you’ll see that the latter is their preference across the board. The numbers for the Rallon DH with the longer chainstay option and the slackest head tube angle possible. Geometry Orbea built four front triangle molds to fit a decent range of riders. The reach numbers start at a 427mm reach for the size small and jump up roughly 25mm per frame size to 500mm for the XL. Given that the Rallon doubles up on carbon molds for the enduro version, I’m surprised there isn’t a fifth XXL size included and a shuffling of those reach numbers. For the chainstays, consumers have two rear triangle lengths to choose from: 442 or 450mm chainstays. Due to the concentric pivot around the axle, a flip-chip at the dropout isn't possible. The only way to change that would be to purchase a completely separate rear triangle. The one adjustment that can be made to the geometry comes at the head tube. Those specific angles range from 64.4-degrees down to 63.3 using a proprietary cup system. 63.8 degrees is also possible with the neutral offset cup. Specifications Release Date 2025 Price $8299 Travel 200mm Rear Shock Fox Float X2 Factory Fork Fox 40 Factory 29 Grip X2 Headset Orbea Cassette Shimano CS710 10-speed Crankarms Race Face Turbine, 165mm w/34T ring Chainguide ethirteen Vario DH Bottom Bracket Race Face BSA Rear Derailleur Shimano Saint 10-speed Chain Shimano HG95 10-speed Shifter Pods Shimano Saint 10-speed Handlebar Orbea alloy MC10, 20mm Stem Race Face Atlas, 50mm Grips Fizik Comfortfeel grips 32mm Brakes Shimano XTR, 203mm Galfer Shark rotors Hubs DT Swiss 350 Rim Oquo MC32TEAM Tires Maxxis Assegai 29x2.5" 3C MaxxGrip DH Front, Minion DHR II 27.5x2.4, 3C MaxxGrip DH rear Seat Fizik Gravity Alpaca X5 Seatpost Race Face Turbine Compare to other DH Bikes Most of the components hit the primary needs, such as suspension, wheels, tires, and brakes. Not quite your dream list? Lucky for you, Orbea has a custom configurator to select components and paint schemes to suit your taste. RIDING THE Rallon DH Test Bike Setup Orbea invested heavily in this platform and went all in by supporting a factory downhill team, so I wanted to see how the Rallon stacked up against other World Cup-winning bikes. That meant first selecting a size large frame and the longer chainstay option. Still, 450mm is on the shorter side for the size L and XL frames. We’ve seen riders on medium-sized bikes choose longer rear center lengths . To gain even more stability out of the Rallon, the angle-adjusting headset spent most of its time in the slackest setting. I also preferred the additional weights bolted to the Gravity Link in most scenarios, but more on that further down. The pressure in the Float X2 was first set to 30% sag (seated) under the direction of Orbea’s tech team, but that was far too soft. I started with 4cc of volume spacers, but eventually bumped that up to 8cc. More pressure was added to the shock, too, landing in the sweet spot at 24% sag (235 psi). I favored the less progressive shock position, which led to a calmer and more predictable ride. That matched my preferred fork setup (12% sag or 86 psi with 5 volume spacers). Matt Beer Location: Squamish, BC, Canada Age: 38 Height: 5'10" / 178 cm Weight: 170 lb / 77 kg Industry affiliations/sponsors: None Instagram: @mattb33r Getting into the nitty-gritty details, Orbea builds the Rallon with a 20mm rise handlebar and 50mm stem. I found that too low and slow for my preferences. A 35mm rise Burgtec bar and 45mm length stem put me in a more favorable riding position.
