I suspect most riders will make a similar swap given how popular the "high bar" trend is these days. Testing Info I spent the majority of my time on the Rallon in the Whistler Bike Park on jump, tech, and race trails alike. This is where the low center of gravity really stands out. Unfortunately, it's also where the you notice the noises from within the frame the most too. More time was spent on the Cypress downhill tracks in West Vancouver, where the amount of cushion the Rallon offers tackles the frequent mid-size square steps with ease. Download the Trailforks App Descending After landing the Rallon on home turf and putting it through familiar tracks, I still stand by my initial impressions: there’s an overarching sense of being grounded. Even without the added weight, you ride down low in the frame, almost hovering. Tack on that extra 600-ish grams to a generous stack height and you're locked in. The handling reminds me somewhat of the 220mm-travel Commencal Supreme, which is another ground-hugging bike. The Rallon has a similar tendency to set the rider’s feet close to the ground as if you're on a set of skis. Similar to the Supreme, the Rallon has a way of pressing weight down on the bars, rather than pushing from behind the front axle. To me, this gives the sense that the head angle rides steeper than expected. The benefit here is that you get excellent stability from a low center of gravity without the need for an outrageous head angle. All of that locks the front tire to the ground on flat corners and shallow berms. I’d say that the Rallon’s low weight and in-check geometry work well to carry speed through rolling terrain and jump tracks, for the most part. I found the rear shock tends to push through the middle of the stroke far too easily, leaning towards comfort over support. As noted in the setup, I resorted to the lower leverage rate and kept upping the rear shock pressure for more support. Closing up the low-speed compression only made a minor improvement to that diving effect when you needed to pump the bike. A coil shock or a firmer tune could potentially help here, but I didn't venture down that rabbit hole. When combined, the lack of mid-stroke support and “square” geometry left me with reservations about fully opening it up on the Rallon. The bike can obviously hang with other World Cup winners, in the right hands, but it’s not as relaxed as some other downhill sleds I've ridden in the past twelve months. I found the need to ride further back behind the bike to keep the confidence up. Deciding where the front wheel should go takes more awareness through the chunder and down steep chutes, rather than pressing autopilot and letting it fly. After riding back-to-back laps, the benefits are clear: extra weight keeps the bike from being deflected. Conversely, it takes more intent to redirect the bike, particularly in the tight, steep chicanes. Whether that’s a benefit or a hindrance may depend on how aggressive a rider you are. For instance, World Cup DH riders are strapping hefty amounts of lead to their frames (in some cases, three times as much weight). I gather that most gravity riders will appreciate the grounded sense and confidence with the weights tacked onto the otherwise lightweight package. More than one rider inquired about the saddle position brought on by the steep seat tube angle, normally found on enduro bikes. I can’t recall any instances where the seat posed a mobility issue or felt out of place. If anything, being able to slam the saddle low and further forward may help shorter riders move about the bike with ease. On the topic of comfort, the frame flex is contrasting. The front triangle is firm and precise - on the brutish side, yet not so rigid you get rattled to pieces. At the rear, it’s the polar opposite. A bridgeless seatstay flexes and follows the ground well, possibly too easily for some riders’ tastes. Some tire marks on the paint can be seen inside the rear triangle, and on several occasions I had to tighten the rear axle after it worked its way loose. All of those flex characteristics come with subjectivity, but one thing that's inarguable are the noises heard throughout the frame. The Rallon DH needs some work in this department. It’s not up to scratch with the current crop of DH bikes. One culprit is the internal cable routing, both through the downtube and the chainstay. The other is the Saint derailleur - the chain goes on its own journey, flailing excessively. More foam insulation, a couple of STFU chain dampers, and an Ochain can calm down those drivetrain tantrums. The other issue is the Gravity Link cover. I'd happily swap this for a guard that bolts on to the Gravity Link mounting points, since that has to be removed to make any shock changes anyhow. Technical Report Shimano Saint shiftset: The Saint shifting performance is still there, however, further advances from SRAM and TRP have been made to calm down drivetrain noise. The pivoting B-knucle on the Saint derailleur makes the Rallon louder than it should be. I’d also like higher gears - either a 10-tooth on the cassette or 36 36-tooth ring would be appreciated when you need an extra crank into a high-speed gap jump. Shimano XTR brakes w/ Galfer pads and Shark rotors: There’s a new XTR model on the block, but these are still a solid performer. If you’re not a fan of the power curve of Shimano brakes, you might find this setup to be more intuitive. Galfer rotors and pads smooth out the sudden spike in power that Shimano levers can initiate. At the same time, I’ve found they fade less. They still offer that extra bite after longer periods of dragging the brakes. Oquo MC32 rims: These alloy wheels may have been developed for enduro, but the FMD Orbea Team has been racing downhill on them this year with positive reports. In the Whistler Bike Park, they’ve withstood their fair share of bashing with minimal attention. They’re still rolling round and straight, which is better than most alloy downhill rims fare after a summer-long thrashing. Which Model is the Best Value? As it stands, the D-LTD complete bike is built with an adequate parts list. Orbea hit the main talking points well with premium suspension, hopped-up brakes, and reliable wheels. Comparably, those components stack up well against competitors’ price tags. Personally, I’d take advantage of the MyO custom bike builder and choose a truly unique Rallon from the ground up, paint included. That would start with a frame kit, which costs $4,299. To keep the price moderate, I’d be tempted to start off with a Fox DHX2 coil shock, then grab a SRAM XO1 shift set, TRP Evo Pro brakes, plus the Burgtec cockpit mentioned earlier. We could go on and on, but I’d be satisfied with the rest of the components from the D-LTD build. Orbea Rallon DH: $8,299 USD / Frame kit: $4,299 (w/Fox Float X2) Santa Cruz V10: $8,899 USD / Frame kit: $3,949 (w/Fox DHX2) How Does It Compare? If we’re going to compare it to a staple downhill bike that everyone knows and loves, the obvious candidate is a Santa Cruz V10. This equally expensive carbon downhill bike is a people pleaser. Not only are there meaningful geometry adjustments that make it adaptable enough to suit racers and freeriders alike, but each size is far more tunable in terms of fit. Santa Cruz also publishes specific damper adjustments to start the rider off with a base setting. The suspension is plush, supportive, and shakes off hard landings. What more could you want? One edge that the Rallon might have on the V10 is compliance, depending on what you’re looking for. I’d use the word “strong” to sum up the V10’s frame compliance, whereas the Rallon’s softer rear triangle doesn’t step out in the rough chatter as easily. Pros + Low center of gravity glues the bike to corners. + Unique frame construction; auxiliary enduro setup possible, on-board tool, and in-frame storage. + Lightweight, even with purposefully added weights. Cons - Making shock adjustments is time-consuming. - Rear suspension tends to push through mid-stroke when support is needed. - Noisey drivetrain, cable routing, and downtube guard. Pinkbike's Take Orbea’s two-in-one Rallon concept is ambitious and clever. The bike packs in plenty of unique features that set it apart from the current crop of downhill bikes. At the same time, I'd like to see better derailleur and brake line silencing inside the frame, and a more secure Gravity-Link cover. Still, many downhill riders will appreciate how grounded, light, and comfortable the Rallon feels on the trail. — Matt Beer Visit the high-res gallery for more images from this review. Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews DH Bikes Orbea Orbea Rallon Author Info: Matt Beer Squamish, BC, Canada Member since Mar 16, 2001 607 articles Matt has been a Pinkbike reader since the site's early days. He discovered mountain biking in Newfoundland, where a small community of dedicated riders and trail builders set him on a path to earn two Canadian National Downhill Championship titles. Now based in Squamish, BC, Matt works as a tech editor for Pinkbike, specializing in gravity-focused mountain bike reviews, tech, and racing coverage.

title: Review: The New Pivot Firebird  is Well Balanced & Refined
author: Seb Stott
article_text: V5 Pivot Firebird review.mp4 https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/TTPAPn0t Copied Loading ad Live 00:25 00:00 06:04 “Don’t mess it up!” That was the brief from Pivot’s enduro team for the V5 Firebird. The new bike is more about evolution than revolution, with none of the suspension or drivetrain weirdness developed for their Phoenix downhill bike. It has tweaked geometry and suspension, plus impressive adjustability and storage features that Pivot hopes will take their race-winning enduro platform up a notch. The Firebird is designed to be a capable enduro race bike, with 165 mm of rear travel and a 170mm fork. But it’s more versatile than those numbers would suggest thanks to impressive pedalling efficiency and balanced geometry that make it happy to cover ground and rewarding on flatter trails. It’s also got room to carry more snacks and tools than most of its rivals. Pivot Firebird Details • Wheel size: 29" or MX compatible • 165 mm rear travel, 170mm fork • 63.8° head angle, 77° seat angle • Size specific & adjustable chainstay • Three bottle bosses & downtube storage • Sizes: XS, S, M, L, XL • Claimed frame weight from 6.18 lbs / 2.8 kg • Actual weight as tested: 16.1 kg / 35.5 lb • Lifetime original owner frame warranty & bearing replacement • Price: $6,499 to £13,699 ($8,699 as tested) • pivotcycles.com While the V5 Firebird isn't a radical redesign, the subtle refinements seem to pay off on the trail. Contents Introduction Construction & Features Suspension Design Geometry & Sizing Setup Climbing Descending Value How Does it Compare? Pros & Cons Pinkbike's Take Everything from the suspension to the geometry feels well-balanced and refined. — Seb Stott Frame Details The most obvious change to the frame is the addition of storage under the bottle cage. The downtube is large enough to swallow a regular inner tube plus a pouch for tools and snacks. The door is particularly well-designed with a secure yet easy-to-use latch that doesn’t rattle even with a full-sized water bottle attached. There's also a magnet to hold a master link on the underside of the door. There are also three bosses under the top tube that are intended to carry tools but, at least on the XL frame, can accommodate a Fidlock water bottle in addition to a full-size bottle on the downtube. There are two more bosses under the downtube plus a small door in front of the bottom bracket that is to assist with cable installation but could accommodate an emergency gel or two. This is one of the most adjustable enduro frames on the market. A clever flip chip at the rear axle allows the chainstay length to be increased by 8 mm. It’s one of a small number of frames that offer this feature while retaining SRAM’s UDH and Transmission compatibility. Interestingly, Pivot made use of a patent they had for a similar swinging dropout used on their fat bike. Even though the drive side dropout rotates when the chainstay length is extended, Pivot say there’s no need to adjust the B-tension. However, they do recommend using a chain with one more link with the longer setting to stay within SRAM and Shimano’s chain length recommendations. That makes it more of a set-and-forget feature than something to chop and change. The chainstay length is also size-specific, with 7 mm separating a small and XL. This difference is accommodated with the front triangle only – the pivot locations change slightly relative to the bottom bracket – so the rear triangle and links are common to all sizes. This is nowhere near enough to maintain the ratio of front-centre to rear-centre across the sizes. But combined with the adjustability, there is a 15 mm difference between the smallest and longest combination, which is enough to notice. More specifically, the chainstay length in the small sizes is particularly compact, a plus for short riders who struggle to fully unweight the front wheel. The long setting in the XL size (tested) is not especially long. A second flip chip adjusts the bottom bracket height by 5 mm and the frame angles by 0.4 degrees. This isn’t enough to keep the geometry the same with 29” or 27.5” wheels, so it is lower and slacker with a mullet setup. Pivot say either setting can be used with either wheel size, but the high setting is strongly recommended with MX wheels. Yet another flip chip at the lower shock mount alters the leverage curve for a more linear or progressive suspension feel. As if that weren't enough, the oversized ZS56/56 headset makes it possible to install an aftermarket reach adjust or head angle adjust headset. While the rear triangle is the same for all sizes, the front triangle’s carbon layup is "tuned" so taller riders have a stiffer frame and smaller ones get more flex. Cables are clipped to the inside of the downtube rather than running through tubes within the tube; this makes it easier to change brake routing depending on which side of the handlebars you want the rear brake to go. As you would hope, the cables come out of ports beside the head tube and not through the headset. It's fair to assume that the downtube storage and flip chips will increase the frame's weight compared to the outgoing model. The 2022 version weighed 1 kg less when we tested it , albeit with some lighter parts and a smaller frame size. Fair to say the new Firebird isn't the lightest, but it's hardly heavy by modern enduro standards. Out back, Pivot are sticking with the SuperBoost 157 mm rear axle standard, which they say makes a 30% stiffer and more reliable wheel. An obvious downside of this is that there are fewer replacement wheel options than the 148 standard, but on the other hand, the SuperBoost wheel is generally interchangeable with those on DH bikes, which could come in handy for those who own a DH and enduro bike. The rear triangle is shaped to provide maximum heel clearance, and Pivot say this is actually easier to achieve with a SuperBoost rear end. It also uses a press-fit bottom bracket. According to Pivot’s Chris Cocalis, “Press fit doesn’t creak; poor tolerances creak.” Pivot say their manufacturing process ensures reliably precise alignment. There are three colors to choose from: White Gold, Yolo (yellow) and limited edition Purple Galaxy. The frame is covered by a lifetime first-owner warranty and a lifetime bearing warranty – if the pivot bearings wear out they’ll send a new set for free (fitting not included). Suspension Design Pivot did consider using a six-bar suspension layout like their Phoenix downhill bike for the Firebird, but decided any marginal benefits on the descents were more than offset in terms of climbing and complexity. Caption Settings Dialog Beginning of dialog window. Escape will cancel and close the window. Text Color White Black Red Green Blue Yellow Magenta Cyan Transparency Opaque Semi-Transparent Background Color Black White Red Green Blue Yellow Magenta Cyan Transparency Opaque Semi-Transparent Transparent Window Color Black White Red Green Blue Yellow Magenta Cyan Transparency Transparent Semi-Transparent Opaque Font Size 50% 75% 100% 125% 150% 175% 200% 300% 400% Text Edge Style None Raised Depressed Uniform Dropshadow Font Family Proportional Sans-Serif Monospace Sans-Serif Proportional Serif Monospace Serif Casual Script Small Caps Defaults Done Pivot Firebird V5 suspension by seb-stott Add to Favorites Added to Favorites Views: 2,749 Faves: 1 Comments: 0 The DW4 design offers consistent levels of anti-squat, which helps the bike to pedal with minimal unwanted bob or slouching across a wide sag window. It also provides consistent anti-rise levels, which could make braking behaviour more consistent. The overall result is a bike that resists pitching whether pedalling or braking, so the chassis remains stable and centred in its travel. Anti-squat. Anti-rise. The flip chip at the lower shock mount offers two options for frame progressivity: 32% when the eyelet is mounted towards the rear and 27% towards the front, when measured from 0 to 100% of travel. The more progressive setting is similar to the old Firebird and is more progressive than most without being an outlier. This is the setting I used most and found the balance of support and bottom-out resistance to be about right with the single green volume spacer that comes installed with the shock. Pivot say the suspension travel is barely affected by the progressivity fip chip or the high/low setting, but switching to the longer chainstay will increase travel by a few millimetres. I measured the real-world travel on my test bike at a shade over 170 mm in the long setting. Geometry & Sizing Compared to the previous Firebird, the new model is 5 mm lower, 0.2 degrees slacker and with a slightly steeper seat tube in the smaller sizes. The charts above show the geometry in the stock configuration: the low setting with 29” wheels and the high setting with MX wheels. By my measurements, the seat angle at my pedalling height measures 76.5 degrees in the low setting. That's slightly slacker than claimed, but shorter riders will get a steeper position because of the offset seat tube. Note the XS is available as a mullet only. Pivot's sizing guidelines are as follows: X-Small: 4'11"–5'4" | 1,50–1,63 m Small: 5'2"–5’8” | 1,57–1,73m Medium: 5'9"–6' | 1,73–1,83m Large: 5'11"–6’4” | 1,80–1,93m X-Large: 6'1"+ | 1,85m+ At 6’3” / 191 cm, I found the XL to be a great fit, but, interestingly, Pivot's guidelines wouldn't rule out the large. Bear in mind that the reach can be adjusted by +/- 5 mm with an aftermarket headset . Specifications Release Date 2025 Price $8699 Travel 165 mm Rear Shock 2026 Fox Factory Float X2 Fork Fox Factory 38, GRIP X2 - 170mm Headset Pivot Precision Sealed Cartridge 56/56 Cassette Sram X0 1295 Eagle Transmission 10-52t Crankarms Sram X0 Eagle DUB 32t - 165mm (XS-SM), 170mm (MD-XL) Chainguide Pivot upper guide Bottom Bracket SRAM DUB PF92 Pedals N/A Rear Derailleur Sram X0 Eagle Transmission 12-Speed Chain Sram X0 Eagle Transmission Flattop Front Derailleur N/A Shifter Pods Sram AXS Pod Controller Handlebar Phoenix Team Carbon - 20mm Rise 760mm (XS), 20mm Rise 780mm (SM), 35mm Rise 780mm (MD-XL) Stem Phoenix Team Enduro V2- 32mm (XS), 40mm (SM-LG), 50mm (XL) Grips Phoenix Factory Lock-On Brakes Sram Maven Silver, 200 mm rotors Wheelset DT Swiss XM1700 - Mixed or 29” Hubs DT Swiss 350, 36t Star Ratchet Spokes DT Swiss, 32 Rim 30 mm, alloy Tires Front: Continental Kryptotal-F 29” x 2.4” Soft Compound Enduro Casing Rear: Continental Kryptotal-R 27.5” or 29” x 2.4” Soft Compound Enduro Casing Seat S, SM: Phoenix WTB Pro High Tail Trail MD-XL: Phoenix WTB Pro Volt Seatpost Fox Transfer Factory, 125-150mm (XS), 155-180mm (SM), 185-210mm (MD-LG), 215-240mm (XL) Compare to other All Mountain/Enduro/XC One less-obvious spec highlight is Pivot’s own carbon bar, which they say matches the benchmark compliance of OneUp’s carbon handlestick. Test Bike Setup Testing kicked off with four full days of shuttle laps in Finale Ligure, followed by a handful of rides on familiar soggier trails in Scotland’s Tweed Valley. Pivot provides a handy sag indicator, which makes initial setup much easier than measuring and calculating sag with a non-RockShox shock. This corresponds to 33% shock sag, but I soon added 10 psi to the shock, which left me with 19 mm (30%) sag. I also removed one of the two volume spacers from the fork to balance out the suspension feel and stuck with the single green spacer in the shock. After playing around with most of the geometry adjustments, I settled on the longer chainstay, low flip-chip setting and more progressive suspension. At 190 cm / 6’3”, the XL size fit me well. I did swap the 50 mm stem for a 40 mm, but I was happy with either on this bike. Tire pressures were 23 psi & 27 psi in Finale and 22 psi and 26 psi in Scotland. Seb Stott Location: Tweed Valley, Scotland Age: 32 Height: 6'3" / 191cm Inseam: 37" / 93cm Weight: 187 lbs / 85 kg, kitted Testing Info I rode Little Champery in Finale Ligure seven times thanks to shuttling from Finale Bikers. This allowed me to compare most of the Firebird's features (BB height, chainstay length and stem length) back-to-back. It's also a trail where the bike felt particularly impressive, both in the sweeping turns up top and the rough rock gardens at the bottom. Climbing The Firebird is particularly stable and efficient when pedalling. The suspension is remarkably still whether spinning or sprinting, but it feels especially good when accelerating out of the saddle. It's not the supplest when pedalling over bumpy terrain, but it’s not terrible; the suspension does a decent job of conforming to the terrain while still staying on top of its travel. My only criticism is the seat angle. It’s designed to avoid putting the rider too far over the front wheel for riding the rocky traverses around Pivot’s Phoenix HQ, but for me, the position feels a touch off the back for the brutally steep, but not especially complicated climbs we have in the Tweed Valley. With the saddle slammed all the way forward it’s not far off my ideal position, and the bike still climbs well because the suspension stays high in its travel, but I think the XL could do with having the seatpost a little more upright. After all, how many people have their saddle slammed all the way backwards ? Descending The stable suspension platform continues on the descents. The bike feels sturdy and solid, perhaps helped by a stiff wheel and frame package, and the suspension is well-damped and measured. It doesn’t give away travel unduly and never wallows. There’s excellent cornering support and a responsive feel that belies the travel and works particularly well in supportive turns. At the same time, the suspension responds well to small bumps and tames the most hectic terrain. It feels like there's plenty of travel in reserve and gets more impressive at higher speeds and on harder hits. It just doesn’t cosset and cushion you as much as some enduro bikes (the Deviate Claymore comes to mind). Having said that, there is a lot of scope to adjust the suspension feel with damping, volume spacers and the frame’s flip chip, so if you want a more pillowy feel that's what it's there for. In the stock setup with one volume spacer and 28-30% sag, I found the end of the travel only on the biggest hucks-to-flat and sometimes felt the bottom-out bumper doing its thing towards the end of the stroke. But it’s not a dramatic or harsh ramp: it’s more a soft catch for when you’re getting silly. The handling is similarly well-judged. The XL’s wheelbase is stable without being overly stretched out, which retains some of the agility you want for enduro-style riding. But the generous stack height and 35 mm bar offer a roomy cockpit out of the box. I started with the shorter chainstay setting and found it to be easy to manoeuvre without feeling out of touch with the front tire in loose, open corners. Swapping to the longer setting isn’t the night and day difference some might imagine. In a back-to-back test, the difference is subtle, but it adds a little confidence in long-radius turns. The change to the suspension is probably more noticeable if the shock isn’t adjusted to compensate for the extra leverage - it felt slightly more supple. It takes a little more force to initiate a manual, but to me this isn’t a downside so much as a difference. The high setting with 29" wheels offers extra clearance for tight awkward sections and doesn’t detract from cornering confidence when you commit to the turn, but to me it made the bike feel more nervous on fast, steep sections. Pedal clearance is ample even in the low setting. Again, it's a relatively subtle change. The Firebird is a solid-feeling bike in rough and technical terrain yet remains responsive and efficient on flatter sections. It’s more on the sporty rather than squishy side, but it’s never harsh or nervous. It feels like an appropriate tool for a wide range of riding from DH shuttles to trail rides. Don't say there aren't enough options. What's the Best Value? It's hard to justify spending $2,200 more than the starting price as the "best value" option, but I think if value-for-money is your primary concern you'd be better served elsewhere. The Firebird is an unashamedly bougie bike whichever model you choose. With that in mind, I think the Pro X0 Eagle Transmission build that I tested makes the most sense relative to its rivals. It has the top-spec suspension components that you want in a posh enduro bike without the Live Valve or carbon cranks this bike doesn't need. As if there aren't enough options in the chart above (I had to shorten the model names from Transmission to TR to fit them all in), the Pro and Team models can be upgraded to Reynolds Blacklabel Enduro Pro carbon wheels for an additional $1,200. But I wouldn't bother with that. All in all, there are 64 combinations of spec options, colour and wheel sizes available. Also, Europeans will have a choice of Newmen wheels instead of DT or Reynolds. Pivot Firebird Scott Ransom How Does It Compare? The Firebird fits into an elusive category of big-hitting enduro bikes that are happy to climb and don’t feel out of place on mellower trails. Some other bikes on that list would be the Merida One-Sixty, Canyon Strive, Orbea Rallon and Scott Ransom. The Pivot wins practicality points for its non-headset cable routing, impressive warranty, superior storage options and helpful adjustability. It’s probably the best-pedalling bike on that list with the shock left open too, although the Merida’s steeper seat tube gives it an edge when the climb points skyward. When descending, the Pivot feels most like the Scott, with a stout-feeling frame and well-controlled suspension. I would say the Pivot’s suspension is more consistent and predictable, perhaps thanks to the 2026 X2 shock. On descending performance alone I would put the Firebird a hair ahead of that particular pack, although the Merida and Canyon are considerably more affordable. Technical Report Fox Transfer Seatpost: My test bike came with a 210 mm seatpost which was a little shy about returning to full height. It works fine if the lever is pressed fully and the saddle is unobstructed but if the saddle catches on your shorts or the lever is half-pressed it gets stuck a few centimetres from top-out. This is a little disappointing for a brand-new high-end post. SRAM Maven Silver brakes: While the power and lever feel are excellent, the rear brake on my bike had an inconsistent bite point out of the box that persisted even after multiple re-bleeds. Following SRAM's piston massage procedure has since solved this (at least for now). Continental Enduro casing tires: I’ve spent a lot of time on the DH casing Kryptotals but the enduro casings impressed. They stood up to four days of shuttling in Finale and provided predictable grip on Italian rocks and Scottish mud alike. They do squirm noticeably more than the DH version, but they are appreciably lighter and faster-rolling. I would prefer the SuperSoft compound up front though. Pros + Well-balanced overall ride and handling rarely feels out of place + Superb pedalling efficiency adds verstaility + Impressive frame storage and adjustability + Lifetime frame and bearing warranty Cons - Expensive - Seat angle could be steeper, at least for the XL size - Super Boost rear axle limits wheel options Pinkbike's Take Mountain bikes are all about fine-tuning and subtle tweaks these days. The new Firebird isn’t a game-changer that’s head and shoulders ahead of its rivals; nor is it radically different to its predecessor. But it does ride like an extremely well-oiled machine. Everything from the suspension to the geometry feels well-balanced and refined. It also offers exceptional storage and adjustability which give it a USP. Its impressive pedalling efficiency makes it a good choice for those who want a capable enduro machine that’s got your back on the toughest terrain but doesn't sap the life out of mellower trails or punchy climbs. — Seb Stott Photos: Carlo De Santis Visit the high-res gallery for more images from this review. Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Enduro Bikes Pivot Pivot Firebird Author Info: Seb Stott Innerleithen, Scotland Member since Dec 29, 2014 526 articles Seb's been working as a mountain bike technical editor since 2015, and has reviewed hundreds of bikes and components in that time. Before that, he studied experimental physics at the University of St Andrews, where he got into the habit of measuring everything possible. Based in the Tweed Valley, Scotland, he loves riding trail, enduro and e-bikes, and is happiest when testing them back-to-back to see how they compare.

title: Review: Norco Fluid FS Carbon - A Comfortable Companion
author: Dario DiGiulio
article_text: REVIEW Norco Fluid FS C1 WORDS: Dario DiGiulio PHOTOS: Dario DiGiulio / Eric Mickelson Norco released the latest version of the alloy Fluid as the bike for "every rider, every trail," and hit the nail on the head when it came to the spec, geometry, overall performance, and ultimately, cost. Priced between $2,000 and $4,000 USD, it really hit a sweet spot for many riders. That made the introduction of the Fluid Carbon a bit puzzling, given the big jump in cost relative to the performance gains, but assumptions aren't why we're here. The carbon front triangle does save 600 grams compared to the alloy, while also providing some improved characteristics on trail. With a 130/140mm travel layout, 29" wheels front and rear, and a carbon front triangle mounted to an aluminum rear, the Fluid FS Carbon still aims to be the bike for every rider and every trail, just with a lighter overall package and fancier accoutrement. Fluid Carbon Details • 29" wheels • 130mm frame travel, 140mm fork • 65° head angle • 430-535mm reach (510mm size XL) • Size-dependent chainstay length (440mm size XL) • 77° seat tube angle, size dependent • 4 sizes available, XL tested • Weight: 32.9 lb / 14.9 kg (XL) • Price: $5,999 USD • norco.com Contents Introduction Construction & Features Geometry & Sizing Suspension Design Specifications Setup Climbing Descending Technical Report What's the Best Value? How Does it Compare? Pros & Cons Pinkbike's Take Easy going, right out of the gate. The Fluid's geometry puts you in a confident, neutral body position, and the suspension is there to keep things in shape as you start to push the speed and rally through rougher terrain. — Dario DiGiulio Frame Details It's the Fluid, but in carbon! Well, not entirely. The Fluid's Carbon and Alloy variants are identical, save for the front triangle, which in this case is made of those luxurious black fibers we've grown so used to. Otherwise, the rear ends are both aluminum, with the same linkage parts, hardware, geometry, and kinematic. With the carbon front triangle come some little creature comforts to suit the fancier frame. The internal cable routing has well-designed gizmos to clamp them at the lower exit, as well as multiple plug options for the top entry to suit whatever cable orientation you're using, be it moto brakes, wireless everything, or some other elusive third option you've cooked up. There's a small but elegant detail on the chainstay, with two exit ports at the rear end to suit either SRAM or Shimano drivetrains, as they both function best with a certain cable orientation. I'd personally prefer external routing, but well-thought-out executions like this make internal bikes much easier to live with. Like the alloy bike, there is a UDH hanger out back, excellent dropper insertion, and a simple yet effective 4-bar suspension layout. Geometry & Sizing Norco has an excellent grasp on geometry and sizing, and the Fluid is a fine example of that understanding. Via their Ride Aligned sizing concept, the bikes scale proportionally with each size jump, to assure you're not simply stretching out the reach and hoping everything else falls into place. In the case of the Fluid, the geo is optimized for all-around trail riding, with a definite bias towards steeper terrain, thanks in large part to a high front end. The four sizes available all share a 65° head angle, 30mm bottom bracket drop, and all fall between 76° and 77° in the seat angle, with the larger sizes erring on the steeper side. Reach numbers grow by 30mm per size, starting at 420mm and ending at 510mm; head tubes grow by 10mm, and stack by 9mm. Chainstays are also size-specific, getting 5mm longer with each jump, with 425mm shorties on the Small and a well-balanced 440mm on the Extra Large. You can generally rely on the Ride Aligned system to dial in your setup, find fit information, and even choose a bike size. At 6'3", the sizing calculator firmly places me on an XL, with a 510mm reach and 644mm stack. I abided to the robot's assertions, and though the bike fit well, I would have been just as happy on the size Large. I feel like this comes up in just about every review, but I'm generally a fan of erring on the small size when I'm caught between two options, as my local terrain benefits that sort of fit and handling. Be that as it may, the XL was comfortable, roomy, and well balanced feeling, with handling that still felt plenty intuitive. More on that later in the ride impressions. Suspension Design The Fluid relies on a 210x55mm shock to deliver 130mm of rear wheel travel, driven by a fairly typical 4-bar layout. Norco is striving for anything but typical when it comes to the suspension performance, however, with a serious effort putting in to balancing the factors to deliver a well-rounded ride characteristic. Norco is tight-lipped when it comes to any sort of graphical suspension information, but their engineers were willing to divulge their parameters and design goals for some of the kinematics. On Leverage: One of our goals for the Fluid was to rely less on air spring progression for support by adding progression to the leverage curve to get a better balance of support from air spring and compression damping. In order to do this while keeping shock pressures in check for heavier riders and hit 130mm of rear travel, we needed to bump up shock stroke. The Fluid runs a 210x50 where as the Optic runs a 190x45 shock. This let us bump overall progression significantly on the Fluid compared to the Optic. The leverage curve progression is also very consistent through travel and doesn’t become regressive in end stroke like you sometimes see. We’ve found a curve with consistent progression through travel helps with shock tuning and provides a consistent feel as you push into the bike. On Anti-Squat: We look at anti-squat, leverage curve and damper tune as all contributing to the pedaling characteristics of the bike. Getting more support from the leverage curve and damping circuit helps to support the rider under acceleration and lets us reduce anti-squat while still having a bike that pedals well. It can be a bit of a different feel to bikes with high anti-squat that may have a bit more get up and go under power on smooth trails but we’ve found our approach to give a good balance between an efficient feel on smooth trails while providing traction for technical climbing. Like the alloy Fluid before it, the Fluid Carbon's shock has been tuned to match the characteristics of this specific kinematic. By no means is this unusual, but it's worth keeping in mind if you're going to be "upgrading" and swapping shocks right out of the gate. Specifications Price $5999 Travel 140mm Rear Shock RockShox Super Deluxe Ultimate, 210x50mm Fork RockShox Pike Ultimate, 140mm Headset FSA Sealed Bearing Cassette SRAM 1275 T-Type, 10-52T Crankarms SRAM GX DUB T-Type, 30T Bottom Bracket SRAM DUB BSA Threaded Rear Derailleur SRAM GX Eagle AXS T-Type Chain SRAM GX Eagle T-Type Shifter Pods SRAM Pod Controller with MMX Handlebar One Up Carbon, 800mm, 20mm Rise Stem 6061 Alloy, 40mm Length, 35mm Clamp Grips WTB Wavelength Brakes SRAM G2 RSC, Organic Pads Wheelset Crank Brothers Synthesis Enduro Alloy Tires Vittoria Mazza 2.4 / Martello 2.35" Seat Fizik Alpaca Terra Seatpost SDG Tellis VS Compare to other All Mountain/Enduro/XC RIDING THE FLUID FS CARBON Test Bike Setup Thanks to the Ride Aligned system, most of my settings were very easy to find right out of the gate. Norco's setup recommendations are still the best I've encountered in the market, and offer granularity that other calculators don't even consider. Sure, bar width, tire pressure, and stem length might be second nature to experienced riders, but to some these are all daunting steps to take in getting used to setting up a mountain bike's many variables. I have a good amount of experience with all the components featured on the C1 build, and generally found myself pretty aligned (ha) with the settings Norco recommended. The calculator recommended 84psi in the fork, I ran 90. It suggested 214psi in the shock, and I ended up between 210 and 215. If anything, it's a great starting place to get rolling and comfortable on a new ride. Dario DiGiulio Location: Bellingham, WA, USA Height: 6'3" / 191cm Inseam: 34" / 86cm Weight: 180 lbs / 81.6 kg Industry affiliations / sponsors: None Instagram: @danger_dario Climbing In fear of making this a theme of the review, I'm going to characterize the Fluid's climbing character as neutral . I suppose saying that is kind of a cop-out, but it just strikes a nice balance between grip and support that makes for a pleasant time over varying terrain. It doesn't necessarily urge you to hammer on the pedals at any sign of free speed, but instead offers a comfortable and capable platform to pick through tech sections and motor up fire roads alike. I found the body position comfortable, even on the 510mm reach bike, which is pretty much the limit of my comfort range. Thanks to the ample rear center, long reach, and high stack, you're really in the middle of the bike, which makes body English a bit less necessary in moderately technical sections, but can sometimes be a handful if you're really trying to muscle the bike around. I didn't run into any major frustrations with this, but simply noticed that there was more bike to move when it was tight and picky. I'm not going to make any claims about noticing the weight savings of the carbon frame, because 600 grams - though a significant chunk - isn't enough to separate an XC whippet from a big beefy enduro rig. Imagine a water bottle that you've taken a few sips out of, then imagine an empty one; that's the difference. That said, if you're pining for the lightest Fluid in all the land, then the carbon frame is obviously a good start. Fun fact: the lightest fluid is actually hydrogen, but you'll have to cool it down to -253°C to get that gas to change state. Descending Easy going, right out of the gate. The Fluid's geometry puts you in a confident, neutral body position, and the suspension is there to keep things in shape as you start to push the speed and rally through rougher terrain. Those fundamentals are key to a good bike, and in this case they're identical to the preexisting aluminum-framed Fluid. As to whether the carbon frame offers a significant upgrade to that platform, I have a harder time saying so definitively. I enjoyed my time on both forms of Fluid, so don't let this come across as disparagement, it's just simply a matter of choosing between your preferred frame material. We did thorough testing of that alloy Fluid in the last trail bike focused Field Test , and it performed favorably in a group of bikes that were uniformly more expensive and exotic. The carbon Fluid is still the same bike, though that relative cost factor is lost a bit. Some might find the frame feel of the carbon front end to really elevate their experience, but for me the two were similar enough that I wouldn't be too biased one way or the other. Don't consider this a rebuke on carbon in general, Norco just did a great job of making their alloy frame stiff enough and deliver a comfortable ride feel. I wouldn't call the Fluid an wildly exciting bike, it's more in the capable and controlled camp. You can certainly pop, jib, and play around on trail just as much as other similar trail bikes, but its character biases more towards handling rough sections of terrain than returning all the energy you put into the pedals. Overall, there's something almost blasé about how effective the Fluid is - it just gets along with anything, without any real shortcomings in any specific direction. At the risk of sounding like Levy, sometimes you want a bike that excites you a bit, but in this case the bike is happier to keep you on the line and in control. Climbing and descending both feel well-controlled and capable, giving the bike a predictable feel regardless of what you throw at it. Norco Fluid Carbon Transition Smuggler How does it compare? These two bikes are very similar on paper, with geometry, travel, wheel size, and intended terrain all hitting the same notes. There are small differences in bottom bracket drop, stack, and seat tube angle, but ultimately it comes down to ride feel, value, and on-trail feel. The Smuggler feels like an energetic and zippy bike, up and down the hill, whereas the Fluid is a bit more composed and muted in rough terrain. That might just come down to the Fox vs. RockShox feel difference on the two builds I tested. If weight is your ultimate goal, then the Smuggler's all-carbon frame is going to be a better option for getting the grams as low as you can. I'd have a hard time choosing an all-out preference between the two, and ultimately the decision would probably come down to value, where the Norco handily wins. Equivalent builds are thousands of dollars cheaper, and any outstanding component you'd want to upgrade could be done well within those savings. Which Model is the Best Value? This might be cheating, but I have to give the value award to the highest end Fluid A1 build. Yes, it's not a Fluid Carbon, per se, but the bike is essentially the same beast, and the value is much higher. For just under four thousand American dollars, you get a very capable mountain bike, suited to beginner or seasoned rider alike. In comparison to the highest end Carbon build, I still prefer some of the components on the Alloy model - 34 over Pike, TRP Trail EVO over SRAM G2. The difference between the two is less than a pound, and again, the frame's performance is pretty much identical. If I were constrained to the carbon options, I'd probably opt for the well-priced $2,099 frame kit (shock included), and build it up myself with all the parts my heart desired. Technical Report SRAM Transmission GX AXS Drivetrain: Still great, and particularly unfussy on this bike. The shifts were always good, the bike remained quiet, and the drivetrain does feel like a performance upgrade over other offerings. Whether it's worth the overall build's price hike, I'm a little more skeptical, but having that consistent and functional base does make the rest of the bike feel all the more dialed. SRAM G2 RSC Brakes: I do not like them, Sam-I-Am. The G2s are fine, slowing you down with enough bite and some power on tap if you really reef on the levers, but they just don't offer the kind of performance modern trail bikes deserve. This build also came with SRAM's organic pads, which are quiet and have good initial bite, but fall short on long descents and are quite scary in the wet. I'd consider these the first upgrade item on the bike. Vittoria Mazza / Martello Trail Tires: With a casing feel similar to Maxxis' EXO, and moderately sticky rubber, these tires are fine in mellower terrain, but pretty under-gunned as you start to push the bike in hard terrain and conditions. They lack the support to hold up at low pressures, and the rubber compound isn't as sticky as the competition, especially in the wet. They are light, so you'll have a nice quick-feeling set for faster trail days if such a thing appeals. The Fluid can push hard enough to warrant some serious rubber though, so I'd recommend picking some up. Pros + Composed, comfortable ride quality + Well-rounded suspension performance + Excellent geometry and size-scaling Cons - Alloy bike rides just as well, presents better value - Grippy, comfortable climbing gives it more of a long-travel feel Pinkbike's Take The Fluid Carbon is a great trail bike, but so is the Fluid Alloy. If it were my choice I'd probably go with the latter, but luckily the choice is yours. Both offer well-controlled suspension performance, excellent geometry, and generally good components. There are many great trail bikes these days, but Norco really hit the nail on the head when it comes to ride feel and geometry for those who want to push their short-travel bike hard. — Dario DiGiulio Visit the high-res gallery for more images from this review. title: Field Test Review: 2025 Specialized Stumpjumper 15 Alloy
author: Dario DiGiulio
article_text: Specialized Stumpjumper https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/7aBFghTX Copied Loading ad Live 00:25 00:00 22:07 PINKBIKE FIELD TEST REVIEW Specialized Stumpjumper 15 Alloy Words by Dario DiGiulio; photography by Max Barron & Tom Richards The Stumpjumper is Specialized's now ubiquitous do-everything trail bike, with adjustability and adaptability integral to the bike's character. This year's update to the 15th iteration of the bike wasn't some massive overhaul, but more of a refinement, with very similar geometry to the outgoing Stumpjumper EVO, and the introduction of the Genie shock. While not necessarily required, this shock is core to the bike's purported improvement in suspension performance, and does achieve some impressive marks on paper. The Stumpjumper 15 was initially released as a carbon-only bike, which divisively only allowed for wireless shifting drivetrains. With the release of the alloy Stumpy came the option to run a cabled drivetrain, with all other features kept the same. There are an impressive six sizes to the Stumpjumper range, with significant geometry adjustments on tap for the end consumer. Stumpjumper 15 Comp Alloy Details • Aluminum frame • 145mm frame travel, 150mm fork • 29" or MX wheels • Horst link suspension • 64.5° head angle • 76.5° seat angle • Reach: 400, 425, 450, 475 (tested), 500, 530mm • Chainstay: 430, 432, 435, 435 (tested), 445, 445mm • Sizes: S1, S2, S3, S4 (tested), S5, S6 • Weight: 16.3 kg / 36 lb • Price: $4,500 USD • More info: specialized.com Head tube angle, bottom bracket height, and fit figures are all editable via a flip chip on the chainstay of the bike and a series of quick-change headset cups up front. If the full 29" setup isn't to your liking, the bike can be set up MX via the use of a different rocker link and rear wheel. Worth noting is the fact that the S1 and S2 bikes are mixed-wheel only, meaning the 29" rear wheel option is eliminated to keep geometry and fit more consistent. The geometry of the Stumpjumper scales nicely over the size range, with 25mm jumps in reach matched by a healthy growth in stack height (608, 618, 627, 640 tested , 654, 667mm) to keep the fit consistent. The chainstay length grows through the range as well, with essentially three rear center lengths splitting the range. This Stumpjumper 15 Comp Alloy is the least expensive bike in this test by a healthy margin, ringing up at $4,500 USD. For the price, you get a well-chosen parts spec, and the same adjustments and suspension performance as the highest-end models in the range, with prices nearly triple that of the bike we tested. As is often the case with bikes that hit impressive budget targets, the aluminum Stumpy isn't terribly light. Tipping the scales at 16.3kg / 36lbs, this is also our heaviest bike on test - but you'll need to read further to see how that shook out on trail. FIELD NOTES Climbing The Stumpjumper 15's updated suspension design touts big-bike travel feel, which isn't always good news for those who value climbing performance above all else. That said, the Stumpy's ability to hum through rough and technical terrain is very impressive, thanks in part to that active suspension feel. Rear wheel traction is plentiful, meaning you can focus on putting down power without worrying about spinning out when things get tricky. As terrain smooths out and climbs hit a more consistent gradient, the Stumpjumper isn't the most energetic of the bunch, due more to the active suspension and slack seat angle than the weight of the Comp Alloy bike we had on test. The climb switch will be a friend to some, though most of us opted to climb with the bike open in most circumstances. Those looking to maximize the pep in the new Stumpjumper platform would benefit from adding more spacers to the Genie, to reduce the extra volume and add some support through the main portion of the stroke. There's a tradeoff with grip, but that's to be expected. Descending The Stumpjumper can be a hard bike to pin down for a simple review given the breadth of adjustments and alterations you can make to the platform. That said, it seems to shine brightest in its stock setup, with the neutral headset orientation and high bottom bracket setting. No painting yourself into a corner, this is simply where the Stumpy feels most adaptable and ready to tackle varied terrain. The adjustments go deeper on the Stumpjumper 15 though, as shock setup plays a critical role in the dynamic ride quality of the bike. Put plainly, the Genie's wide setup window allows you to take the suspension feel all the way from taut and energetic all the way to deep and planted, all via the air can spacers. As above, too many variables to comment on all at once, but yet again the bike seems to shine in the stock setup. With one or two volume spacers in the extra volume chamber, the Stumpy has a very deep and gluey feeling suspension, much like a bike with a decent bit more travel might. The geometry and corresponding handling of the bike feels natural, intuitive, and well rounded, which lends itself to the varied riding one might expect to do on a capable trail bike. A few of us regularly referred to the bike as "safe" feeling, thanks to an easygoing nature that managed to extend even into some pretty gnarly sections of trail. With some work on the adjustment front, and after careful setup of the shock, I think the right rider could find great success racing this bike. As to that "right" rider, it can be hard to define, but a light touch seems to accentuate the bike's talents well. In this way, the Stumpjumper should work well for new riders and experienced folks alike, growing with that skillset and adapting well to changes in terrain. Technical Report Genie Shock: The Genie is a novel approach to shock design, relying on a large volume main air chamber, which effectively becomes a smaller volume at 70% of travel. While this transfer point is not adjustable, the volume of the two chambers is, meaning you can alter the spring curve of the bike as it goes through travel. That alteration requires some time, a shock pump, and ideally a repeatable section of trail where you can bracket through all the spacer settings to get a sense of which you prefer. This isn't required by any means, but like the other adjustments one can make to the bike, the shock setup is worth playing with in order to optimize the Stumpjumper for you. Specialized Alloy Wheelset: The components on these bikes suffered a hard two weeks in Whistler, wheels chief among them. The aluminum wheelset on the Stumpjupmer felt comfortable and held tension fairly well through testing, but wasn't quite as bombproof as others, suffering one nasty dent that required some attention. That said, considering the abuse the wheel suffered, this wasn't too big a deal, and remained rideable for the rest of the test. TRP Trail EVO Brakes: These brakes essentially flew under the radar throughout the test, but don't let that come across as a detraction of any sort. They were plenty powerful, with impressively consistent lever action and light feel. Though they're not the biggest brakes in TRP's lineup, they'd be one of the last components I'd opt to swap off this bike. What's it Like to Live With? The Stumpjumper fairs well in this category, with easy adjustments, very large in-frame storage, and a build in tool to make quick trailside fixes as need be. The flip chip procedure can be done trailside, and the head tube angle adjustment is an equally easy procedure, though you may need to swap in a different cup if you want to go with the ±1° option. We noticed a few creaks in the rear suspension linkage, but found that an occasional check to the bolt tightness kept that at bay. Pros + Suspension is impressively sensitive + Extensive adjustment to alter geometry, wheel size, and shock performance + Can descend like a bigger bike Cons - Quite heavy for a trail bike, due primarily to frame weight - Genie shock warrants extended setup testing - Can be hard to balance sensitivity and support The 2024 Trail Bike Field Test is presented by Rapha & Schwalbe Posted In: Reviews and Tech Field Test Reviews Trail Bikes Specialized Specialized Stumpjumper 15 Alloy Pinkbike Field Test Author Info: Dario DiGiulio Bellingham, WA, United States Member since Dec 25, 2016 484 articles Dario has been obsessed with bikes from an early age, and has worked in the industry since 2015. With a Bachelor's in Architecture from Cal Poly, San Luis Obispo, he aims to bring that mix of creativity and technicality to his work in the mountain bike world.

title: Review: Hope Tech 4 V4 Brakes - Strong and Sensitive
author: Dario DiGiulio
article_text: Hope Tech 4 Review https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/TPOtuzju Copied Loading ad Live 00:25 00:00 00:55 Hope Tech 4 V4 The heavily machined, often colorful look of Hope brakes always stand out on a bike, with a cult following devoted to the British brand's high-end stoppers. The Tech 4 V4 has been their benchmark gravity brake for some time now, earning a spot on DH, freeride, and enduro bikes alike. Though they've since introduced an additional heavy hitter in the gravity lineup - namely, the EVO GR4 - the Tech 4 V4 remains one of the best brakes money can buy. Read on for our long-term impressions, as well as some comparisons to the new GR4. V4 Details • DOT fluid • Adjustments: Reach, bite point • 5 pad options • Tested: Green Race pads, 2.3mm thick 200mm rotor • 2x18mm, 2x16mm pistons • Price: $628 USD (pair) • Brake weight: 285g (front) • Rotor weight: 222g (203mm) • hopetech.com BUY AT: Competitive Cyclist Setup Hope's bleed process is a bit less refined than the other options on test, requiring some additional faff to install the bleed cup atop the master cylinder. That said, it's a repeatable and time-tested method, with solid results and only some cleanup to worry about. The Hope-branded DOT fluid is easy enough to come by, and we found that they never needed a re-bleed after initial setup. There are five pad options and a few different rotors to choose from, opening up a wide array of tuning opportunities to the end user. As with all of the brakes on test, we asked the brand to send us the most powerful combination of both - in this case, that was the green Race pads, and their 2.3mm thick 200mm rotor, without the fancy floating aluminum carrier. The Tech 4 lever features both reach and bite point adjustment, which work somewhat dependently with each other, meaning you'll likely be trimming in one setting after altering the other. No complaints here, as the desired setup position is still easy to achieve, and a fairly short deadband can be achieved at any reach adjustment. One setup annoyance plagues the Tech 4 V4, and that has to do with the width of the caliper body. Hope's own website calls this out, and it's reflected in real life, where some bikes and forks struggle to accommodate the caliper's width. If your frame has an unusually tight rear brake area, or you're hoping to run the V4 with a 180mm rotor up front, it's best to check with Hope or do a test fit beforehand. Livability: The Hopes are some of the easiest to live with on test, thanks to a fuss-free hydraulic system, easy alignment, and solid adjustments. The caliper fitment could be an issue for some, but we experience no problems. Feel Hope achieved a very light lever action with the Tech 4 V4, but preserved a nice level of feedback, allowing the user to sense where they are in the stroke without much of a learning curve. The long lever was comfortable and easy to setup for both Matt and I, but we both noticed a bit of flex within the lever when you're really reefing on them. That long lever gives you a smooth stroke, with no break-in force required to get the action moving. Once you hit the bite point, power comes on with a progressive ramp that feels aggressive enough to achieve max power quickly, but still feather things lightly when desired. The bite point is perfectly consistent, and doesn't shift with heat buildup. One complaint that feels adjacent to feel is the noise the Tech 4 V4 caliper makes when braking. Best described as a screech, this high-pitched noise isn't alleviated with caliper alignment or any bed-in procedure, and is just something to get used to. Likely to bug some and be a non-issue for others, it's ultimately just the sound of them working. Feel Ranking: The light lever action feels intuitive and very easy to control, giving the Hope's an accurate and precise feel. With a quick ramp up past the bite point, you can brake efficiently, but still feather the lighter end of that power when needed. Water Bottle Pull Test: 493 g pull force (3rd overall) As you'll see in the full-length video review, this test simulated the force required to get the brake to the bite point. Water was incrementally added to a bottle attached to the brake's lever, until bite force was achieved. Power The Tech 4 V4 balances power and sensitivity in a way that should appeal to many riders. With a fairly progressive feel, you can get up to max power quickly, but still control that initial bite point. Compared to brakes like the Maven or Saint, the Hope feels less grabby, but still offers similar power in sustained pull moments when you're just working to slow the bike down. Fans of a very aggressive ramp in power might prefer those other options, but the Tech 4 V4 is not to be ignored here. The Race pads offer a the best initial bite of their lineup, and are impressively resistant to fade. The screeching noise is an unfortunate byproduct, but worth it for maximizing the brake's performance. Power Ranking: Strangely, the Hopes didn't fare as well in our perfectly scientific Honda Fit Stop test as expected, slotting in 7th place. That said, their feel on trail is impressive, with accessible power that feels adequate for any gravity use case. Honda Fit Stop Test: 30.2 m / 99 ft (7th overall) As you'll see in the full-length video review, this test was meant to highlight the raw stopping power of each brake. We attached Matt to the back of Dario's Honda Fit, revved up to a consistent speed, at which point Matt used only the bike brakes to slow the vehicle to a measured stop. The new GR4. Updates Hope's newest gravity brake takes many cues from the Tech 4 V4 and introduces some refinements for user experience and ease of service. The EVO GR4 features 2x17mm and 2x18mm pistons, but still uses the same pad shape. The lever is compatible with three different lever blades, the longest and straightest of which (dubbed: Power) best mimics the feel of the V4. All of the lever options have a bit more of a hook to them, which some users will likely prefer. There is an increase in power, and less flex in the system, yielding a firmer feel through the lever blade. The lever action feels slightly less light and free into the stroke than the V4, but still has a consistent and easy motion to it. Both remain excellent options, but the implementation of some additional aftermarket tunes and the addition of a threaded bleed port on the lever will probably lead some users to the GR4. The new model seems to be less screechy, but the noise from pad friction hasn't totally been eliminated. Pros + Sensitive, accurate lever feel + Massive power + Easy to modulate Cons - Lever flex - Screeching pads - Wide caliper won't fit all setups Pinkbike may earn commission on any sales through affiliate links. Learn more . title: Review: Northwave Overland Plus Clipless Shoes
author: Matt Beer
article_text: Back in the 90s, Northwave’s red and yellow Integral XC shoes were unmistakable. Today, the colors are a little more subdued in the clipless Overland Plus model, but functionality and performance are still high on the list of objectives list for this all-around trail shoe. The Overland Plus looks to hit the right marks of a solid trail or enduro shoe. There’s a Vibram sole, breathable yet tough outer material, and a stiff sole capped with an EVA footbed. Other highlights include Northwave’s patented SLW3 dial-closure system and a Velcro strap to balance out the tension. Northwave Overland Plus Details • Vibram Fort Augustus rubber sole • EVA midsole • SLW3 lacing dial • Velcro strap • Colours: black and green • Sizes: EU 37-48 • Weight: (per shoe, size 42) • MSRP: $175.99 USD • northwave.com For such a feature packed shoe, you’d expect the Overland Plus to have a hefty price tag, but at $175.99 USD, it lands below most other brands premium models. They’re not as svelte as their modern XC shoes, the Rebel 3 , but they come in at 894g, a respectable number when compared against similar style shoes. Fit Northwave shoes seem to run a bit longer than normal. I typically go for a size 42, but in this case the fit is on the longer side, and unfortunately the Overland Plus comes in full sizes only. Those span from 37 through to 48. The width aligns with most other performance clipless shoes. Although the side panels of the shoe are flexible and the toe box is roomy, they may fit a slender foot best. A high arch also meant wincing through a ride or two before the shoe molded to my foot. Between the Velcro strap at the top and the SLW3 dial around the toes and midfoot, balancing the tension was convenient. A long and cushioned tongue provides ample overlap under the Velcro strap, which meant no pinching at the forefoot. Performance What would make the ideal clipless trail shoe? It would likely have a slim profile that is well vented and be stiff under foot. Offering enough adjustment to suit a variety of foot shapes and pedal cleat positions would be expected too. In the case of the Overland Plus, it nearly checks all of those boxes. Up top, the lightweight material is both breathable and abrasion resistant while the dual closure system holds the foot in place while moving the bike around. It’s flexible enough, but not too squirmy. I really dig this style of closure system; a strap at the top and lace dial in the middle/bottom. The Velcro strap locks your foot in without cutting off circulation, like some other shoes are notorious for. There’s generous padding in the tongue without feeling squishy and vague. Across the lower foot, the SLW3 dial takes in the slack and doesn’t create any pressure points. The SLW3 dial can be loosened, or tightened by a single click, which makes easing the tension a snap during longer rides. Underneath, I have mixed feelings about the sole of the Overland Plus. The stiff shank is preferable for power transfer, however, it lands with a clunky feel while hiking in rocky or rooty terrain. The smooth Vibram rubber doesn’t offer much purchase in soft organic dirt especially in the wet. The cleat box also had a forward bias. For my cleat position preference, I couldn’t move the cleats rearward enough. I felt as if I was standing on my toes while descending, leading to fatigue through the foot and shins (tibialis anterior muscle). Pricing and Comparison Another high-performance clipless shoe that isn’t too far from the style of the Overland Plus is the Fox Union Boa. The latter is slightly more uniform in materials but has an equally stiff sole, comparable venting, and uses a dial closure. Next to the lacing systems and materials, the largest difference is the size of the cleat box. The cleat position can be moved much further back on the Union shoe, even behind the ball of the foot. At $175.99 USD, the Overland Plus is considerably cheaper than the Union Boa but no less durable. If something were to go wrong with either dial system, you’d be more likely to find a replacement for the commonly used Boa at a shop, depending what region of the world you’re in. With that said, online ordering from either brand has never made it easier to make repairs. One small inconvenience of the SLW3 system from Northwave compared to a Boa is that you have to hold the dial’s latch in the open position to unwind the wire and open the shoe. Conversely, the SLW3 can be loosened one position at a time, whereas the Boa used on the Union can only open completely. Both systems can be closed one click at a time. I prefer to pedal uphill, but I’m not adverse to a hike-a-bike. In this case, neither shoe is amazing for walking uphill due to their stiff soles. The Overland Plus locks you foot in for taking on steeper grades whereas the Union Boa can produce some heel lift and chafing. In terms of grip though, the Union’s moderately deep lugs give better traction across all conditions. Durability I’ve been using the Overland Plus on everything from downhill to gravel bikes. Given their on-the-bike benefits, they’ve been treated to more pedal-focused rides and less adventures traipsing through the forest. They’ve been fully submerged on shorter winter rips and haven't been suffocating on longer rides through progressively warmer spring temps. All of the stitching, seams and bonded areas are holding up well. I did manage to snag my toe while climbing and nicked the rubber but that hasn't caused the material to lift any further. So far, the breathable materials on the upper sections haven’t been compromised by any wayward sticks or rocks poking into the trail. All of the stitching and seams are holding strong, plus the Velcro is still grabbing tightly. The SLW3 dial has been trouble free and thanks to its position out of harm’s way, it never saw any impacts either. Pros + Dual closure system allows for fine tuning fit + Dial can be opened one notch at a time for on the move adjustments Cons - Cleat positioning is limited to a forward bias - Lacks grip for hiking Pinkbike's Take Northwave’s Overland Plus is so close to being the ideal mid-weight trail shoe. The dual closure system is one of the best I've tried; a Velcro strap and SLW3 dial can hone in on a personalized fit that locks in your foot without cutting off circulation. Underneath though, it's a different story. Gravity riders will likely wish the cleats could be positioned further back. The stiff sole could benefit from a deeper tread and take the shoe one step further in soft conditions. — Matt Beer Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Shoes Northwave Author Info: Matt Beer Squamish, BC, Canada Member since Mar 16, 2001 607 articles Matt has been a Pinkbike reader since the site's early days. He discovered mountain biking in Newfoundland, where a small community of dedicated riders and trail builders set him on a path to earn two Canadian National Downhill Championship titles. Now based in Squamish, BC, Matt works as a tech editor for Pinkbike, specializing in gravity-focused mountain bike reviews, tech, and racing coverage.

title: Review: Vorsprung Telum Coil Shock - Canadian Made & Custom Tuned
author: Mike Kazimer
article_text: After years of offering shock tuning services, along with manufacturing their own line of fork upgrades, Vorsprung Suspension decided to create a shock of their own. A three year development process resulted in the Telum, a Canadian-made, custom tuned, twin tube shock. The overall design and resulting list of features aren't wildly different from what's currently offered from other manufacturers, but the smart details and the ability to revalve the shock without pulling it entirely apart help it stand out from the crowd. There's also the fact that each shock is custom tuned for the bike it will be installed on, and for the rider. Vorsprung Telum Details • Adjustments: low speed compression, high speed compression, rebound • Adjustable hydraulic bottom out • Climb switch • Weight: 971 g (with spring, 205 x 65mm) • Made in Canada • MSRP: $1,200 USD • vorsprungsuspension.com That level of customization does come at a price – the Telum retails for $1,200 USD, making it one of the more expensive options on the market. Vorsprung Telum review https://cdn.jwplayer.com/previews/v7wxvyNr Copied Live 00:00 09:21 09:21 Adjustable rebound, high and low speed compression, and hydraulic bottom out. The climb lever creates a firm but not totally rigid pedalling platform. Details Mentioning that the Telum's design isn't a radical departure from the norm isn't meant as a slight – after all, there's no need to start from scratch when there are decades of previous developments to pull from in the moto and mountain bike worlds. The Telum uses a twin tube design with a solid, 22mm diameter main piston. As the shock compresses, the piston pushes oil through the damping circuits in the reservoir bridge, and then it recirculates between the inner tube and the outer tube, back behind the main piston. One of the reasons Vorsprung went with the solid main piston design was to allow for the shock to be revalved without being disassembled. With their Rapid Revalve design it's possible to externally adjust the force required to flex the internal shims, which enables significant changes to the level of damping without a full overhaul. It's worth noting that a revalve isn't something that most riders will need to do unless they're switching the shock to a different bike or had a significant change in weight or riding style. The shock has 18 clicks of low-speed compression, 12 clicks of high-speed compression, 18 clicks of rebound adjustment, and 18 clicks of hydraulic bottom-out, along with a Sprindex coil; that provides a wide range of possible configurations. All of the adjustments are done with either a 3 or 6mm allen key – there aren't any tool-free adjustments, other than the lockout lever. The hydraulic bottom out kicks in for the last 15mm of the shock's stroke. Steve Mathews, Vorsprung's founder, has put together four videos that do an excellent job of explaining each and every detail, free of any unnecessary marking fluff. Those videos are worth a watch if you want to take an even deeper dive into the inner workings of the Telum. Ordering Filling out a detailed questionnaire is part of the purchasing procedure for the Telum. Along with the typical rider weight and bike model information, riders are asked about their riding style, aggression level (on the bike, not in life), strength to weight ratio, plus typical jump size and terrain steepness. It may seem a little excessive, but it's similar to getting fitted for a custom suit (or at least I assume so – I've never actually owned a suit, bespoke or otherswise). Just like you wouldn't expect a tailor to measure one arm and call it good, the more information Vorspung can gather the greater the chance that the shock they ship out will need as little setup time as possible. Ideally, it can be installed and ridden upon arrival without needing any adjustments. The information you provided is saved in Vorsprung's Tuning Hub database, as is the exact tune that the shock is shipped with. All of that information is accessible by the rider, which can come in handy if you decide to try a different tune. In my case, I noted my preference a balanced setup optimized for steep terrain at a variety of speeds, and rated myself advanced / expert with a 'very athletic' strength to weight ratio. For my 160 lb weight I ended up with a 475 lb spring, although the beauty of the Sprindex design is that that can easily be changed by turning the collar. Performance When the shock arrived I did exactly what Vorsprung suggested – I installed it, checked the sag (27%), and did a quick bounce to make sure the settings didn't feel too wacky before heading out for a shakedown lap. That initial ride went well, free of anything that would cause me to pull out my multi-tool and start turning dials. I've been able to try a wide range of excellent shocks on my Raaw, so it wasn't a mindblowingly different ride experience compared to what I was used to, but it was very quiet, very calm, and free of any annoying top-outs. Given the choice, and if it doesn't negatively affect the performance, I'll take a silent shock over a noisy one every single time. Three months later the shock is still as smooth as when it arrived, and I haven't had to make any drastic setup changes – I'm still within a click or two of the original recommended setup. The best shocks fade into the background, and the Telum sits firmly in that category. It enters its travel smoothly, and then remains nicely composed when dealing with repeated sharp impacts. The hydraulic bottom out feature works well, and there isn't any harshness to speak of at the end of the stroke. Many of my favorite rides involve an hour or so of grinding up a steep fire road, which gave me plenty of time to think about the shock's lockout. The Raaw is a pretty calm pedaling bike even with a shock fully open, but the level of support that the Telum provides without being jarring is commendable. It's the style of lockout I prefer – the shock can still move to take the edge of rougher sections of road or trail, but it's also firm enough to provide a more supportive pedaling platform. If you do forget to flip the lever to open before descending it's not the end of the world – a blowoff feature prevents any damage to the shock, and since it's not a full, super firm lockout it's not an unbearably harsh ride. What about the Revalve feature? I haven't had a need to go down that route, but it's nice to know it's possible. It does require one additional tool, but the steps are simple to follow, and it doesn't require getting covered in oil or having access to a vacuum bleeder. How Does It Compare? I've had a wide range of coil shocks mounted to my Raaw Madonna over the last 12 months, including an Ohlins TTX22, RockShox Vivid Coil Ultimate, and the Push SV Eight. Each shock has their own slightly different feel out on the trail, but with a little experimentation I was able to find a good setup with each option. If I had to rank them, the Vorsprung would be one step from the top, the spot currently occupied by the SV Eight. There's just something about that SV Eight that keeps me coming back to it – it feels slightly more sensitive than the Telum, and it's a little livelier overall, a sensation I prefer. I can achieve a feeling that's similar with the Telum, but it's not identical. At the end of the day, both shocks are excellent, and I'm certainly not being held back by either one. Prices (shock + spring): Push Elevensix = $1,600 (spring included) Vorsprung Telum: $1,200 (spring included) EXT Storia: $1069 + $130 = $1199 Push SV Eight: $865 + $95 = $960 Ohlins TTX22: $845 + $100 = $945 Fox DHX2: $679 + $130 = $809 RockShox SuperDeluxe: $619 + $39 = $658 Pros + Excellent custom tuning service + Very quiet & extremely composed in rough terrain + Wide range of adjustments + the ability to revalve without disassembly Cons - Expensive (but it is fully custom tuned for each rider) - No tool free adjustments Pinkbike's Take Imagine if a band came out with a greatest hits collection as their first album. That's essentially what Vorsprung has done with the Telum – this isn't a tentative step on the long road to perfection. Instead, it's a fully realized, extremely well executed option, one that can go toe-to-toe (or eyelet to eyelet) with anything else on the market. It's quiet, consistent, and the custom tuning helps ensure that it's fully dialed right out of the box. — Mike Kazimer Visit the high-res gallery for more images. Posted In: Reviews and Tech Reviews Shocks Vorsprung Vorsprung Telum Author Info: Mike Kazimer Bellingham, WA, United States Member since Feb 1, 2009 1,975 articles Based in Bellingham, Washington, Mike Kazimer is Editor in Chief at Pinkbike, where he’s been covering all things mountain bike related for the last 14 years. Before joining Pinkbike, he worked in bike and ski shops for over a decade. Between all the biking and skiing he managed to pick up an English degree from Western Colorado University.

title: Review: Squidworx Modular Flat Pedals
author: Matt Beer
article_text: Most pedal companies these days have a composite model in their lineup, which typically comes in at a lower price point, but none of those options have the same level of customization as the Squidworx Modular Pedals. Two replaceable composite wings or cages attach to the main aluminum body to make up the platform. This allows for the most vulnerable part of the pedal platform to be replaced when damaged, exchanged for a different sizes or even one of the various colors, by using the costly materials in this area. Squidworx Pedal Details • Aluminum main body, steel axle, nylon composite cages • 3 modular platform sizes • Dual cartridge bearings with full length bushing axle system • 14 pins per side, 5.5 or 4mm heights available • Colors: 12 various colors for MD platform, black only for SM & LG • Area: 98, 108 or 116mm x 97mm width • Thickness: 12mm • Weight: 458g per pair (419g claimed) • Price: $129.95 CAD • squidworx.bike Replaceable pins double as the fixing point for the composite cages which comes in three lengths and various colors. Details 14 replaceable pins are scattered across either side of the platform - 8 on the aluminum portion of the body and 3 on each composite cage. All the pins thread into an aluminum barrel, instead of the pedal body, making them easy to replace. Each set of composite cages are held in place by four of the pin sets closest to the axle. Squidworx offers 5.5mm and 4mm pin options, and customers can choose to have all of the pins the same size, to have a concave option with taller pins around the perimeter and shorter ones in the center, or a convex option with taller pins in the middle. You’ll need a 5mm socket or wrench on either side to unthread the pins. This is not included with the pedals, but is available on the Squidworx website for $5 CAD.
